CITY OF SAN MATEO
City Council Regular Meeting
June 16, 2025
7:00 PM
City Hall Council Chamber
Entrance is on O’Farrell Street
COUNCIL MEMBERS
Robert Newsom Jr.
Adam Loraine
Nicole Fernandez
Danielle Cwirko-Godycki
Lisa Diaz Nash
 
AMENDED AGENDA
THIS MEETING CAN BE ATTENDED IN PERSON OR REMOTE BY JOINING ZOOM – SEE CODES BELOW.
PUBLIC COMMENTS WILL BE ACCEPTED BOTH IN PERSON AND REMOTELY.
SEE END OF AGENDA FOR OPTIONS AND INSTRUCTIONS ON HOW TO PARTICIPATE IN THE MEETING. 
To join via Zoom – click here: June 16, 2025
To join via telephone:  (408) 638-0968      
Webinar ID: 835 2406 0663    Passcode: 203331
The City Council meeting will conclude by 11:00 p.m. unless otherwise extended by council vote. 
Any unheard items will automatically move forward to the next regular meeting.

CONSENT CALENDAR
All matters listed under the Consent Calendar are considered by the Council to be routine and will be enacted by one motion without
discussion. If discussion is desired, that item may be removed and considered separately.
 
4.
Police Digital Forensic Analyst Classification and Reclassification — Approval
Adopt a Resolution to approve the job specification and salary range for the new classification Police Digital
Forensic Analyst and reclassify the incumbent in the Police Digital Forensic Specialist position to Police Digital
Forensic Analyst.
1 of 1111

--- page 2 ---
CEQA: These classification updates are not subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does
not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on
the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
5.
Deputy Director of Public Works Job Specification Updates — Approval
Adopt a Resolution to approve the updates to the job specification of the Deputy Director of Public Works
position.
CEQA: The job specification updates are not subject to CEQA because it is a government fiscal activity which does
not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on
the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
6.
2nd and El Camino Public Garage — Licensing Agreement
Approve a licensing agreement, substantially in the form presented, between the City of San Mateo and Sutter
Bay Hospital (dba Mills-Peninsula Medical Center) to permit use of 60 City-owned parking spaces on the 5th floor
of the 2nd Avenue and El Camino Real public garage for hospital staff parking.
CEQA: This agreement is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the
operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private
structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of
use.(CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
7.
Fiscal Year 2025-26 General Liability and Property Insurance — Purchase Authorization
Authorize a staff committee comprised of the City Manager, the Finance Director, and the City Attorney to select
the most cost-effective program to insure the City's general liability, property risks, and Library; and authorize the
City Attorney to execute all required documents to purchase the selected insurance for fiscal year 2025-26.
CEQA: This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a
physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.))
8.
Fiscal Year 2024-25 Year-End Supplemental Budget Adjustments – Approval
Adopt a Resolution approving year-end supplemental budget adjustments for fiscal year 2024-25.
CEQA: This FY 2024-25 year-end budget adjustment report is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a
government fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a
potentially significant physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
9.
Poplar Creek Golf Course Well Drilling Project — Contract Approval
Adopt a Resolution authorizing a transfer of $215,423 from the Golf Course Upgrades FY 18-19 Project (618280)
to the Golf Course Well Drilling Project (618278) for a total project budget of $718,794. Award a contract to
GeoCon Consultants, Inc. in the amount of $511,000; establish a 15 percent contingency reserve of $76,650 for a
total amount not to exceed $587,650; authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the contract in
substantially the form presented and issue change orders up to the contingency amount.
CEQA: This award of contract is not a project subject to CEQA because it can be seen with certainty that it will not
cause a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065). In accordance with CEQA
Guidelines Section 15304, the underlying project of drilling a well is categorically exempt from CEQA because it
results in a minor alteration of land. The onsite retention ponds, which serve the irrigation system, will remain the
same size during this project. The new irrigation well will not exceed the previously approved water withdrawal
from the aquifer. It will only draw the amount of water needed to maintain the levels in the retention ponds.

--- page 3 ---
10.
East Hillsdale Park Playground Improvements Project – Construction Management Services Contract Award
Award a contract to 4LEAF, Inc. (Consultant) for construction management services for the East Hillsdale Park
Playground Improvements Project (Project); on a time and expense reimbursement basis for actual work
performed, in an amount of $200,000; authorize a contingency amount of $30,000, for a total amount not to
exceed $230,000; and authorize the Parks and Recreation Director to execute the contract, in substantially the
form presented.
CEQA: This contract for construction management services is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be
seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in the environment (Public Resources Code Section
21065).
11.
East Hillsdale Park Playground Improvements Project – Approval
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure for awarding a purchase contract with
Miracle Playsystems (Vendor) for the purchase of playground equipment, for the East Hillsdale Park Playground
Improvements Project (Project) in the amount of $191,246.54; authorize a contingency amount of $19,124.65 for
a total amount not to exceed $210,371.19; and authorize the Parks & Recreation Director to execute the contract
in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15301.). The purchase of the play equipment by the city will not cause a physical change to the
environment.
12.
Citywide Median Island Mulching — Agreement
Approve an agreement with JetMulch, Inc., for the application of mulch to 153 median islands in the amount of
$326,727; authorize a 10 percent contingency of $32,627, for a total amount not to exceed $359,354; and
authorize the Director of Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and
issue change orders within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is exempt from CEQA because it can be seen with certainty that the application of mulch will
not have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3)).
13.
Worker Resource Center and Amendment for 171 N Amphlett Blvd. – Operations and Lease Agreements
1. Adopt a Resolution appropriating $864,900 from the General Fund and General Fund Housing Set-Aside for
Samaritan House to operate the Worker Resource Center for three years, a three-year lease for the building at
171 North Amphlett Boulevard, and trash collection and miscellaneous maintenance costs for three years; 2.
Approve an agreement with Samaritan House to provide services to operate the Worker Resource Center, for a
three-year term, in the amount of $195,000 annually for a total amount of $585,000, and authorize the City
Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented; and 3. Approve Amendment No. 2 to the
lease agreement with Sakai Family Partnership for the property at 171 North Amphlett Boulevard in the amount
of $279,900 and authorize the City Manager to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity
that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)
(5))
14.
Below Market Rental Rates, Sales Prices and Unit Community Priorities – Adoption
3 of 1111

--- page 4 ---
Adopt a Resolution to establish 2025 maximum rental rates and sales prices for affordable housing as required by
the City of San Mateo's Below Market Rate (BMR) housing program; and establish Community Priority for units
containing three or more bedrooms, which may allow a developer to provide fewer BMR units in exchange for
units with more bedrooms as long as all other BMR program provisions are followed.
CEQA: The establishment of 2025 maximum rental rates and sales prices for affordable housing is not a project
subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in the environment.
(Public Resources Code Section 21065.)

project, and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: The appropriation of funds and agreement approval is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an
organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes to the
environment.(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)

CEQA: ​Approval of this Cost Sharing Agreement is categorically exempt from CEQA, because the underlying

or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical
features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)​

permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical
equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15301.))

20.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Chemicals — Purchase Contracts Award
Award purchase contracts of chemical supplies necessary for Wastewater Treatment Plant operations to the
following chemical suppliers: 1) Univar Solutions USA LLC to supply sodium hypochlorite in the amount of
$1,455,820.00; sodium bisulfite in the amount of $285,134.63; sodium hydroxide in the amount of $457,985.51;
and citric acid in the amount of $55,689.50; 2) Pencco, Inc. to supply ferric chloride in the amount of
$496,272.38; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the contracts in substantially the form
presented.
CEQA: The purchase of various chemicals to enable the treatment of wastewater at the City’s Wastewater
Treatment Plant is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a continuing maintenance activity involving the
purchase of supplies. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(2).)
21.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Biosolids Hauling and Disposal — Contract Change Order
Approve Contract Change Order No. 3 to an agreement with Synagro-WWT, Inc. for wastewater treatment plant
biosolids hauling and disposal services to exercise a one-year extension in the amount of $750,000, for a revised
total contract amount not to exceed $2,794,250; establish a contingency reserve of $100,000; and authorize the
Public Works Director to execute future change orders and extensions within the contingency balance.
CEQA: This biosolids hauling and disposal is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it
consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public
or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no
expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
22.
Integrated Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition System Maintenance and Improvement – Contract Change
Order
Approve a Resolution authorizing an alternative purchasing procedure to award an agreement to Enterprise
Automation, LLC in an amount not to exceed $125,000 for upgrading and maintaining the City’s Supervisory
Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system; establish a contingency reserve of $30,000; and authorize the
Public Works Director to execute the contract in substantially the form presented, execute change orders within
the contingency reserve.
CEQA: This Council action is exempt from CEQA in that it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility this
activity will have a significant effect on the environment, because this activity relates to software technology
support and improvements without any necessary physical changes that may impact the environment.(CEQA
Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)

25.
Staff Augmentation Services for the Wastewater Treatment Plant — Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 2 with waterTALENT LLC for staff augmentation services for the wastewater treatment
plant in the amount of $200,000, for a new total agreement amount of $699,000, and extending the term
through June 30, 2026; establish a $100,000 contingency reserve; and authorize the Public Works Director to
execute the amendment in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the contingency
amount.
CEQA: Approval of this amendment is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that
there is no possibility that approval of an amendment to provide staff augmentation services may have a
significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
26.
Purchase of a Telehandler — Approval
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure to award a purchase contract to PAPE
Material Handling, Inc. for the purchase of a JLG 1055 Telehandler to facilitate Wastewater Treatment Plant and
Field Maintenance operations in the amount of $182,924.27; establish a contingency reserve amount of $7,000;
and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement and any change orders within the contingency
amount.
CEQA: Adoption of a Resolution approving this purchase contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a
continuing maintenance activity involving the purchase of supplies. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(2).) In
addition, approval of this purchase contract is exempt from CEQA, in that it can be seen with certainty that there
is no possibility that the purchase of a telehandler to move material within the Wastewater Treatment Plant as
part of its normal operations and to support the City’s Field Maintenance Divisions may have a significant effect
on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).))
27.
Wastewater Treatment Plant On-Call Consulting Services — Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 2 to the On-Call Professional Consultant Services Agreement with Carollo Engineers,
Inc. for Wastewater Treatment Plant-related services in the amount of $475,000, for a new total agreement of
$1,325,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form
presented and to amend the agreement within the agreement amount.
CEQA: The proposed action is exempt from environmental review under the California Environmental Quality Act
(CEQA), since entering on-call professional services agreements is an organizational or administrative activity that
will not result in direct or indirect significant physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section
15378(b)(5).) In addition, on June 6, 2016, the City Council certified a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact
6 of 1111

--- page 7 ---
Report (Final PEIR) (SCH No. 2015032006), which serves as the primary environmental compliance document for
implementation of the Clean Water Program. On July 16, 2018, the City approved Addendum 1 to the Final PEIR,
and on April 15, 2019, approved Addendum 2. Any resultant capital improvement or operations and maintenance-
related projects resulting from the on-call professional consulting services shall be reviewed on an individual basis
or evaluated for consistency with the PEIR and Addenda.

the Marina Branch Public Library Restoration Project in the amount of $824,900; and authorize the Public Works
Director to execute the agreements in substantially the form presented, issue change orders as allowed by the
contract documents, and approve change orders within the construction agreement contingency amount.
CEQA: This approval of these Agreements is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it
consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public
or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no
expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.))

30.
East 3rd Avenue Landfill Hydrogen Sulfide Monitoring – Agreement Amendment
Adopt a Resolution to appropriate $95,000 from the unassigned balance of the Landfill Fund to the Public Works
operating budget; approve Amendment No. 1 to the Agreement with Stearns Conrad and Schmidt Consulting,
Inc. dba SCS Engineers for monitoring and reporting services for hydrogen sulfide at the landfill in the amount of
$57,650, for a new agreement total of $107,405; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $9,300; extend
the term of the Agreement until December 31, 2026; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the
amendment in substantially the form presented and future amendments within the contingency amount.
CEQA: Approval of an amendment to the agreement with SCS for continued monitoring, reporting and
investigatory work at the East Third Avenue Landfill is exempt from CEQA review as information gathering,
because it consists of basic data collection, research, experimental management, and resource evaluation
activities which do not result in a serious or major disturbance to an environmental resource. (CEQA Guidelines
Section 15306.) In addition, approval of a budget appropriation from an unassigned fund balance of the Landfill
fund to the Public Works Department operating budget is exempt from CEQA review, because it consists of a
government fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a
potentially significant physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)

PUBLIC COMMENT
Members of the public wishing to comment on any item not appearing on the agenda may address the City Council at this time. State
law prevents Council from taking action on any matter not on the agenda; your comments may be referred to staff for follow up.
Public comment is limited to a total of 15 minutes; however, an opportunity for additional public comment may be provided later in
the agenda.
 
PUBLIC HEARING
City Council action on the following public hearing(s) represents a final decision concluding all administrative proceedings. Judicial
review may be had only if a petition is filed with the Court not later than the 90th day following the date the decision is made.
Judicial review of environmental determinations may be subject to a time period for litigation as short as 30 days following the date
the decision is made.
 
38.
Zoning Code Amendments and Below Market Rate Program Update — Ordinance Introduction
Introduce an Ordinance amending the Zoning Code (Title 27) to address affordable housing compliance options,
planning application procedures, and set minimum densities for residential mixed-use development projects; and
approve a Resolution to update the City’s Below Market Rate (Inclusionary) Program to implement various
policies and programs established in San Mateo’s 2023-2031 Housing Element (Sixth Housing Cycle); and find that
the Ordinance and Resolution are exempt from further environmental review pursuant to California
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Section 15183.
CEQA: The Environmental Impact Report (EIR) for General Plan 2040 analyzed new residential development of up
to 19,764 new housing units and growth around the City through 2040. On March 18, 2024, the City Council
certified the EIR. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines Section 15183, the
zoning amendment to modify housing development provisions and development standards (“Project”) is exempt
from further environmental review because the Project, which generally allows for more housing types to be
permitted uses in specific zoning districts and remove regulatory barriers from the zoning code, is consistent with
the anticipated land uses under the land use designations adopted as part of General Plan 2040 for which an EIR
was certified...

40.
Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee Increase for Fiscal Year 2025-26 — Adoption
Conduct a public hearing and adopt a Resolution to increase the Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee by
2.4 percent to account for the annual Consumer Price Index (CPI) increase.
CEQA: This Council action adopting a Resolution to establish the Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee rate
for Fiscal Year 2025-26 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it consists of a government funding mechanism
or other government fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may
result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)

AGENDAS: Agendas and material are posted on the City’s website on the Friday preceding each Council Meeting and can be viewed on the City's
website at www.cityofsanmateo.org . Any supplemental material distributed to the Council after the posting of the agenda will be made part of the
official record.
WATCHING A MEETING ON TV: City Council meetings are broadcast live on Comcast/channel 27, Wave/channel 26, or AT&T/channel 99.
For transmission problems during the broadcast, please call (650) 522-7099. For all other broadcast comments, call (650) 522-7040, Monday-Friday,
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
WATCHING A MEETING ON A COMPUTER: There are three ways to stream.
1) Public Meeting Portal www.cityofsanmateo.org/publicmeetings
2) City YouTube channel and stream it on YouTube: http://youtube.com/CityofSanMateo
3) Watch TV live stream: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/193/Channel-San-Mateo-Live-Stream 
PUBLIC COMMENTS/REQUEST TO SPEAK
Prior to the Meeting 
Send comments to: clerk@cityofsanmateo.org until 4 p.m. the day of the meeting.
During the meeting
By Zoom: Click the link at the top of the agenda and you’ll be added to the meeting. All attendees are muted by default. When the item of interest is
open for consideration, select the “Raise Your Hand” icon and you will be called on at the appropriate time.
By telephone: Call (408) 638-0968 and enter the conference ID found at the top of the meeting agenda. When the item of interest is open for
consideration, select *9 to raise your hand. When called upon, press *6 to unmute, state your name and provide your comments.

ACCESSIBILITY: In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, those with disabilities requiring special accommodations to participate in this
meeting may contact the City Clerk’s Office at (650) 522-7040 or clerk@cityofsanmateo.org . Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will enable
the City to make reasonable arrangements to ensure accessibility to this meeting.

PREPARED BY:
Human Resources Department 
MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025
SUBJECT:
Police Digital Forensic Analyst Classification and Reclassification — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve the job specification and salary range for the new classification Police Digital Forensic 
Analyst and reclassify the incumbent in the Police Digital Forensic Specialist position to Police Digital Forensic Analyst.
BACKGROUND:
The Police Department requested that a classification study be conducted for the employee holding the Police Digital 
Forensic Specialist position, as the position had evolved. A third-party consultant conducted the study and recommended 
that the position Police Digital Forensic Specialist be changed to Police Digital Forensic Analyst, the job description be 
revised to ensure that it is accurate and reflects the duties currently being performed by the employee in the position, and 
the salary range of the Police Digital Forensic Analyst be equivalent to the Police Evidence Analyst at an annual rate of
$111,650.75 - $133,145.65. Additionally, a third-party consultant was hired to analyze whether the Police Digital Forensic 
Analyst position is exempt from Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) overtime requirements and the recommendation was to 
classify the position as FLSA non-exempt.
The Police Department and Human Resources support the recommendations made by the consultants. Consequently, the 
Police Department requests approval to update the job specification for the classification of Police Digital Forensic 
Specialist to accurately reflect the duties of the incumbent, create a new FLSA non-exempt classification Police Digital 
Forensic Analyst, establish the salary range for the new classification at $111,650.75 - $133,145.65 annually, and reclassify 
the incumbent in the Police Digital Forensic Specialist position to Police Digital Forensic Analyst.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The salary range increase for the Police Digital Forensic Analyst classification will result in an additional cost of 
approximately $25,000 to the General Fund. There is only one (1) funded position. For the remainder of Fiscal Year 2024- 
25 the additional costs are immaterial and can be accommodated in the Police Department‘s operating budget. This new 
position will be incorporated into all future budgets.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
These classification updates are not subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. __ (2025)
APPROVING THE CLASSIFICATION UPDATES IN POLICE DEPARTMENT
WHEREAS, pursuant to the San Mateo Municipal Code Section 2.57.030(d), approval of new 
classifications and related job specifications, salary schedules, compensation changes, elimination or 
reinstatement of positions, reallocation of positions, reclassifications, and revisions of job specifications 
must be approved by City Council Resolution; and
WHEREAS, the proposed changes as detailed below are in response to Police Department’s 
requests to conduct a classification study on the Police Digital Forensic Specialist position to determine if 
the incumbent is performing the duties of a higher-level position; and
WHEREAS, the proposed job specifications for the classifications of Police Digital Forensic Analyst 
is attached hereto as Exhibit A; and the proposed salary range for the classification of Police Digital 
Forensic Analyst is attached hereto as Exhibit B; and all exhibits are incorporated by reference; and
WHEREAS, the employee associations representing the impacted classifications have been 
informed of the classification updates and have not brought forth any objections or concerns.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY 
RESOLVES that: 
1.
The classification updates are not subject to California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3)) because it can be seen with certainty that they will not cause a 
physical change in the environment; and
2.
The job specifications for the classification Police Digital Forensic Analyst attached hereto as 
Exhibit A; and 
3.
The salary range for the classification Police Digital Forensic Analyst attached hereto as Exhibit 
B; and
4.
The reclassification of the incumbent in the Police Digital Forensic Specialist position to the  
new classification Police Digital Forensic Analyst are approved.
14 of 1111

--- page 15 ---
Exhibit A
POLICE DIGITAL FORENSIC ANALYST
DEFINITION
Under general direction, to perform forensic examination and analysis of digital devices for police 
investigations; to assist in standard evidence collection practice; to ensure effective storage, 
maintenance, control of evidence and related computer forensic equipment and supplies; to 
maintain necessary data and statistics; and perform related duties as required.
DISTINGUISHING CHARACTERISTICS
This classification is responsible for performing a variety of non-sworn, advanced and highly 
specialized tasks related to examination of and collection of digital forensic evidence.  It is 
distinguished from other civilian police classifications by its primary responsibility for evaluating 
and analyzing digital forensic evidence.  
SUPERVISION RECEIVED AND EXERCISED
Receives supervision from the Investigations Sergeant.
May provide lead direction to interns or other staff as necessary.  
EXAMPLES OF DUTIES - Depending upon assignment, duties may include, but are not limited to, 
the following:
Conduct forensic analysis, comparison and examination of evidence from mobile devices, 
computers and other digital devices for the purpose of police investigations.
Examine, disassemble, reassemble, evaluate, extract and interpret data from cell phones, smart phones, 
mobile devices, tablets, desktop and laptop computers, personal computers, servers, closed-circuit 
television (CCTV) systems, security equipment, vehicles, compact discs, digital video discs, DVR’s, 
gaming consoles, portable hard drives and related devices. 
Transfer data and evidence from evidentiary items to other forms of media for presentation to sworn 
personnel, court, and for long term storage as evidence, ensuring proper evidentiary protocols are 
followed.
Prepare and maintain statistical and operational logs, records, databases, and other files according 
to department policies; prepare written reports on analysis completed and communicate findings.
Collect, preserve, label, catalog, and store evidentiary items for presentation in criminal 
proceedings in accordance with legal standards.
Track all evidentiary devices and performs audits of property and evidence. 
15 of 1111

--- page 16 ---
Prepare digital evidence for owner release or disposal.
Assist by providing on-scene forensic examination of evidence from mobile devices, computers 
and other digital devices when search warrants are being served.
Maintain records and prepare reports detailing findings and investigative methods for digital 
forensic examinations.
Research and evaluate new technical developments in digital forensic examination and analysis of 
digital evidence.
Maintain a consistent and current understanding of laws and technology in the areas of digital 
forensics and other areas of responsibility and recommend changed in policies and procedures 
consistent with laws. 
Perform crypto-currency tracking. 
Serve as primary point of contact between the Police Department and US Secret Service Electronic 
Crimes Task Force.
Maintain and ensure operability of computer forensic equipment.
Represent the Department at community meetings and with other agencies on issues relating 
cybercrime and computer and network safety.
Prepare evidence displays and testify in court as an expert witness in computer/digital forensics.
Provide file information to authorized personnel.
Train new employees in legal requirements, department policies and procedures as they relate to 
digital forensic evidence collection.
Perform administrative and law enforcement support activities as designated. 
Assume responsibility for ensuring the duties of the position are performed in a safe, efficient 
manner.
Perform related duties as assigned.
QUALIFICATIONS
Knowledge of:
Crime scene forensic investigation procedures.
Operating systems, intrusion, and computer forensic tools.
Forensic science as it applies to the collection and preservation and handling of evidence.
16 of 1111

--- page 17 ---
Methods and techniques used in the identification, collection and preservation of digital 
forensic evidence.
Photographic techniques, equipment, and documentation used in crime scene photography.
Rules and procedures of providing testimony in court.
Programs used in the digital examination of evidence such as Cellebrite, and other computer 
applications related to the work, including word processing and commonly used law enforcement 
databases.
California Electronic Communications Privacy Act, and other Codes, regulations, policies 
and procedures related to the tasks to which assigned.
Advanced mobile device recovery techniques such as JTAG and Chip-off is highly 
desirable. 
Applicable Federal, State, and local criminal and civil laws, codes, and regulations. 
Principles and practices of electronic data storage, data encryption, and computer security 
systems.
Ability to:
Use current methods and techniques of digital evidence collection, recognition, identification, 
comparison and verification.
Effectively examine and analyze computer hardware, software, and electronically stored data 
pertaining to law enforcement.
Safely collect, handle, transport, process, secure, and store digital evidence. 
Find and extract electronically stored information to be used as evidence. 
Perform complex digital evidence recovery and analysis work.
Operate office equipment including computer information systems to include digital 
processing, database inquiry and word processing.
Effectively testify as an expert witness in court.
Work cooperatively with other law enforcement organizations and federal agencies.
Maintain confidentiality with classified and sensitive information.
Review and process evidence which may be disturbing in nature.
Maintain the integrity of evidence and chain of custody in accordance with established 
guidelines.
17 of 1111

--- page 18 ---
Prepare accurate written reports; maintain accurate and legible logs and records.
Communicate clearly and concisely, both orally and in writing.
Work in a safe manner, following safety practices and procedures, model and coach others in 
correct procedures.
Maintain confidentiality.
Experience and Training Guidelines
Any combination of experience and training that would likely provide the required 
knowledge and abilities is qualifying. A typical way to obtain the knowledge and abilities 
would be:
Experience:
Two years of experience performing police-related duties specifically related to digital 
evidence technology related to computer forensics including some experience with handling of 
evidence, evidence collection and processing.
Training:
A Bachelor’s of Arts or Science degree from an accredited college in administration of justice, 
evidence technology, computer science or related field.
Licenses and Certificates:
Possession of, or ability to obtain, a valid California Driver’s License and a satisfactory driving record.
Possession of or ability to obtain ENCE certification from ENCASE, and certification to use Cellebrite 
highly desirable
ADA Special Requirement:
Essential duties require the following physical abilities and work environment:
Must be able to work a flexible schedule including emergency call-in, overtime with extended 
hours, call-back, shift-work, nights, holidays, and weekends to accommodate investigatory needs.
Must be able to perform work in a variety of locations to include on-site work at crime scenes, and 
in the Police Department. Work is performed in indoor and outdoor environments. When work is 
performed outdoors, there is full exposure to the elements. The incumbent may be exposed to 
related health hazards to include blood and other bodily fluids/products, infectious diseases, human 
borne parasites, air, water, and blood borne pathogens, and may have contact with complete and/or 
partial human corpses in various stages of decomposition. Other health hazards may include odors, 
toxic chemicals, and hazardous substances. Occasionally, work is performed within confined areas 
or at high altitudes, including photography from helicopters. Physical demands include some 
ability to stand, walk, bend, lift, and reach. May be required to independently work crime scene 
investigations either without supervision or without sworn police presence. Incumbents must be 
18 of 1111

--- page 19 ---
able to meet the physical requirements of the class and have mobility, vision, hearing and dexterity 
levels appropriate to the duties to be performed.

POLICE DIGITAL FORENSIC SPECIALISTANALYST
DEFINITION
Under general direction, to perform forensic examination and analysis of digital devices for police 
investigations; to assist in standard evidence collection practice; to ensureing effective storage, 
maintenance, control of evidence and related computer forensic equipment and supplies; to maintain 
necessary data and statistics; and perform related duties as required.
DISTINGUISHING CHARACTERISTICS
This classification is responsible for performing a variety of non-sworn, advanced and highly 
specialized tasks related to examination of and collection of digital forensic evidence.  It is 
distinguished from other civilian police classifications by its primary responsibility for evaluating and 
analyzing digital forensic evidence.  
SUPERVISION RECEIVED AND EXERCISED
Receives supervision from the Investigations Sergeant.
May provide lead direction to interns or other staff as necessary.  
EXAMPLES OF DUTIES - Depending upon assignment, duties may include, but are not limited to, 
the following:
Conduct forensic analysis, comparison and examination of evidence from mobile devices, computers 
and other digital devices for the purpose of police investigations.
Examine, disassemble, reassemble, evaluate, extract and interpret data from cell phones, smart phones, 
mobile devices, tablets, desktop and laptop computers, personal computers, servers, closed-circuit 
television (CCTV) systems, security equipment, vehicles, compact discs, digital video discs, DVR’s, 
gaming consoles, portable hard drives and related devices. 
Transfer data and evidence from evidentiary items to other forms of media for presentation to sworn 
personnel, court, and for long term storage as evidence, ensuring proper evidentiary protocols are 
followed.
Prepare and maintain statistical and operational logs, records, databases, and other files according to 
department policies; prepare written reports on analysis completed and communicate findings.
Collect, preserve, label, catalog, and store evidentiary items for presentation in criminal proceedings 
in accordance with legal standards.

either without supervision or without sworn police presence. Incumbents must be able to meet the 
physical requirements of the class and have mobility, vision, hearing and dexterity levels appropriate 
to the duties to be performed.

Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Human Resources Department
MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Deputy Director of Public Works Job Specification Updates — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve the updates to the job specification of the Deputy Director of Public Works position.
BACKGROUND:
The Public Works Department has two (2) Deputy Directors that each manage half of the department’s eight technical 
divisions.  Recently, one of the Deputy Directors resigned, requiring opening a new recruitment for a replacement. There is 
a need to revise the job specification to update the duties and minimum qualifications, and provide greater flexibility for 
assigning divisional management responsibilities between both positions.  The proposed revisions to the job specification 
removes a definition related to the prior “Environmental Services Division” or “Engineering and Facilities Services Division” 
and creates a general language that covers all of the relevant potential duties and desired knowledge, skills, and abilities.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact associated with the revisions to the job specification of the Deputy Director of Public Works 
position.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The job specification updates are not subject to CEQA because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution
             Exhibit A – Job Specification
Att 2 – Job Specification with tracked changes
STAFF CONTACT
Lourdes Coles, Senior Human Resources Analyst
lcoles@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7264

classifications and related job specifications, salary schedules, compensation changes, elimination or 
reinstatement of positions, reallocation of positions, reclassifications, and revisions of job specifications must be 
approved by City Council Resolution; and

request to revise the job specification for the classification of Deputy Director of Public Works to accurately 
reflect the duties and requirements of the classification; and
WHEREAS, the job specification for the classification of Deputy Director of Public Works is attached 
hereto as Exhibit A, and incorporated by reference; and
WHEREAS, the employee association representing the impacted classifications has been informed of the 
job specification updates and has not brought forth any objections or concerns.

that: 
1.
The revisions to the job specification for the classification of Deputy Director of Public Works are not 
subject to CEQA because it can be seen with certainty that they will not cause a physical change in 
the environment; and
2.
The revised job specification for the classification of Deputy Director of Public Works is approved 
and attached as Exhibit A.

--- page 29 ---
Exhibit A
DEPUTY DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC WORKS
DEFINITION
Under general direction, to assist in planning, directing, managing, and overseeing the activities and 
operations of the Public Works Department; to coordinate assigned activities with other City departments, 
divisions, and outside agencies; and to provide highly responsible and complex administrative support to 
the Director of Public Works.  May serve as the City Engineer and/or the Director of Public Works in their 
absence.
SUPERVISION EXERCISED AND RECEIVED
Receives general direction from the Director of Public Works. 
Exercises direct supervision over management, supervisory, professional, technical, and administrative 
support staff.
EXAMPLES OF DUTIES
Depending upon assignment, duties may include, but are not limited to, the following: 
Select, train, motivate and evaluate personnel; provide or coordinate staff training; work with employees to 
correct deficiencies, implement discipline procedures. 
Plan, direct, coordinate and review the work plan for the division; meet with staff to identify and resolve 
problems; assign work activities, projects, and programs; monitor workflow; review and evaluate work 
products, methods, and procedures. 
Provide responsible staff assistance to the Director of Public Works; prepare and present staff reports and 
other necessary correspondence. 
Respond to and resolve difficult and sensitive inquiries and complaints. 
Prepare a variety of budgets, reports, correspondence, and special studies.
EXAMPLES OF DUTIES 
Manage the development and implementation of goals, objectives, policies, projects and priorities for 
assigned regulatory and environmental services and programs including wastewater treatment, conveyance 
29 of 1111

--- page 30 ---
system and pump maintenance, storm drains, regulatory compliance, laboratory management, solid waste 
and recycling, and streets and traffic equipment and device maintenance; recommend, within Departmental 
policy, appropriate service and staffing levels; recommend and administer policies and procedures.
Continuously monitor and evaluate the efficiency and effectiveness of service delivery methods and 
procedures; assess and monitor work load, administrative and support systems, and internal reporting 
relationships; identify opportunities for improvement and review with the Director of Public Works; direct 
the implementation of improvements.
Manage and participate in the development and administration of the annual operating budget and capital 
improvement budgets; direct the forecast of additional funds needed for staffing, equipment, materials, and 
supplies; direct the monitoring of and approve expenditures; direct and implement adjustments as 
necessary.
Serve as a liaison with other City departments, divisions, and outside agencies; negotiate and resolve 
significant and controversial issues.
Serve as the subject matter expert representing the City in all claims, lawsuits and disputes related to 
wastewater conveyance, treatment, or discharge.
Conduct a variety of organizational studies, investigations, and operational studies; recommend 
modifications to programs, policies, and procedures.
Manage and review all public works engineering activities including engineering design, traffic 
engineering, construction inspection and survey, contract administration and asset management.  
Manage and review all facilities services activities, including project management, fleet coordination, 
downtown coordination and building maintenance. 
Ensure coordination with Community Development Department to monitor private developments 
provision of land division records and to determine adequate facilities and funds for addition of the 
necessary improvements to the Public Works infrastructure to service the project proposed.
Direct the forecast of additional funds needed for construction, staffing, equipment, materials, and 
supplies; monitor and approve expenditures; implement mid-year adjustments.
Evaluate, direct and monitor engineering, technical and facilities services operations.
Provide the point of contact for all claims processed by the City Attorney’s office and represent the City 
in depositions and court proceedings.
Manage and participate in the preparation and administration of the capital improvement plan and 
budget relating to design, construction, traffic, and water quality engineering projects.
Attend and participate in meetings and conferences with City boards and commissions, council, City 
administrators, public officials, professional organizations, contractors, County, Regional and State 
regulatory agencies regarding matters relating to areas of assigned responsibilities.
30 of 1111

--- page 31 ---
JOB RELATED AND ESSENTIAL QUALIFICATIONS
Knowledge of:
Operational characteristics, services and activities of environmental services, wastewater treatment,

DEPUTY DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC WORKS
DEFINITION
Under general direction, to assist in planning, directing, managing, and overseeing the 
activities and operations of the Public Works Department; to coordinate assigned activities 
with other City departments, divisions, and outside agencies; and to provide highly 
responsible and complex administrative support to the Director of Public Works.  May serve 
as the City Engineer and/or the Director of Public Works in their absence.
Responsible for directing and managing all programs within one of the two following 
technical areas:
•
Environmental Services Division including wastewater treatment plant operations 
and maintenance; sanitary sewer and stormwater conveyance and pump system 
maintenance; field maintenance for streets and traffic control devices and 
equipment; and regulatory compliance, including laboratory services, environmental 
compliance inspections, air quality regulatory compliance and solid waste and 
recycling. 
•
Engineering and Facilities Services Division including capital improvement 
program development and implementation, traffic engineering, private development 
review, asset management, construction management, fleet coordination, downtown 
coordination and building maintenance. Serve as City Engineer. 
Responsibilities also include coordinating assigned activities with other City departments, 
divisions, and outside agencies. 
SUPERVISION EXERCISED AND RECEIVED
Receives general direction from the Director of Public Works. 
Exercises direct supervision over management, supervisory, professional, technical, and 
administrative support staff.
EXAMPLES OF DUTIES
Depending upon assignment, duties may include, but are not limited to, the following: 
Select, train, motivate and evaluate personnel; provide or coordinate staff training; work 
with employees to correct deficiencies, implement discipline procedures.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  6
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 14-1311
File ID: 25-10010

City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
City Manager's Office
MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
2nd and El Camino Public Garage — Licensing Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a licensing agreement, substantially in the form presented, between the City of San Mateo and Sutter Bay 
Hospital (dba Mills-Peninsula Medical Center) to permit use of 60 City-owned parking spaces on the 5th floor of the 2nd 
Avenue and El Camino Real public garage for hospital staff parking.
BACKGROUND:
Sutter Bay Hospital, doing business as Mills-Peninsula Medical Center (MPMC), located at 100 South San Mateo Drive, has 
formally requested the exclusive use of 60 City-owned parking spaces on the fifth floor of the 2nd Avenue and El Camino 
Real City-owned garage. 
Mills-Peninsula Medical Center request is rooted to in their desire to improving safe and convenient access to parking for 
their employees as the facility has undergone expansion. Services at the 100 South San Mateo campus grew by 12 percent 
in 2024 and are on pace for an additional 7 percent increase in 2025. By the end of 2025, an additional 10 physicians will 
be providing services at the 50 South San Mateo building, with plans to add 7-9 more physicians in 2026 and 2027. These 
providers will offer much-needed services to the City and region, including expanded access to primary care, family 
medicine, oncology, cardiac care, and more.
As one the largest private employers in Downtown San Mateo, MPMC’s expansion is expected to create new job 
opportunities and contribute to patronage of local businesses and restaurants adjacent to the campus. To support this 
anticipated growth, MPMC seeks to use its on-site parking garage for patient use and requests that the City allow hospital 
staff and affiliates to utilize dedicated spaces at the 2nd Avenue Garage.
While the lower floors of the 2nd and El Camino public garage generally utilized have a high degree of permit holders and 
spaces are well utilized during peak hours, the parking spaces on the fifth floor are consistently underused. Allocating 
these spaces for the hospital’s exclusive use is not expected to significantly impact public parking availability at this garage 
and frees up spaces at other nearby public garages from current and future hospital staff. If Council grants this request, the 
dedicated parking spaces will increase the hospital’s operational efficiency while maintaining adequate public parking 
access at this and other public garages. Furthermore, consolidating hospital employee parking on the top floor reduces 
vehicle circulation and congestion in the Downtown area by providing close guaranteed parking stalls, and improves 
overall traffic flow in the area.
Based on the above, staff recommends the City enter into a licensing agreement with MPMC. Key terms of the agreement 
include: 
40 of 1111

--- page 41 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
• Term: 12 months, commencing on July 1, 2025
• Renewal Option: 4 additional one-year renewals 
• Termination:  Can be terminated by either party with 60 days’ notice
• License Fee: $150 per month, per space for 60 spaces; will increase annually by the Consumer Price Index 
• Use: Hospital staff, tenants, and authorized personnel only
BUDGET IMPACT:
The City will receive $108,000 annually in parking fees, which will support the maintenance, security, and upkeep of the 
Downtown public garages.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

LICENSE AGREEMENT BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN MATEO AND SUTTER BAY 
HOSPITALS, DBA MILLS-PENINSULA MEDICAL CENTER, FOR THE USE OF CITY-
OWNED PARKING SPACES 
This License Agreement ("Agreement") is dated for reference purposes only this ____ 
day of June, 2025, by and between CITY OF SAN MATEO, a California charter city (herein 
termed "City"), and SUTTER BAY HOSPITALS, a California nonprofit public benefit corporation, 
dba Mills-Peninsula Medical Center (herein termed "Licensee").
RECITALS:
A.
Licensee desires to utilize sixty (60) parking spaces (individually, "Parking Space " and 
collectively, "Parking Spaces") at the 2nd and El Camino Parking Garage (the "Garage") 
owned by the City.
B.
City has the authority to regulate the terms and conditions for the use of public property, 
including the Garage.
C.
Licensee is a nonprofit public benefit corporation providing healthcare services and 
otherwise doing business within City's jurisdiction and desires to provide its Tenants and 
employees with parking in support of Licensee’s business operations.
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the recitals and the other consideration and 
mutual promises contained herein, the receipt and adequacy of which are hereby 
acknowledged, City and Licensee hereby agree as follows:
Section 1 - SCOPE OF AGREEMENT
This Agreement authorizes the licensing of sixty (60) Parking Spaces for the exclusive 
use of Licensee as described in Exhibit A, attached hereto and incorporated herein by 
reference. The precise location of the Parking Spaces shall be determined by the City in 
consultation with Licensee.  Parking Spaces shall be available for twenty-four (24) hours of 
dedicated vehicle parking.
Section 2 - TERM OF AGREEMENT
A.
This Agreement shall be binding on City and Licensee on the date of its approval by the 
City Council of the City of San Mateo ("Effective Date") and shall be for an initial term of 
one (1) year from the Effective Date ("Term") with four (4) additional one-year (1-year) 
options for renewal. Provided that Licensee is not in default under this License, the Term 
will renew automatically for one-year (1-year) periods of time, up to a total of five years 
including the initial one-year term.  Notwithstanding any contrary provision herein, either 
party shall have the right to terminate this agreement, in each party’s respective sole and 
absolute discretion, upon sixty (60) days’ prior written notice. 
B.
Failure on the part of either party to perform any material obligation imposed upon it 
shall constitute a default and a material breach of this Agreement. In such event, the City 
shall be entitled to exercise all rights and remedies hereby reserved under this 
Agreement or made available under applicable Laws. For the purposes of this 
Agreement, a "material obligation" shall include, without limitation, any failure of either 
42 of 1111

--- page 43 ---
2
party to give any required notice to the other and the failure to perform either party’s 
respective obligations hereunder.
C.
Evaluation of parking needs by City and Licensee shall occur every six (6) months to 
understand if the arrangement captured by this Agreement is mutually beneficial and 
whether modifications to this Agreement are necessary.
Section 3 - LICENSE FEE
Licensee agrees to pay City a licensing fee of One Hundred Fifty Dollars ($150) per 
Parking Space for sixty (60) Parking Spaces, for a total monthly amount of Nine Thousand 
Dollars ($9,000) (the "License Fee”) during the initial one-year (1-year) Term of this Agreement. 
The License Fee shall increase annually on the anniversary date of this Agreement based on 
the change in the Consumer Price Index, as determined by the City.
Section 4 - LIMITATIONS AND RESTRICTIONS
A.
City hereby agrees to permit Licensee, subject to reservations, covenants, and 
conditions herein contained, to provide its Tenants and employees with parking in 
support of Licensee’s business operations at designated Parking Spaces.  Licensee may 
install appropriate signage and striping designating the licensed Parking Spaces, subject 
to City approval including any required permit or other permission.  
B.
This Agreement is not a grant by City of any property interest but is made subject and 
subordinate to the prior and continuing right of City and its assigns to lawfully use any or 
all of the City-owned facilities in the performance of its duties. No use of any property 
under this Agreement shall create or vest in Licensee or its successors any ownership 
interest in the property or other interest of City. Nothing in this Agreement shall be 
deemed to grant, convey, create, or vest a perpetual real property interest in land in 
Licensee, including any fee or leasehold interest in land, easement, or any franchise 
rights.
C.
This Agreement is made subject to all easements, restrictions, conditions, covenants, 
encumbrances, and claims of title which may affect the designated Parking Spaces, and 
it is understood that Licensee, at its own cost and expense, shall obtain such permission 
as may be necessary, consistent with any other existing rights. Any work performed 
pursuant to the rights granted to Licensee under this Agreement shall be subject to the 
prior review and approval of City.
D.
The installation, operation, maintenance, and removal of any physical improvements to 
support Licensee’s dedicated Parking Spaces —including but not limited to addressing 
graffiti, general abatement of abandoned property, and upkeep of the area—shall be 
accomplished without cost or expense to the City, and in a manner that does not 
endanger persons or property or unreasonably obstruct travel on any road, walkway, or 
other access on City property.   Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this 
Agreement, Licensee is prohibited from taking any enforcement action with respect to 
vehicles parked in the Parking Spaces without authorization (“Unauthorized Vehicles”), 
including the issuance of citations, invoices, fines or imposing any monetary penalty, and 
further including the towing or removal of such vehicles.   Licensee may only issue 
43 of 1111

--- page 44 ---
3
warnings regarding Unauthorized Vehicles in a format subject to prior written approval 
from the City.
Section 5 - TEMPORARY PROHIBITED ACCESS
City shall have the right to temporarily prohibit all automobiles from being parked in one 
or more of the Parking Spaces, as the need arises, provided, however, prior to any such 
prohibition on access to the Parking Spaces taking effect, except in cases of a public 
emergency, City shall deliver to Licensee thirty (30) days’ written notice setting forth the reason 
for the prohibition on parking and the date and times such prohibition shall remain in effect. If 
Licenses is prohibited from accessing any Parking Spaces for a period that exceeds five (5) 
consecutive days, Licenses shall have a right to proportionately abate the License Fee for all 
days for which it has been deprived access to the applicable Parking Spaces.  The parties 
recognize that occasional repairs are required, that licensed spaces may be closed due to 
flooding, debris, or other impediments, and that public property is occasionally occupied for the 
purposes of conducting repairs, parades, festivals, and other events, whether such event is 
conducted by City or any person not a party to this Agreement. In the event Parking Spaces are 
removed from service by City, City will work with the Licensee to identify nearby alternative 
locations for the duration of time that the Parking Spaces are unavailable. The duration of time 
allowed for a temporary closing or relocation shall be an amount of time that is reasonable for 
such purpose.
Section 6- COMPLIANCE WITH STANDARDS
A.
Licensee agrees to keep the Garage, including the Parking Spaces, in good and safe 
condition and free from any nuisance to the reasonable satisfaction of the City Manager 
or their designee. 
B.
Licensee shall install signage and striping at designated Parking Spaces and otherwise 
use the Garage, including the Parking Spaces, in a manner that does not violate any 
local, state, federal, and other regulatory agency laws, ordinances, rules, or regulations.  
Licensee shall comply with all applicable Laws in its performance under this Agreement.  
Section 7 - NO FRANCHISE RIGHTS CREATED
Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or creating any franchise 
rights.
Section 8 - REMOVAL AND RELOCATION
If any Parking Spaces are not used by Licensee for at least thirty (30) consecutive days 
without payment of the License Fee, City shall have the right to terminate this Agreement by 
written notice only as it relates to the Parking Spaces that are abandoned by Licensee, unless 
Licensee recommences using such Parking Spaces within ten (10) days of receiving written 
notice from the City.  
If this Agreement is terminated as to any of the Parking Spaces, Licensee shall either 
promptly vacate or remove any improvements installed by Licensee, such as signage and 
striping, at its own expense or, at City's discretion, Licensee may be allowed to abandon some 
or all of the improvements in place. If Licensee is permitted by City to abandon its improvements

activities pursuant to this Agreement, Licensee shall notify the City within twenty-four (24) hours 
of the damages and, upon City's request, promptly repair (or arrange for the repair of) such 
damage at Licensee's sole cost and expense and to the satisfaction of City, provided, however, 
that such damage did not arise, in whole or in part, as a result of City’s or any of City’s 
employees’, affiliates’, contractors’, or agents’ gross negligence or willful misconduct.  
Section 11 - HOLD HARMLESS AND INDEMNIFICATION
Licensee, for itself, and jointly and severally, for its agents, contractors, and employees, 
hereby agrees to hold harmless, defend (with counsel selected by Licensee and reasonably 
satisfactory to City) and indemnify City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and 
agents (all of the above hereinafter collectively known as "Indemnitees"), from and against all 
claims, cause of actions, proceedings, losses, damages, liability, cost, and expense (including, 
without limit, any fines, penalties, judgments, litigation costs, attorneys’ fees, and consulting, 
engineering, and construction costs) for actual loss of or damage to property and for injuries to 
or death of any person (including, but not limited to, the property and employees of each party) 
("Liability") when caused by, arising out of or related to the activities or the licensing of the 
Parking Spaces described in this Agreement. The duty of Licensee to indemnify and hold 
harmless includes the duty to defend as set forth in Section 2778 of the Civil Code. It is the 
express intent of the parties that Licensee will indemnify and hold harmless indemnitees from 
any and all claims, suits, or actions arising from any cause whatsoever as set forth above 
regardless of the existence or degree of fault or negligence on the part of City, Licensee or any 
subcontractor or employee of any of these, except to the extent the Liability was the result of the 
gross negligence or willful misconduct or criminal acts of any of the Indemnities. The obligations 
of Licensee under this Section 11 shall survive termination of this Agreement.

insurance that it and its contractors or other agents who will obtain access to the Garage

would have been liable if only one interest had been named as an insured.
C.
Automobile Insurance. Licensee shall, at its own cost and expense (or at its contractor’s 
sole cost and expense), procure and maintain automobile liability insurance providing 
bodily injury and property damage with a combined single limit of at least One Million 
Dollars ($1,000,000) per occurrence for any auto, or if the Licensee has not owned auto, 
hired and non-owned autos. This insurance shall provide contractual liability covering all 
motor vehicles and mobile equipment owned or hired by Licensee to the extent coverage 
may be excluded from general liability insurance.
D.
Property Insurance. Licensee shall, at its own cost and expense, procure, and maintain 
property insurance to protect its interest in the property to be used in performance of this 
Agreement and the City's interest in materials or property affected by the installation, 
covering all risks of physical loss or damage to such equipment and property. The 
coverage under such policy shall have limits of liability adequate to protect the value of 
the equipment and property to be installed, which shall be reasonably determined by 
Licensee.
E.
Other Insurance Requirements.
(1)
Term. The General Liability, Workers’ Compensation and automobile insurance 
required under this Section 12 shall remain in force until all work to be performed, if any, 
is satisfactorily completed, all of Licensee's personnel and equipment have been 
removed from City property, and the work has been formally accepted. The property 
insurance shall remain in force for the term of this Agreement. The failure to procure or 
maintain required insurance and/or an adequately funded self-insurance program will 
constitute a material breach of this Agreement.
All policies will be issued by insurers acceptable to the City, except as other 
stated herein. Upon evidence of financial capacity satisfactory to City, Licensee's 
obligation hereunder may be satisfied in whole or in part by adequately funded self-
insurance.
(2)
Insurance Companies:  Insurance companies must be admitted in California, be 
in good standing with the California Department of Insurance and have an A.M. Best's 
rating of at least A:Vll (or equivalent), except to the extent Licensee satisfies its 
insurance obligations herein through its Captive Insurer (as hereinafter defined) or 
through another vehicle of self-insurance.
(3).
Self-Insured Retention and Deductible Proof of insurance: Prior to the 
commencement of work, if any, under this Agreement, Licensee shall disclose any self-

7
insured retention, and any deductibles must be stated on certificates of insurance, and 
the certificate of insurance and endorsement must be sent to and approved by City.
(4)
Waiver of Subrogation:  Licensee hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to 
subrogation which any insurer of said Licensee may acquire against the City by virtue of 
the payment of any loss under such insurance.  Licensee agrees to obtain any 
endorsement that may be necessary to affect this waiver of subrogation, but this

F. Notwithstanding any contrary provision herein, so long as Licensee hereunder is the 
original Licensee or an Affiliate (as hereinafter defined), Licensee (or an Affiliate) may 
cause the insurance required to be carried herein to be issued by a duly licensed captive 
insurance company owned by or under common ownership or control with the Licensee 
or an Affiliate (the “Captive Insurer”), if and so long as: (i) the Captive Insurer complies 
with applicable regulatory requirements of, and is in good standing with, the state in 
which it is domiciled and is able to provide insurance coverage for Licensee in the state 
in which the Premises is located; (ii) Licensee’s tangible net worth equals or exceeds 
One Hundred Million Dollars ($100,000,000); and (iii) the Captive Insurer ensures the 
availability of funds to cover all losses required to be insured against by Licensee 
hereunder.  
If the Captive Insurer fails to comply with applicable regulatory requirements of and 
maintain such good standing with the state in which it is domiciled or is unable to provide 
insurance coverage for Licensee in the state in which the Premises is located, if 
Licensee fails to maintain the tangible net worth set forth above or if any of the above 
requirements for Licensee to utilize the Captive Insurer are not satisfied, Licensee shall 
immediately procure the insurance coverages required in this section in a manner 
otherwise permissible herein until such requirements are satisfied.  
With respect to (and only to the extent of) any insurance issued by the Captive Insurer, 
the insured Licensee shall not be required to name any person or entity (including, 
without limitation, City or City’s lender(s)) as an additional insured or loss payee as long 
as the Captive Insurer provides a certificate of insurance evidencing the insured 
Licensee’s coverage in accordance with the requirements of this section and that: (A) 
includes wording that the Captive Insurer will respond to the indemnification and hold-
harmless provisions of this Lease, and defend City, subject to the insurance policy 
terms, limitations, and exclusions; and (B) provides the same insurance protection that 
would be provided to City if the insurance was provided by a third party insurer with 
coverage satisfying all of the requirements of this Lease (except the additional insured or 
loss payee requirement and any rating requirement). The Captive Insurer shall provide 
coverage on an occurrence basis.

Any breach by Licensee of its obligations hereunder shall be grounds for City to

Licensee further agrees that upon the delivery of five (5) days’ written notice, it shall, upon City's 
request, promptly cease operations in and remove all vehicles from the Parking Spaces.  Any 
breach by City of its obligations hereunder shall be grounds for Licensee to terminate this

party’s obligations.
Section 14 - ASSIGNMENT
This Agreement shall not be assignable by Licensee without the explicit written approval of City, 
which approval shall not be unreasonably withheld, provided, however, that Licensee may 
assign the rights granted herein to a parent, successor, subsidiary, joint venture, or affiliate of 
Licensee now or hereinafter existing ("Affiliate"), by only providing notice to City of such 
assignment as set forth below. However, this Agreement shall be binding on successors and 
assigns and shall be disclosed to assignee. Assignee shall unconditionally acknowledge and 
agree to be bound by this Agreement in writing to the City within thirty (30) days of assignment 
or it terminates without further action. If Licensee assigns this agreement to any Affiliate, 
Licensee shall provide City with written notice of such assignment within ten (10) business days 
of such assignment.
Section 15 - ENTIRE AGREEMENT
This Agreement contains the entire understanding between the parties with respect to 
the subject matter herein. There are no representations, agreements, or understandings 
(whether oral or written) between or among the parties relating to the subject matter of this 
Agreement which are not fully expressed herein. This Agreement may not be amended except 
pursuant to a written instrument signed by all parties.
Section 16 - SEVERABILITY
If any one or more of the covenants or agreements or portions thereof provided in this 
Agreement shall be held by a court of competent jurisdiction in a final judicial action to be void, 
voidable or unenforceable, such covenant or covenants, such agreement or agreements, or 
such portions thereof shall be null and void and shall be deemed separable from the remaining 
covenants or agreements or portions thereof and shall in no way affect the validity or 
enforceability of the remaining portions of this Agreement, and the parties shall negotiate in 
good faith to replace any such covenant, agreement, or portion found to be null and void.
Section 17 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be 
submitted to mediation. The parties shall meet and confer within thirty (30) days of a written 
request by either party to resolve any dispute hereunder. The parties shall mediate the dispute 
amongst themselves in good faith, and if any dispute is not mutually resolved within sixty (60) 
days of the parties’ initial meet and confer conference, as such period may be mutually 
extended, both parties shall have the right to pursue all remedies afforded to them at law and in 
equity to resolve the dispute. Each party shall bear its own costs of mediating any dispute in 
accordance with this Section 17. The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.
Section 18 - GOVERNING LAW AND VENUE

time for performance shall be extended to the following Business Day.

hereof, the time in which an act is to be performed shall be computed by excluding the first Day 
and including the last.

municipal, county, state, federal or other Agency having joint or several jurisdiction over the

SUBJECT:
Fiscal Year 2025-26 General Liability and Property Insurance — Purchase Authorization
RECOMMENDATION:
Authorize a staff committee comprised of the City Manager, the Finance Director, and the City Attorney to select the most 
cost-effective program to insure the City's general liability, property risks, and Library; and authorize the City Attorney to 
execute all required documents to purchase the selected insurance for fiscal year 2025-26.
BACKGROUND:
For the past five years, the City Council has authorized a staff committee comprised of the City Manager, the Finance 
Director, and the City Attorney to select the most cost-effective program to insure the City’s general liability and property 
risks and authorized the City Attorney to execute all required documents to purchase the selected insurance. The request 
for Council to authorize a committee in fiscal year 2020-21 was to allow the City to enroll in an organization called Public 
Risk Innovations, Solutions, and Management (PRISM), formerly known as the California State Association of Counties 
(CSAC) Excess Insurance Authority. PRISM is a joint powers authority comprised of dozens of government agencies that 
provides coverage up to $25 million above a $1 million Self-Insured Retention (SIR) per occurrence, and a $1 million 
Individual Member Corridor Deductible (IMCD) that erodes over the course of the fiscal year in exchange for a premium 
discount for the excess liability insurance policy.
This remains a challenging insurance market. On the one hand, the property insurance market has improved such that its 
premium for property insurance will change this year by an estimated -0.4 percent to 4.8 percent, versus the 11.94 
percent increase that occurred in 2024. However, the premium increase for excess liability coverage is estimated at 35.8 
percent to 46.1 percent, primarily due to the state of the insurance market. As always, the fluid circumstances of property 
coverage and cyber excess renewal are currently under review by the staff committee.
City of San Mateo 2025-2026 Insurance Budget Estimates

In February 2025, the City Attorney’s Office received the fiscal year 2025-26 Insurance Budget Estimates and PRISM 
Premium Estimates, followed by updated estimates in May 2025. Generally, the estimates are provided as a range rather 
than an exact number. For the purposes of this staff report, the renewal premiums listed above represent an average of 
the low and high budget estimates.  Except however, the premium for the APIP property renewal is $1,099,777 ($72,428 
less than the estimate), the crime insurance premium is $10,629 (marginally less than the estimate) and this year's 
premium for the Library's DIC insurance coverage was renewed in March 2025 for $84,030. The renewal premium 
estimates currently total $3,810,080, which is below the budget high estimated total of $4,017,131. Regardless, the staff 
committee is given broad authority to obtain insurance coverage even if the estimate is somewhat more than the budget 
high estimate.
The staff committee is recommending that Council authorize the committee to select the most cost-effective solution 
continuing into fiscal year 2025-26 as circumstances remain challenging and unusual. The City Attorney would then 
execute all of the documents necessary to obtain that insurance. Included in this evaluation would be liability insurance 
and property insurance (including insuring the City’s critical bridges), and excess cyber liability, if deemed necessary.
In addition, the Main Library's Difference in Conditions (DIC) including Flood and Earth Movement/Building 
Ordinance/ICC/Demo coverage is renewed annually in March. This year's premium for the Library's DIC insurance coverage 
was renewed in March 2025 for $84,030 as noted above. The budget high estimate for this coverage is $98,567, which will 
be renewed in March 2026.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The City paid approximately $3.11 million for general liability and property insurance (including bridges, cyber, and crime) 
for fiscal year 2024-25. The renewal premium for 2025-26, including Library DIC is estimated to be $3,810,080. The 
proposed fiscal year 2025-26 budget assumed $4.1 million for insurance premiums, therefore no modifications to 
appropriations are required.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in 
the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
