Contract Amendment
Page 1 of 2
CAO Approved June 2024
AMENDMENT NO. 2 TO THE AGREEMENT
BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN MATEO AND
WATERTALENT LLC FOR
STAFF AUGMENTATION SERVICES
($99,000 [Original Amount]; $400,000 [Amendment No. 1]; $200,000 [Amendment No. 2])
This Amendment is made and entered into this day of ___________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
("CITY"), and waterTalent LLC ("CONSULTANT").
WHEREAS, on August 30, 2024, CITY and CONSULTANT entered into an Agreement for staff 
augmentation services (“Agreement”) in the amount of $99,000; and
WHEREAS, on September 18, 2024, CITY and CONSULTANT executed Amendment No. 1 to 
increase the Agreement amount by $400,000, for a revised total Agreement amount of $499,000; and
              WHEREAS, CITY and CONSULTANT wish to increase the Agreement amount by $200,000, for a 
revised Agreement amount of $699,000, and to extend the term of the Agreement.
NOW, THEREFORE, the Parties agree as follows:
1. The first sentence of Section 7, “TERM, PROGRESS AND COMPLETION,’’ of the Agreement is 
amended to read:
“The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence and be completed 
by June 30, 2026.’’ 
2. The first paragraph of Section 8, “PAYMENT,” of the Agreement is amended to read:
“Payment shall be made by the CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of 
a payment request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed. In 
consideration for the full performances of the services and any reimbursable expenses 
set forth in this Agreement, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee based on verified 
time and materials not to exceed $699,000.00. Requests for payments shall be itemized 
and correspond to the various items of work described in Exhibit A and shall be based 
on the rate and cost schedule set forth in Exhibit B.”
3. The remaining terms of the Agreement remain in full force and effect.

June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Purchase of a Telehandler — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure to award a purchase contract to PAPE Material 
Handling, Inc. for the purchase of a JLG 1055 Telehandler to facilitate Wastewater Treatment Plant and Field Maintenance 
operations in the amount of $182,924.27; establish a contingency reserve amount of $7,000; and authorize the Public 
Works Director to execute the agreement and any change orders within the contingency amount.  
BACKGROUND:
The Public Works Field Maintenance Division removes and disposes of material from the City’s sewer collection system at 
the Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP). An eductor was recently put into operation at the WWTP to allow for dumping, 
drying, and hauling of the sewer collection system materials. The WWTP does not currently have the equipment necessary 
to move the material from the eductor into a roll-off bin so that it can be hauled away. 
During the eductor design phase, it was decided that the cost of a new piece of equipment would be shared by the WWTP 
and Field Maintenance Divisions. This contract would allow for the purchase of equipment that would be used to move the 
material and provide other functionality for the WWTP and Field Maintenance Sewer divisions. 
As part of the City’s Green Fleet transition, staff consistently explores electric alternatives during the procurement process 
and aims to procure vehicles with battery electric or alternative-fueled technologies whenever possible. Although the 
market has electric-powered telehandlers, the telehandler required to facilitate the WWTP and Field Maintenance 
Divisions’ operations requires a rated capacity of 10,000 pounds, which is a much higher rated capacity than any of the 
available electric-powered options on the market. 
In its search for a telehandler, City staff evaluated prices from Sourcewell selected vendors as well as direct quotes from 
vendors. Sourcewell is an organization that provides cooperative purchasing by first conducting competitive, public bids 
then selecting vendors to achieve competitive pricing. Staff was able to locate and evaluate Sourcewell selected vendors. 
Staff then reviewed prices for three different brands of telehandlers directly provided by three different vendors. PAPE 
Material Handling, Inc.’s price for the JLG Telehandler was the lowest priced unit among the directly solicited vendors and 
Sourcewell selected vendors.
San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3) authorizes the City Council to approve an alternative purchasing 
procedure when it finds doing so is in the best interest of the City. The purchase of one JLG 1055 Telehandler from PAPE 
Material Handling is in the best interest of the City because the equipment meets the operational needs of both the 
WWTP and Field Maintenance divisions, and the price offered is the lowest, even in comparison to pricing offered by 
vendors associated with Sourcewell. 
407 of 1111

--- page 408 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Based on the above, staff recommends approval of the purchase contract with PAPE Material Handling, Inc. in an amount 
of $182,924.27, which is inclusive of the $166,863.64 quoted price plus taxes. Staff also recommends establishing a $7,000 
contingency to address any price fluctuations from market instability that may occur between the time the original quote 
was submitted to the time the contract is executed.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are budgeted in the Field Maintenance Sewer and WWTP Maintenance operating budgets to fund this 
purchase and requested contingency amount. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Adoption of a Resolution approving this purchase contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a continuing 
maintenance activity involving the purchase of supplies. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(2).)  In addition, approval of 
this purchase contract is exempt from CEQA, in that it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility that the 
purchase of a telehandler to move material within the Wastewater Treatment Plant as part of its normal operations and to 
support the City’s Field Maintenance Divisions may have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15061(b)(3).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution
Att 2 – Purchase Contract

Robert Learmonth, Wastewater Treatment Plant Manager 
rlearmonth@cityofsanmateo.org

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ 2025
APPROVING AN ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING PROCEDURE TO AWARD A CONTRACT TO PAPE MATERIAL 
HANDLING, INC. FOR THE PURCHASE OF A JLG 1055 TELEHANDLER 
WHEREAS, the City Council is being asked to award contracts for the purchase of a JLG 1055 Telehandler 
in the amount of $182,924.27 to facilitate Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) and Field Maintenance 
operations; and 
WHEREAS, San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.030 generally requires competitive bidding for 
contacts of $100,000 or more; and 
WHEREAS, Sourcewell is an organization that conducts competitive, public bids to select vendors to 
achieve competitive pricing by leveraging cooperative purchasing; and
WHEREAS, staff also reached out to vendors outside of the Sourcewell to obtain additional competitive 
prices; and
WHEREAS, PAPE Material Handling, Inc.’s price for the JLG Telehandler was found to be the lowest 
priced unit amongst Sourcewell selected vendors and direct quotes received from vendors; and
WHEREAS, San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3) authorizes the City Council to approve an 
alternative purchasing procedure when it is in the best interest of the City; and 
WHEREAS, the purchase of one JLG 1055 Telehandler from PAPE Material Handling, Inc. is in the best 
interest of the City because the equipment meets the operational needs of both the WWTP and Field 
Maintenance Divisions, and the price offered is the lowest, even in comparison to vendors associated with 
Sourcewell; and
WHEREAS, based on these circumstances, staff recommends the approval of the purchase contract with 
PAPE Material Handling, Inc. in an amount of $182,924.27, which is inclusive of the $166,863.64 quoted price 
plus taxes, because it is in the best interest of the City without standard competitive bid practice.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS AND 
RESOLVES that: 
1.
Adoption of this resolution to approve a purchase contract is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is 
a continuing maintenance activity involving the purchase of supplies. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15378(b)(2).)  In addition, approval of this purchase contract is exempt from CEQA, in that it can be seen 
with certainty that there is no possibility that the purchase of a telehandler to move material within the 
Wastewater Treatment Plant as part of its normal operations and to support the City’s Field 
Maintenance Divisions may have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15061(b)(3).)
2.
In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3), the purchase of the JLG 1055 
Telehandler is in the City’s best interest for these reasons:
409 of 1111

--- page 410 ---
a.
The JLG 1055 Telehandler meets the operational needs of the Public Works Department’s 
WWTP and Field Maintenance Divisions; 
b.
The City obtained competitive pricing from Sourcewell, as well as direct quotes from vendors 
not associated by Sourcewell; 
c.
PAPE Material Handling, Inc. was found to have provided the lowest price for a telehandler 
meeting the City’s operational needs.
3.
The purchase of a JLG 1055 Telehandler in the amount of $182,924.27 from PAPE Material Handling, Inc. 
is approved.
4.
The Public Works Director is authorized to execute the purchase contract on behalf of the City.

This Purchase Contract (“Contract”), made and entered into this day of ___________________, 
by and between the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State 
of California (“CITY”), and PAPE Material Handling, Inc., a corporation (“VENDOR”).
RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires to purchase certain JLG 1055 Telehandler hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage VENDOR to provide these JLG 1055 Telehandler by reason of its 
qualifications and experience and VENDOR has offered to provide the required goods on the terms and 
in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - PURCHASE
The goods to be purchased from VENDOR under this Contract are described in Exhibit A to this 
Agreement, which is attached and incorporated by reference. 
SECTION 2 - PRICE AND TAXES
All prices shall be as stated in this Contract and are firm and not subject to escalation.  
This purchase is subject to all California sales tax.  Municipalities are exempt from federal excise and 
transportation taxes.  Prices shall exclude these taxes. Purchase price of the tellehandler is to be 
$182,924.27 inclusive of sales tax.   
SECTION 3 - PAYMENT
Unless otherwise agreed in writing, payment terms shall be net thirty (30) days from date of 
receipt of invoice or acceptance of goods, whichever occurs last.  Invoices must cite the purchase order 
number to prevent delay in payment.  All invoices must be mailed to City of San Mateo, Attn:  Accounts 
Payable, 330 West 20th Avenue, San Mateo, CA 94403.

SECTION 4 - DELIVERY AND PERFORMANCE
Time is of the essence in the performance of this Contract.  If delivery of goods cannot be made 
at the specified time, VENDOR shall promptly notify the CITY of the earliest possible date for delivery.  
Notwithstanding such notice, if VENDOR for any reason fails to deliver goods within the time specified 
or to the CITY’s satisfaction the CITY may terminate this Contract or any part thereof without liability 
except for goods previously provided and accepted.  The CITY’s receipt or acceptance of all or part of a 
non-conforming delivery shall not constitute a waiver of any claim, right or remedy the CITY has under 
this Contract or applicable law. 
SECTION 5 - SHIPMENT AND INSPECTION
VENDOR assumes full responsibility for all transportation, transportation scheduling, packing, 
handling, insurance, and other services associated with delivery of all products.  No charges for 
transportation containers, packing, etc., will be allowed the vendor unless so specified in this Contract.  
All shipments shall be F.O. B. to 2050 Detroit Drive, San Mateo.  Transportation charges shall be shown 
as a separate item on the invoice.
The CITY may revise shipping instructions as to any goods not as yet shipped.  The CITY shall 
have the right to inspect any or all of the goods at VENDOR’s place of business or upon receipt by the 
CITY.  By reason of its failure to inspect the goods, the CITY shall not be deemed to have accepted any 
defective goods or goods which do not conform to the specifications provided or to have waived any of 
the CITY’s rights or remedies arising by virtue of such defects or non-conformance.  VENDOR shall be 
responsible for payment of shipping for the return of any defective goods.  Shipping documents and 
invoices must cite the Purchase Order number.
SECTION 6 - WARRANTIES
In addition to any other expressed or implied warranties and unless otherwise agreed in 
writing, VENDOR warrants that all products delivered hereunder will be new, suitable for use as 
described, of the grade and quality specified, free from all defects in design, material and 
workmanship; in conformity with all samples, drawings, descriptions and specifications furnished; in 
compliance with all applicable federal, state and local laws and regulations, and free of any liens and 
encumbrances.  These warranties shall not be deemed to exclude VENDOR’s standard warranties or 
other rights or warranties which the CITY may have or obtain.
SECTION 7 - INDEMNITY
VENDOR agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising 
out of VENDOR’s performance of this Contract, except for those claims arising out of CITY’s sole 
negligence or willful misconduct.  VENDOR agrees to defend CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents against any such claims. The VENDOR’S duty to indemnify shall survive 
expiration or early termination of this Agreement.

SECTION 8 - TERMINATION
This Contract may be terminated by mutual consent of both parties or by the CITY at its 
discretion.  The CITY may cancel an order for goods at any time with written notice to VENDOR, stating 
the extent and effective date of termination.  Upon receipt of this written notice, VENDOR shall stop 
performance under this Contract as directed by the CITY.  If the Contract is terminated, VENDOR shall 
be paid in accordance with the terms of the Contract for goods delivered and accepted.
SECTION 9 - REMEDIES
In the event of VENDOR’s breach of this Contract, City may take any or all of the following 
actions, without prejudice to any other rights or remedies available to the City by law:  (a) require 
Vendor to repair or replace such goods, and upon VENDOR’s failure or refusal to do so, repair or 
replace the same at VENDOR’s expense; (b) reject any shipment or delivery containing defective or 
nonconforming goods and return for credit or replacement at VENDOR’s option, said return to be 
made at VENDOR’s cost and risk; (c) cancel any outstanding deliveries and treat such breach by 
VENDOR as VENDOR’s repudiation of this contract.  In the event of the CITY’s breach hereunder, 
VENDOR’s exclusive remedy shall be VENDOR’s recovery of the goods or the purchase price payable for 
goods shipped prior to such breach.
SECTION 10 - COMPLIANCE WITH LAW
VENDOR warrants that it will comply with all federal, state, and local laws, ordinances, rules 
and regulations applicable to its performance under this Contract.  VENDOR shall obtain and maintain 
throughout the life of the Contract all permits or licenses required in connection with the manufacture, 
sale, and shipment of the products ordered under this Contract.  
SECTION 11 - ASSIGNMENT
VENDOR shall not delegate or subcontract any duties and services or assign any rights or claims 
under this Contract without the CITY’s prior written consent.
SECTION 12 - ARTWORK, DESIGNS, PATENTS, COPYRIGHTS AND TRADEMARKS
VENDOR hereby agrees that the sale, use or incorporation into manufactured products of all 
machines, software, hardware, materials and other devices furnished under this Contract are free and 
clear of infringement of any valid patent, copyright, or trademark.  VENDOR shall hold the CITY 
harmless from any and all costs and expenses, including attorney fees, liability, and loss of any kind 
growing out of claims, suits or actions alleging such infringement, and VENDOR agrees to defend such 
claims, suits or actions.

The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such

SECTION 16 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this provision that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 17 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
 
CONTRACTOR warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONTRACTOR nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act. 
SECTION 18 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Contract, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the California State Board 
of Mediation and Conciliation, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a 
"blindfolded" process.

SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant On-Call Consulting Services — Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 2 to the On-Call Professional Consultant Services Agreement with Carollo Engineers, Inc. for 
Wastewater Treatment Plant-related services in the amount of $475,000, for a new total agreement of $1,325,000; and 
authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented and to amend the 
agreement within the agreement amount.
BACKGROUND:
The City is currently under a Regional Water Quality Control Board Cease and Desist Order requiring improvements to its 
sewer collection system and Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) to eliminate sanitary sewer overflows and 
unauthorized discharges of untreated or partially treated wastewater to San Francisco Bay. The Clean Water Program 
(CWP) includes several capital improvements that are currently under construction and intended to comply with the 
Cease-and-Desist Order.
As part of the CWP, the City has identified capital improvements needed to maintain and/or improve WWTP reliability, 
performance, and safety (Projects). Staff solicited on-call professional services to support these needs, to include 
alternatives analyses and engineering studies; design services; bid period services; engineering services during 
construction; and operational support. 
On August 15, 2022, City Council awarded two agreements to Carollo Engineers, Inc. (Carollo) and Brown and Caldwell for 
on-call professional consulting services in a total of $400,000. The agreement terms were for three years with up to two 
optional one-year extensions with an additional budget of up to $100,000 per year. 
On October 21, 2024, City Council approved Amendment No. 1 for an additional $450,000 to Carollo’s agreement to fund 
engineering services for additional scope to provide services for the 3-Water Pumping and Ferric Chloride Project and the 
Waste Activated Sludge Thickening Alternatives Analysis Project. Amendment No. 1 increased the total agreement amount 
to $850,000. 
On March 6, 2025, the City issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for the Dewatering System Centrifuges 1 and 2 Project to 
the two on-call consultants, Brown and Caldwell and Carollo. Proposals were received on April 3, 2025. Staff evaluated 
both proposals and determined Carollo as the best qualified consultant to provide the scope of work and expertise 
required for this project. The fee is $614,532 to complete the base scope of services, which includes general project 
management services; basis of design and preliminary design; detailed design and bid services. 
The balance of the Carollo on-call agreement is not sufficient to fund the fee associated with this project and an increase 
419 of 1111

--- page 420 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
of $423,429 is needed. Staff also recommends $51,571 to be added to address any unknown or unforeseeable tasks if 
required.
For Carollo to provide engineering services for these tasks, staff recommends approving Amendment No. 2 to increase the 
on-call agreement by $475,000 for a new total agreement of $1,325,000. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are sufficient funds available in the Clean Water Program WWTP Annual Major Components (46t090) project budget 
to fund this amendment. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The proposed action is exempt from environmental review under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), since 
entering on-call professional services agreements is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct 
or indirect significant physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).) In addition, on June 6, 
2016, the City Council certified a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) (SCH No. 2015032006), 
which serves as the primary environmental compliance document for implementation of the Clean Water Program. On July 
16, 2018, the City approved Addendum 1 to the Final PEIR, and on April 15, 2019, approved Addendum 2. Any resultant 
capital improvement or operations and maintenance-related projects resulting from the on-call professional consulting 
services shall be reviewed on an individual basis or evaluated for consistency with the PEIR and Addenda. 
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

A and shall be based on the rate and cost schedule set forth in Exhibit B.”
2.
The remaining terms of the Agreement remain in full force and effect.

June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Marina Branch Public Library Restoration Project – Agreements
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with DL Falk Construction, Inc. for construction of the Marina Branch Public Library Restoration 
Project in the amount of $5,405,000; establish a contingency reserve for said agreement in the amount of $540,500; 
approve an agreement with Consor PMCM, Inc. for construction management services for the Marina Branch Public 
Library Restoration Project in the amount of $824,900; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreements 
in substantially the form presented, issue change orders as allowed by the contract documents, and approve change 
orders within the construction agreement contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The Marina Branch Public Library Restoration Project (Project) will implement critical improvements, including foundation 
stabilization with new helical piles and grade beams, re-leveling of the existing foundation and structures, and compliance 
with Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) requirements. Additional improvements encompass the replacement of 
entrance and egress ramps, site drainage improvements, deck replacement, lighting (external and indoor), flooring, roof 
replacement, heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) equipment, plumbing improvements, fire sprinkler and 
alarm systems, and electrical system replacement. 
The City entered into an agreement with Siegel & Strain Architects in July 2023 to design the Project and the design was 
completed in April 2025. The design includes two (2) additive bid items: (A) to fabricate and install a bookworm sculpture, 
and; (B) to fully remove and replace stucco along the exterior of the building. The Architect’s estimate for the base bid and 
additive bid items was $5,000,000. 
Construction Agreement
In accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.030, a competitive public process was used to obtain bids for the Project. 
A Notice Inviting Sealed Proposals was issued to advertise the Project on April 25, 2025. On the May 22, 2025, bid opening 
date, three (3) bids were received.

423 of 1111

--- page 424 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Staff reviewed the lowest bid submitted by DL Falk Construction, Inc., and determined that DL Falk Construction, Inc. is the 
lowest responsive, responsible bidder. Staff recommends City Council award the Agreement to DL Falk Construction, Inc. 
inclusive of the scopes of Additive Bid Items A and B. In addition, staff recommends a 10 percent contingency be 
established given potential unforeseen site conditions, the complexity of performing a retrofit project, and caution that 
will need to be exercised due to the project’s proximity to Seal Slough. 
Construction is anticipated to commence in the summer of 2025 with completion in spring of 2027, resulting in an 
approximately 16-month temporary closure of the library. Staff are implementing outreach to inform the community and 
library patrons.
Construction Management Agreement 
The City issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for Construction Management, Inspection, and Hazardous Materials 
Compliance Services for the Project on February 26, 2025. On the April 4, 2025, deadline to submit proposals, one (1) 
proposal was received from Consor PMCM, Inc. (Consor). Staff reviewed the proposal, interviewed Consor, and 
determined the firm’s proposal to be satisfactory based on the Project team’s experience with similar projects and 
comprehensive project approach to meet the goals of the RFP. 
Consor will be providing construction management and inspection services that include oversight of the contractor, review 
of construction documents such as submittals, change orders, progress payments and requests for information, adherence 
to schedule, and coordination with City and design staff. They will also monitor the contractor’s hazardous materials 
handling and verify compliance with CEQA measures that stem from the project’s proximity to Seal Slough and potential 
sensitive habitats. 
Staff recommends entering into a contract with Consor to perform the construction management services for this Project 
for an amount not to exceed $824,900.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Currently, sufficient funds are not available in the Marina Library Structural Improvements (665100) project account 
budget to approve the construction agreement; however, the remaining funding needs to sufficiently cover the full cost of 
the agreement and recommended contingency have been requested through the 5-year Capital Improvement Program 
budget development process and will be available under the Marina Library Structural Improvements (665100) project 
pending approval and adoption of the Fiscal Year 2025-26 budget. Sufficient funds are available in the project account to 
approve the construction management contract with Consor.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This approval of these Agreements is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the 
operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, 
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Contract
This public works contract (“Contract”) is entered into by and between the City of San Mateo (“City”) and 
DL Falk Construction, Inc. (“Contractor”), for work on the Marina Branch Public Library Restoration 
Project (“Project”).
The parties agree as follows:
1.
Award of Contract.  In response to the Notice Inviting Bids, Contractor has submitted a Bid 
Proposal to perform the Work to construct the Project. On June 16, 2025, City authorized award of 
this Contract to Contractor for the amount set forth in Section 4, below. City has elected to include 
the following Project alternate(s) in the Contract: Additive Bid Alternate A and Additive Bid Alternate 
B.
2.
Contract Documents.  The Contract Documents incorporated into this Contract include those 
within the Contract Book, and inclusive of the documents listed below. The definitions provided in 
Article 1 of the General Conditions apply to all of the Contract Documents, including this Contract.
2.1

2.2

3.
Contractor’s Obligations.  Contractor will perform all of the Work required for the Project, as 
specified in the Contract Documents. Contractor must provide, furnish, and supply all things 
necessary and incidental for the timely performance and completion of the Work, including all 
necessary labor, materials, supplies, tools, equipment, transportation, onsite facilities, and utilities, 
unless otherwise specified in the Contract Documents. Contractor must use its best efforts to 
diligently prosecute and complete the Work in a professional and expeditious manner and to meet 
or exceed the performance standards required by the Contract Documents.
4.
Payment.  As full and complete compensation for Contractor’s timely performance and completion 
of the Work in strict accordance with the terms and conditions of the Contract Documents, City will 
pay Contractor $5,405,000 (“Contract Price”) for all of Contractor’s direct and indirect costs to 
perform the Work, including all labor, materials, supplies, equipment, taxes, insurance, bonds and 
all overhead costs, in accordance with the payment provisions in the General Conditions.
5.
Time for Completion.  Contractor will fully complete the Work for the Project, meeting all 
requirements for Final Completion, within 320 working days from the start date set forth in the 
Notice to Proceed, and 80 working days for the plant establishment maintenance period.(“Contract 
Time”). By signing below, Contractor expressly waives any claim for delayed early completion.
6.
Liquidated Damages.  As further specified in Section 5.4 of the General Conditions, if Contractor 
fails to complete the Work within the Contract Time, City will assess liquidated damages in the

--- page 427 ---
amount of $1,200 per day for each day of unexcused delay in achieving Final Completion, and such 
liquidated damages may be deducted from City’s payments due or to become due to Contractor 
under this Contract.

7.1
General.  This Contract is subject to all applicable requirements of Chapter 1 of Part 7 of 
Division 2 of the Labor Code, including requirements pertaining to wages, working hours 
and workers’ compensation insurance, as further specified in Article 9 of the General 
Conditions.  
7.2
Prevailing Wages.  This Project is subject to the prevailing wage requirements applicable 
to the locality in which the Work is to be performed for each craft, classification or type of 
worker needed to perform the Work, including employer payments for health and welfare, 
pension, vacation, apprenticeship and similar purposes. Copies of these prevailing rates 
are available online at http://www.dir.ca.gov/DLSR.
7.3
DIR Registration.  City may not enter into the Contract with a bidder without proof that the 
bidder and its Subcontractors are registered with the California Department of Industrial 
Relations to perform public work pursuant to Labor Code § 1725.5, subject to limited legal 
exceptions.
7.4
Minimum Wage. Bidder agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage
ordinance as codified in San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs
from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer Price Index escalator.
Contractor and its Subcontractors are required to pay the higher of the currently
applicable state prevailing wage rates or City minimum wage.
8.

Contractor certifies as follows: “I am aware of the provisions of Labor Code § 3700 which require 
every employer to be insured against liability for workers’ compensation or to undertake self-
insurance in accordance with the provisions of that code, and I will comply with such provisions 
before commencing the performance of the Work on this Contract.”
9. 
Conflicts of Interest.  Contractor, its employees, Subcontractors, and agents may not have, 
maintain, or acquire a conflict of interest in relation to this Contract in violation of any City ordinance 
or requirement, or in violation of any California law, including Government Code § 1090 et seq., or 
the Political Reform Act, as set forth in Government Code § 81000 et seq. and its accompanying 
regulations. Any violation of this Section constitutes a material breach of the Contract.
10.
Independent Contractor.  Contractor is an independent contractor under this Contract and will have 
control of the Work and the means and methods by which it is performed. Contractor and its 
Subcontractors are not employees of City and are not entitled to participate in any health, retirement, or 
any other employee benefits from City.

overnight delivery service, or by email as a PDF file. Notice is deemed effective upon delivery,

This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), and Consor PMCM, Inc. a California corporation (“CONSULTANT”), whose address is 1663 
Mission Street, Suite #425 San Francisco, California 94103.
RECITALS:
A.  CITY desires certain construction management and inspection services hereinafter 
described.
B.  CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these construction management and 
inspection services by reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and 
CONSULTANT has offered to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth 
herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.

CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.
CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining

and be completed on June 30, 2027.

request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full

of $824,900, pursuant to rates stated in Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by 
reference.
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for 
which they are made is executed or not. Any reuse of documents outside the scope of work for which 
it was developed, or any alteration thereof without Consultant review and approval shall be at City’s 
sole risk and without liability to Consultant.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONSULTANT or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.

professional negligence), City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents against any 
such claims. The Consultant’s obligation to defend professional negligence claims is limited to 
reimbursement of expenditure, including reasonable attorneys fees and costs, incurred by the City, in 
defending claims or lawsuits, ultimately determined to be due to negligent acts or omissions of the 
Consultant or any of its officers, employees or subcontractors. The CONSULTANT’S duty to indemnify 
shall survive expiration or early termination of this Agreement.

thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit C to this

Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT 
and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written 
consent of CITY.

of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this

is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.

SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.
SECTION 20 - LITIGATION

with CONSULTANT's services under this Agreement.  Unless the action is brought by CONSULTANT, or 
is based upon CONSULTANT's wrongdoing, CITY shall compensate CONSULTANT for preparation for 
testimony, testimony, and travel at CONSULTANT's standard hourly rates at the time of actual 
testimony.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
Task 1 – Construction Management and General Inspection
CONSULTANT shall provide the construction management and general inspection services outlined 
below.
•
Provide administrative support including, but not limited to, maintaining project files, 
convening necessary meetings, providing meeting notes, monthly progress reports, 
photographic documentation, pay application reviews/negotiations and on-site observation. 
•
Provide, administer, and manage Project communication and documentation until Final 
Completion.
•
Monitor Erosion Control BMP’s and environmentally sensitive area fencing installation and 
maintenance. Verify construction operations do not go past the top of bank. Verify construction 
waste and materials does not enter Seal Slough.
•
Review scheduled submittals and provide schedule management support.
•
Provide close-out support including furniture, fixtures and equipment installations, move-in 
coordination, punch list management, O&M manuals, as-built reference documentation review, 
final release of retention pay review and warranty work management.
•
Ensure all permits are pulled and inspections are conducted.
•
Act as construction project coordinator and the point of contact for all communications and 
interaction with the contractor, affected local agencies, design team, utility companies, City 
staff, members of the Public and other firms performing work. 
•
Review and monitor the construction schedule. Prepare weekly reports documenting the 
progress of construction. Take photographs and videotape recordings of the construction 
progress on a regular basis.
•
Schedule, manage, perform and document all field and laboratory testing services as provided 
in project specifications.
•
Coordination with the design team.
•
Evaluate, negotiate, recommend, and prepare change orders.
•
Prepare and recommend progress payments.
•
Identify potential claims and make recommendations to resolve said claims.
•
Perform field inspection activities, monitor contractor’s performance and enforce all 
requirements of applicable codes, specifications, and contract drawings.
•
Oversee the design clarification process.
•
Oversee quality control and materials testing.
•
Provide final punch-list inspection services and project closeout activities, including preparation 
of the final construction project report.
•
Turn over all construction documents to the City, including As-Builts.
•
All reports, calculations, measurements, test data and other documentation shall be prepared 
on forms specified by or otherwise acceptable to the City.
•
Daily reports, extra work diaries, materials testing reports, and other reports shall be submitted 
to the City. 
437 of 1111

--- page 438 ---
Professional Services
Page 9 of 15
Rev 12/23
Task 1 Deliverables: 
•
Meeting notes.
•
Documention for all field and laboratory testing services as provided in project specifications.
•
Daily inspection reports, extra work diaries, materials testing reports and other reports.
o
Daily inspection reports to include trades/subcontractors on site, work performed, and 
photos in PDF format.
•
Weekly and monthly progress reports documenting the progress of construction. 
•
Written review response for submittals, RFIs, change orders, progress payments.
•
Photo and video documentation.
•
Final punchlist and construction documents, including As-Builts.
Task 2 – Special Inspections
CONSULTANT shall perform and coordinate the following special inspections:
•
Perform all special inspections, with the exception of the geotechnical and soils special 
inspections, as required by the 2022 California Building Code (CBC) Chapter 17. The special 
inspections shall be performed by an approved agency in accordance with 2022 CBC, Chapter 
17, the statement of special inspections as required by 2022 CBC Sections 1704.2.3 and 1704.3 
for building structural elements, and as outlined in the Project Plans. 
•
Coordinate all special inspections related to geotechnical and soils construction that will be 
completed by a separate firm. 
Task 2 Deliverables:
•
Special inspection documentation and testing reports. Special inspection schedule to align with 
contractor schedule.
Task 3 – Environmental Services
CONSULTANT SHALL provide qualified staff required for compliance with standard measures from the 
Marina Branch Library Restoration California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Categorical Exemption.
1.
A qualified Biologist shall perform preconstruction/pre-disturbance surveys to ensure that 
no nests of migratory will be disturbed during project implementation.
2.
A Qualified Biologist shall provide Worker Environmental Awareness Training to all 
personnel involved in construction adjacent to Seal Slough. The training shall include, at a 
minimum, a review of the aquatic animals and plants, and sensitive habitats, that could be 
found in or downslope from work areas; measures to avoid and minimize adverse effects on 
those resources; and a review of all conditions and requirements of environmental permits.
Task 3 Deliverables:
•
Preconstruction and predisturbance surveys.
•
Worker environmental awareness training documentation and attendance.
Task 4 – Hazardous Materials Compliance
CONSULTANT shall provide qualified personnel for coordination for regulatory compliance for removal, 
containment, abatement, and disposal of hazardous materials including regulated asbestos containing 
materials, lead containing paints, PCB’s, mercury containing fluorescent light tubes or ballast mercury 
438 of 1111

--- page 439 ---
Professional Services
Page 10 of 15
Rev 12/23
switches, equipment or systems that may contain Freon or other fluorocarbons, or smoke detectors 
which may contain a radioactive element.  Services to be provided shall include, but are not limited to: 
1.
Specification requirements - Verify the contractor complies to the Requirements for 
Disturbance of Lead in Construction specifications, Asbestos Requirements specifications, 
and Other Hazardous Materials specifications.
2.
Pre-Abatement and Post-Abatement Submittal Review - Review of all contractor submittals 
related to asbestos and lead abatement, including work plans, safety procedures, and 
material specifications, verify that each submittal complies with applicable regulations and 
project specifications before work begins, and provide written recommendations for 
adjustments required to address any noted discrepancies or issues. After the abatement 
work is completed, review all final submittals, including clearance testing results and project 
closeout documents, confirm that the abatement work has been completed in accordance 
with the approved specifications and that all necessary documentation is in order.
3.
Air Monitoring - provide oversight and approval of perimeter air monitoring during the 
abatement of asbestos and lead-containing materials. Ensure that airborne contaminant 
levels are monitored throughout the abatement process to protect workers and public 
safety and to confirm that air quality remains within regulatory limits. 
a. Coordinate with abatement contractor to set up air monitoring stations at strategic 
locations around the perimeter of the work area.  
b. Oversee the real-time air monitoring process, ensuring that samples are collected 
according to regulatory standards and project specifications. This includes verifying that 
the correct sampling methods and equipment are used. 
4.
Data Review and Approval – Ship samples to a laboratory with a 24-hour turnaround time. 
Review the air monitoring results upon receipt to ensure that they are within acceptable 
regulatory limits, and if the results indicate elevated levels, work with the contractor to 
address the issue and determine if additional controls or sampling are needed.
5.
Lead Wipe Sampling for Reoccupation - Conduct lead wipe sampling on key surfaces, 
including windowsills and floors, in the affected areas.  Use standardized methods to collect 
samples, which are then analyzed in a certified laboratory to determine lead dust levels.
a. Regulatory Compliance – CONSULTANT shall ensure that lead levels are below the 
regulatory limit of 10 micrograms per square foot. If levels are found to be above this 
threshold, additional cleaning and re-sampling may be necessary until the area meets 
safety standards.
Task 4 Deliverables:
•
Written review responses for pre-abatement and post-abatement submittal reviews.
•
Written responses of review of air monitoring results.
•
Detailed report of the lead sampling results, along with any regulatory standards and methods 
for re-cleaning or for further action, if needed.

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate 
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or 
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate.
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.

subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.

otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  29
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-4661
File ID: 25-10006
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Traffic Signal Modification at Poplar Avenue and Humboldt Street — Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Phoenix Electric Company for construction of the Traffic Signal Modification at Poplar Avenue 
and Humboldt Street in the amount of $367,000; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $55,000; and authorize 
the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders as 
allowed by the contract documents and within the contingency reserve.
BACKGROUND:
The City adopted its first Local Roadway Safety Plan (LRSP) in May 2024. The LRSP analyzes traffic safety performance and 
crash patterns and recommends strategic and proven solutions to improve safety performance at priority locations 
citywide. After a year of community engagement and review by the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission (SIC) and 
City Council, the adopted LRSP is a data-driven plan focused on improving safety. In addition to adopting the plan, City 
Council directed staff to prioritize implementing the top 10 priority projects identified in the plan. 
Project Scope
The LRSP identified the intersection of Poplar Avenue and Humboldt Street (Project) as the third highest-priority location 
in the City in need of safety improvements. It is the highest priority location that is within the City's jurisdiction, with the 
top two locations being on El Camino Real and subject to Caltrans’ jurisdiction. This Project improves safety by reducing 
conflict points between turning cars and people walking or driving, giving pedestrians a head start to cross, improving 
visibility with better lighting and signal upgrades, helping emergency vehicles move through more easily, and making 
crosswalk ramps easier and safer to use.
The Project will execute the improvements identified in the LRSP which includes modifying the signal system to add 
protected left turns in all directions, leading pedestrian intervals, new signal heads with upgraded high visibility back 
plates, emergency vehicle preemption (EVP) for Poplar Avenue (EVP already exists on Humboldt Street), and accessible 
pedestrian signals. The Project will also reconfigure the intersection safety lighting and move some utility boxes out of 
pedestrian ramp surfaces. The Project will accomplish these improvements without changing any existing vehicle lane 
configurations or removing any on-street parking. The Engineer’s Estimate for the project is $406,200.
Outreach
In addition to the year of engagement to the community, SIC, and City Council as part of the LRSP, staff distributed 
informational mailers to residents near the project site in May 2025. Staff also delivered a presentation for the Project at 
the College Park Elementary campus in Spring 2025 as part of a construction update meeting conducted in collaboration 
with the San Mateo-Foster City Unified School District.
445 of 1111

--- page 446 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Project Bid
The Project was advertised on April 3, 2025, and a total of seven (7) bids were received and opened on May 1, 2025. The 
bids received are listed below in order of increasing bid amount. 
CONTRACTOR
BID AMOUNT
Phoenix Electric Company
$367,000
St. Francis Electric, LLC
$386,670
Ray’s Electric
$418,185
Tennyson Electric, LLC
$441,500
Bear Electrical Solutions, LLC
$458,000
Mike Brown Electric Company
$474,840
Liffey Electric
$535,000
Based on a review of the bids received, staff recommends Council award the agreement to Phoenix Electric Company as 
the lowest responsive, responsible bidder.
Staff also recommends a $55,000 contingency (approximately 15 percent of contract value) to be established because the 
Project is replacing four large traffic signal mast arm poles at a busy intersection near a freeway ramp and two schools, and 
the schedule is expected to coincide with construction at the College Park Elementary School site. These factors create a 
high level of complexity for the Project with greater potential for unforeseen circumstances.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in Poplar Avenue/Humboldt Street Signal Modify - Left Turn (460049) project to award this 
agreement in the amount of $367,000 and establish a contingency reserve of $55,000.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

of the provisions of Sections 1810 and 1816, inclusive, of the Labor Code of the State of California.

mediation. The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request. The mediator shall be agreed to by 
the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one name from mediators 
listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the California State Board of Mediation and Conciliation, 
or other agreed-upon service. The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties. Neither party shall be deemed the prevailing party. 
No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation and making a good faith 
attempt to reach a mediated settlement. The mediation process, once commenced by a meeting with the 
mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but not more than 60 days, unless the maximum 
time is extended by the parties.

Exhibit A  
Insurance Requirements for Construction Contracts 
Contractor shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract, and for five years thereafter, insurance 
against claims for injuries to persons or damages to property which may arise from or in connection with the 
performance of the work hereunder by the Contractor, his agents, representatives, employees, or subcontractors.  
MINIMUM SCOPE AND LIMIT OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:  
1. 
Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 covering CGL 
on an “occurrence” basis, including products and completed operations, property damage, bodily injury 
and personal & advertising injury with limits no less than $5,000,000 per occurrence. If a general 
aggregate limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location 
(ISO CG 25 03 or 25 04) or the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.   
2. 
Automobile Liability:  Insurance Services Office Form CA 0001 covering Code 1 (any auto), with 
limits no less than $5,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.  
3. 
Workers’ Compensation insurance as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employers’ Liability insurance with a limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury or 
disease.  
4.  
Builder’s Risk (Course of Construction) insurance utilizing an “All Risk” (Special Perils) coverage 
form, with limits equal to the completed value of the project and no coinsurance penalty provisions. 
5. 
Surety Bonds as described below. 
6. 
Contractors’ Pollution Legal Liability and/or Asbestos Legal Liability and/or Errors and 
Omissions (if project involves environmental hazards) with limits no less than $1,000,000 per 
occurrence or claim, and $2,000,000 policy aggregate. 
 
If the contractor maintains broader coverage and/or higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City 
requires and shall be entitled to the broader coverage and/or higher limits maintained by the contractor. Any 
available insurance proceeds in excess of the specified minimum limits of insurance and coverage shall be 
available to the City. 
Self-Insured Retentions  
Self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. At the option of the City, either: the 
contractor shall cause the insurer shall to reduce or eliminate such self-insured retentions as respects the City, its 
officers, officials, employees, and volunteers; or the Contractor shall provide a financial guarantee satisfactory to 
the City guaranteeing payment of losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses. 
The policy language shall provide, or be endorsed to provide, that the self-insured retention may be satisfied by 
either the named insured or City. 
Other Insurance Provisions  
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
1. 
The City, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers are to be covered as additional 
insureds on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations performed by or on 
behalf of the Contractor including materials, parts, or equipment furnished in connection with such work 
or operations and automobiles owned, leased, hired, or borrowed by or on behalf of the Contractor. 
General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to the Contractor’s insurance 
(at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, CG 11 85 or both CG 20 10, CG 20 26, CG 20 33, or CG 20 
38; and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
2. 
For any claims related to this project, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance coverage at least as broad as ISO CG 20 01 04 13 as respects the City, its officers, officials, 
employees, and volunteers. Any insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its officers, 
officials, employees, or volunteers shall be excess of the Contractor’s insurance and shall not 
contribute with it.  
3. 
Each insurance policy required by this clause shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except 
with notice to the City. 
452 of 1111

--- page 453 ---
Builder’s Risk (Course of Construction) Insurance  
Contractor may submit evidence of Builder’s Risk insurance in the form of Course of Construction coverage. Such 
coverage shall name the City as a loss payee as their interest may appear. 
If the project does not involve new or major reconstruction, at the option of the City, an Installation Floater may be 
acceptable. For such projects, a Property Installation Floater shall be obtained that provides for the improvement, 
remodel, modification, alteration, conversion or adjustment to existing buildings, structures, processes, machinery 
and equipment.  The Property Installation Floater shall provide property damage coverage for any building, 
structure, machinery or equipment damaged, impaired, broken, or destroyed during the performance of the Work, 
including during transit, installation, and testing at the City’s site. 
Claims Made Policies – (If at all possible avoid and require occurrence type CGL policies) 
If any coverage required is written on a claims-made coverage form:  
1. 
The retroactive date must be shown, and this date must be before the execution date of the contract or 
the beginning of contract work. 
2. 
Insurance must be maintained and evidence of insurance must be provided for at least five years after 
completion of contract work.  
3. 
If coverage is canceled or non-renewed, and not replaced with another claims-made policy form with a 
retroactive date prior to the contract effective, or start of work date, the Contractor must purchase 
extended reporting period coverage for a minimum of five years after completion of contract work.  
4. 
A copy of the claims reporting requirements must be submitted to the City for review.  
5. 
If the services involve lead-based paint or asbestos identification/remediation, the Contractors Pollution 
Liability policy shall not contain lead-based paint or asbestos exclusions. If the services involve mold 
identification/remediation, the Contractors Pollution Liability policy shall not contain a mold exclusion, 
and the definition of Pollution shall include microbial matter, including mold.  
 
Acceptability of Insurers  
Insurance is to be placed with insurers authorized to conduct business in the state with a current A.M. Best rating 
of no less than A: VII, unless otherwise acceptable to the City.  
Waiver of Subrogation  
Contractor hereby agrees to waive rights of subrogation which any insurer of Contractor may acquire 
from Contractor by virtue of the payment of any loss. Contractor agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be 
necessary to affect this waiver of subrogation. The Workers’ Compensation policy shall be endorsed with a 
waiver of subrogation in favor of the City for all work performed by the Contractor, its employees, agents and 
subcontractors.  
Verification of Coverage  
Contractor shall furnish the City with original Certificates of Insurance including all required amendatory 
endorsements (or copies of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause) and a copy 
of the Declarations and Endorsement Page of the CGL policy listing all policy endorsements to City before work 
begins. However, failure to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the 
Contractor’s obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements, required by these specifications, at any time.     
Subcontractors  
Contractor shall require and verify that all subcontractors maintain insurance meeting all the requirements stated 
herein, and Contractor shall ensure that City is an additional insured on insurance required from subcontractors. 
For CGL coverage subcontractors shall provide coverage with a form at least as broad as CG 20 38 04 13.

Maintenance bond 
 
The Payment Bond and the Performance Bond shall be in a sum equal to the contract price. If the Performance 
Bond provides for a one-year warranty a separate Maintenance Bond is not necessary.  If the warranty period 
specified in the contract is for longer than one year a Maintenance Bond equal to 10% of the contract price is 
required.  Bonds shall be duly executed by a responsible corporate surety, authorized to issue such bonds in the 
State of California and secured through an authorized agent with an office in California. 
Special Risks or Circumstances 
City reserves the right to modify these requirements, including limits, based on the nature of the risk, prior 
experience, insurer, coverage, or other circumstances.

City Council

Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
East 3rd Avenue Landfill Hydrogen Sulfide Monitoring – Agreement Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to appropriate $95,000 from the unassigned balance of the Landfill Fund to the Public Works operating 
budget; approve Amendment No. 1 to the Agreement with Stearns Conrad and Schmidt Consulting, Inc. dba SCS Engineers 
for monitoring and reporting services for hydrogen sulfide at the landfill in the amount of $57,650, for a new agreement 
total of $107,405; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $9,300; extend the term of the Agreement until 
December 31, 2026; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form 
presented and future amendments within the contingency amount. 
BACKGROUND:
The Department of Public Works administers professional service agreements for environmental monitoring and 
reporting services at the closed East Third Avenue Landfill to fulfill post-closure directives from the San Mateo County 
Environmental Health Services (County). Waste Discharge Requirements require monitoring and reporting for 30 years 
following the closure of the landfill. Stearns Conrad and Schmidt Consulting, Inc. dba SCS Engineers (SCS) provides 
groundwater and leachate monitoring and reporting, landfill cover inspections, survey of landfill surface and preparing iso-
settlement maps, landfill gas monitoring and reporting, operation and maintenance of the City’s combustible gas 
monitoring system, and on-call additional consulting services, as needed.
On July 29, 2024, the Department of Public Works awarded SCS an agreement in the amount of $49,775 to perform 
investigation work in the upland wetland area of the closed East 3rd Avenue Landfill to locate the source of hydrogen 
sulfide gas (H2S) and odor issues. Under this agreement, SCS provided document review, collection of liquid samples, H2S 
sampling and monitoring, biological assessment, and a ground penetrating radar survey. 
Additional investigation work is necessary and requires permits from several regulatory agencies. The scope of work for 
Amendment No. 1 includes meetings with regulators, staff, and the permitting subcontractor (LSA). SCS will also provide 
local enforcement agency notifications for work that extends into the toe of the existing landfill slope adjacent to the 
identified odor source area upon receipt of approved permits. 
Additional investigation and potential remediation costs are anticipated in FY 2025-26. The permitting effort included as 
part of Amendment No. 1 is necessary to advance additional investigation and possible remediation efforts. The proposed 
Capital Improvement Program Budget for FY 2025-26 includes an estimated $120,000 to support investigation and 
remediation. 
This work is necessary to advance quickly in order to investigate the source of H2S and odor issues. SCS has provided 
monitoring and reporting for the landfill for the last eight years and has consistently demonstrated a high level of 
455 of 1111

--- page 456 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
responsiveness, technical expertise, and client advocacy. SCS has a proven track record in helping the City meet all 
regulatory requirements and deadlines and reducing overall landfill monitoring costs and liabilities. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
After accounting for both routine monitoring and a supplemental investigation to evaluate H2S and odor concerns, the 
available FY 2024-25 budget for landfill monitoring services was exceeded sooner than anticipated. A supplemental budget 
appropriation of $95,000 of unassigned fund balance in the Landfill Fund is needed to increase the Public Works operating 
budget and cover the costs already incurred for the investigation, as well as the permitting work required before any 
further exploratory or remediation efforts can proceed. Sufficient funds are available in the unassigned fund balance of the 
Landfill Fund for this appropriation.  
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Approval of an amendment to the agreement with SCS for continued monitoring, reporting and investigatory work at the 
East Third Avenue Landfill is exempt from CEQA review as information gathering, because it consists of basic data 
collection, research, experimental management, and resource evaluation activities which do not result in a serious or 
major disturbance to an environmental resource. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15306.) In addition, approval of a budget 
appropriation from an unassigned fund balance of the Landfill fund to the Public Works Department operating budget is 
exempt from CEQA review, because it consists of a government fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to 
any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines 
Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

RESOLUTION NO. _____ (2025)
AUTHORIZATING AN APPROPRIATION IN THE AMOUNT OF $95,000 FROM THE UNASSIGNED FUND BALANCE 
OF THE LANDFILL FUND TO THE PUBLIC WORKS OPERATING BUDGET 
WHEREAS, the City Council also considers mid-year budget balancing through adjustments, 
appropriations, designations and transfers to adjust the current-year budget to meet anticipated changes in 
departmental budgetary needs; and
WHEREAS, the City entered into an agreement for Monitoring and Reporting Services for Hydrogen 
Sulfide Gas (H2S) at East 3rd Avenue Landfill with Stearns Conrad and Schmidt Consulting, Inc. dba SCS Engineers; 
and
WHEREAS, both routine monitoring and supplemental investigation to evaluate H2S and odor concerns 
exceeded the available Fiscal Year 2024-25 budget for landfill monitoring sooner than anticipated; and 
WHEREAS, additional investigation work in the upland wetland area of the landfill necessary and 
requires permits from several regulatory agencies, expanding the scope of work of the agreement; and
WHEREAS, an amendment to the Fiscal Year 2024-25 operating budget is necessary to address funding 
of those services; and
WHEREAS, sufficient funds are available in the unassigned fund balance of the Landfill Fund to satisfy 
the funding needs.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, RESOLVES that:
1. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines section 15378(b)(4), this 
action is categorically exempt from CEQA because it is a government fiscal activity that does not 
involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical 
impact on the environment.
2. An appropriation in the amount of $95,000 from the unassigned fund balance of the Landfill Fund to 
the Public Works operating budget is authorized.
