SCOPE OF WORK 
Under this work scope, SCS will provide permitting services to allow for work to be conducted in the 
upland wetland area of the Site. Services are anticipated to start this spring and are aimed to have 
permits in place toward the end of summer (contingent upon review and acceptance of the governing 
regulatory agencies). These services included associated meetings with regulators, City, and our 
permitting subcontractor during the permitting process. SCS will also include local enforcement agency 
(LEA) notifications for work that extends into the toe of the existing landfill slope adjacent to the 
identified odor source area upon receipt of approved permits. 
Please find details for each task of our proposed work scope below. 
Task 1 - U.S. ARMY CORP OF ENGINEERS (USACE) PERMIT 
Under Task 1, SCS will procure the services of and manage our subcontractor, LSA of Richmond, 
California, who is well-versed in the Site and will perform the required USACE Permit(s) for the Project. 
LSA has worked on numerous permits in the San Francisco Bay Area that have been approved for a 
myriad of projects. SCS has established a great rapport with LSA and they provided the constraints 
analysis for the Site in support of our Phase I. As part of this work, SCS will provide support to LSA 
during the permitting effort, develop the project description, and take part in any required USACE 
meeting(s) with LSA in performance of the work. 
Under Task 1, SCS and LSA assume that the Project will be able to be permitted under one of the 
Nationwide Permits (NWPs) issued by the USACE. NWP # 38 - Cleanup of Hazardous and Toxic Waste 
would most likely be the appropriate NWP for the project. NWPs allow for small fills of jurisdictional 
areas and do not require an in-depth Alternatives Analysis (AA) to demonstrate that no practical 
alternative exists that impacts less jurisdictional area. LSA will prepare a NWP Preconstruction 
Notification (PCN) package for submittal to the USACE in accordance with San Francisco District 
regulations and guidelines. The PCN will include all the required information, including the 
jurisdictional delineation in the biological constraints report. SCS will support LSA needs during this 
permitting effort and review the deliverable prior to forwarding it to the City for review/approval. 
Task 2 - RWQCB WATER QUALITY CERTIFICATION (WQC) 
Under Task 2, SCS will manage our subcontractor, LSA, who will perform the required Regional Water 
Quality Control Board (RWQCB) Permit(s) for the Project. SCS will assist LSA with the Water Quality 
Certification (WQC) requirement and provide regulatory meetings with LSA throughout the permitting 
process. 
The WQC from the RWQCB (San Francisco Bay Region) will be required pursuant to Section 401 of the 
Clean Water Act. The WQC application package will include an SCS cover letter describing the project 
and impacts to jurisdictional features, a copy of the USACE PCN package, RWQCB standard application 
Exhibit A1
461 of 1111

--- page 462 ---
form, figures depicting the type and extent of impacts to jurisdictional features, evidence of CEQA 
compliance, and an application filing fee. The application filing fee will be based on the type and extent 
of impacts in areas within the jurisdiction of RWQCB. Prior to finalizing the application, LSA will submit 
a draft package to SCS for our review and comment prior to SCS sending it to the City for 
review/comment. 
Task 3 - Alternatives Analysis (AA) 
A WQC application requires an AA which discusses potential alternatives to the proposed project that 
would be less impactful to jurisdictional features. Based on our phone conversations, we are assuming 
that impacts from the proposed project will be less than 0.10 acre which would qualify for a Tier 1 AA. 
The AA will describe the avoidance and minimization measures that will be implemented to protect 
water quality and beneficial uses, as well as include a description from the engineer related to how 
the impacts are minimized. SCS will assist LSA with the AA and provide input to LSA to meet this permit 
requirement 
Task 4 - Preconstruction Nesting Bird Survey 
Under Task 4, the Project will likely require a preconstruction survey for nesting birds protected 
under the federal Migratory Bird Treaty Act and the California Fish and Game Code. A qualified LSA 
wildlife biologist will conduct the preconstruction survey and a summary memo with the results of the 
preconstruction survey will be prepared. 
Task 5 - Project Management & City Meetings 
Under this task, SCS will provide overall management for the project and provide 4-hours of 
meetings with LSA, and four (4) 1-hour meetings with the City during the permitting process. 
Meetings will be held via Microsoft Teams. 
Once the above services are complete, SCS will provide a proposal for estimated remediation costs 
under separate cover. 
STAFFING 
Under this project, SCS will dedicate the following persons who will take the lead in this effort. 
Project Director/Technical Engineer - Maura Dougherty, P.E. 
Project Manager- Mark J. Erickson, E.I.T. 
Project Manager/Geologist - Patrick Harms, P.G. 
SCS will also provide additional support staff as part of the project team. Work under the contract will 
be managed by both Mark Erickson, E.I.T., and Patrick Harms, P.G. Mark Erickson and will be the main 
points of contact (POC) for the City.

SCHEDULE 
SCS will start our services immediately upon approval of this proposal given the timeframe 
for permitting to be complete and in place prior to performing remedial activities. Our 
permitting subcontractor, LSA, is aware of the urgency of this Project and has committed staff to the 
effort and will start their services as soon as SCS receives Notice to Proceed (NTP) from the City, 
463 of 1111

--- page 464 ---
ASSUMPTIONS/CLARIFICATIONS 
1. Under Task 1, there is no application fee for the USACE Permitting Services.
2. Under Task 2, the RWQCB application filing fee is based on the type and extent of impacts in
areas within the jurisdiction of RWQCB and fees are not included in our proposed services. These
fees are anticipated to cost $4,200 for Tier I alternatives analysis (disturbance area of 0.1 Acre). The
annual fee is estimated at $3,550. Therefore, approximate costs (based on the RWQCB fee
calculator for Dredge and Fill) for this permitting effort is anticipated to be $7,750.
3. Under Task 2, costs for a Biological Assessment (BA), should it be required during USACE
discussions with the USFWS, is included in our proposed services as an additional cost. If not
required, that Task 2 adder will not be billed to the City.
4. Under Task 3, SCS and LSA have estimated 5 hours each to provide for internal meetings on
required pre-field permitting activities. Should more time be required, SCS will charge additional time
under our current MSA (City Fees Schedule) with the City and markups to any required additional LSA
time. LSA hourly rates will be based on a Senior Biologist rate at $195/hour.
5. Under Task 4, SCS and LSA have estimated 4 hours each to provide to coordinate deliverable
efforts in performance of the work. Should more time be required, SCS will charge additional time
under our current MSA (City Fees Schedule) with the City and markups to any required additional LSA
time. LSA hourly rates will be based on a Senior Biologist rate at $195/hour. Additionally, field
related work for the nesting bird survey includes 8 hours of biologist services at $135/hour for field
surveys and 2 hours of senior-level biologist services at $195/hour for project management.
6. Under Task 5, SCS and LSA have estimated 4 hours each (four (4) 1-hour meetings) with the City
throughout the permitting process. Should more time be required, SCS will charge additional time
under our current MSA with the City and markups to any required additional LSA time, with approval
from the City. LSA hourly rates will be based on a Senior Biologist rate at $195/hour.

June 16th, 2025
SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Mosquito Abatement — Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a three-year agreement with the San Mateo County Mosquito and Vector Control District for mosquito 
monitoring and abatement at the Wastewater Treatment Plant in an amount not to exceed $45,000; establish a 
contingency reserve of $5,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the 
form presented and issue change orders within the contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The San Mateo County Mosquito and Vector Control District (District) provides countywide mosquito abatement services 
to the public as a public health service. The District operates pursuant to the statutory laws relating to mosquito 
abatement and pest abatement districts in California Health and Safety Code, Division 3, Chapter 1, Sections 2060-2910, 
inclusive.
The Wastewater Treatment Plant poses a potential risk for mosquito breeding. The proposed Abatement Services 
Agreement is for mosquito surveillance and control activities by the District at the Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) 
that cannot be handled within the normal scope of the District’s programs.
Pursuant to San Mateo Municipal Code section 3.60.050(g), contracts with other governmental agencies are exempt from 
the City’s purchasing ordinance. However, pursuant to City policy, intergovernmental contracts require City Council 
approval. Therefore, staff recommends the City Council approve a three-year agreement, through Fiscal Year 2027-28, with 
the District for mosquito monitoring and abatement services at the WWTP in an amount not to exceed $45,000, with a 
contingency reserve in the amount of $5,000.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Funding is budgeted annually in the WWTP Operations operating budget for these services. Pending approval and adoption of 
the Fiscal Year 2025-26 budget, sufficient funding will be available to cover the costs of the agreement.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Subject: Mosquito Abatement Services Agreement FY2025- 2026, 2026-2027, and 2027-2028 
Dear Mr. Fabry, 
Enclosed is the “Abatement Services Agreement” between our agencies for mosquito surveillance and 
control activities by the District for the wastewater treatment plant in San Mateo.   
The “Abatement Services Agreement” is designed to address unique mosquito control issues that cannot 
be managed within the normal scope of this District’s programs. It should be clearly understood that the 
residents of San Mateo will continue to receive the same level of public health service as before, in 
addition to the extra services provided by this agreement. As part of an integrated mosquito control plan, 
District personnel will conduct surveillance using CO2-baited traps and other traditional mosquito 
sampling methods at your location. Surveillance data will be used to develop a tailored mosquito control 
strategy that may involve the application of pesticides. Any questions about the treatment program should 
be directed to Casey Stevenson, Operations Director.   
This District operates in accordance with the statutory laws relating to mosquito abatement and pest 
abatement districts in California Health and Safety Code, Division 3, Chapter 1, Sections 2060 - 2910 
inclusive. Please sign, date, and return the original copy of the “Abatement Services Agreement” to the 
San Mateo County Mosquito and Vector Control District as soon as possible.  
Sincerely, 
Brian Weber  
District Manager

ABATEMENT SERVICES AGREEMENT 
The SAN MATEO COUNTY MOSQUITO AND VECTOR CONTROL DISTRICT (“District”) and 
CITY OF SAN MATEO WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT (“Owner”) agree as follows: 
1.
The District will perform abatement services for the Owner’s problem site as described below.  As
reasonably necessary, the District will develop and implement mosquito surveillance and control
activities with appropriate materials and equipment.
2.
Problem site:  Wastewater Treatment Plant at 2050 Detroit Dr, San Mateo.
3.
Owner shall reimburse District at a rate of $93.23 per man-hour for inspection and/or treatment work,
along with the cost of District-supplied materials.  Man-hours will include setting and inspection of
CO2 trap devices on or around the problem site.
 Approx.:  8 Months 2 people, 2 hours each week  
 Approx.:  4 Months 1 person, 2 hours every three weeks 
 Approx.:  3 – 20 Gal BVA2 @ approx. $11.52 gal 
4.
District personnel will inform the property owner if mosquito breeding habitats can be eliminated
without using pesticides (i.e., water circulation, fish stocking, and sealing entry/exit points). If non-
pesticide methods cannot be identified or if the recommended actions fail, District personnel will
implement control measures using public health pesticides, which may include materials that kill
adult mosquitoes (adulticides). The choice of appropriate materials will rely on surveillance results
and any additional information obtained by Mosquito and Vector Control staff. A 24-hour notice will
be given to Plant Management prior to adulticiding.
5.
The emergence of any unusual issues requiring extra manpower, equipment, and materials will
necessitate a change order as an addendum to this agreement. It is understood that any modifications
will be mutually agreed upon before the initiation of the order. All extra costs related to change orders
will be negotiated separately.
6.
The owner will pay for such services within 30 days of receipt of each bill.
7.
This agreement shall be effective July 1, 2025, through June 30, 2028.  Either party may terminate the
agreement upon 30 days’ written notice to the other party.
8.
During the term of this agreement, both contracting parties specifically waive the right to use the
abatement procedures set forth in Health and Safety Code Sections 2060 through 2067, inclusive.
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties execute this Agreement this _____ day of __________, 2025. 
San Mateo County Mosquito and Vector Control District

SUBJECT:
Hillsdale Caltrain Commuter Shuttle – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a two-year agreement with Commute.org (Peninsula Traffic Congestion Relief Alliance) for the Hillsdale Caltrain 
Commuter Shuttle in the amount of $169,028; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in 
substantially the form presented. 
BACKGROUND:
The City has historically collaborated with Commute.org to provide commuter shuttle bus services to key business parks 
and destinations within the city through biennial agreements. These commuter shuttle services are provided through a 
third-party vendor from the San Mateo County Transit District and managed by Commute.org. The City’s partnership with 
Commute.org is highly advantageous, with Commute.org managing shuttle logistics and contributing 75 percent of the 
funding, while the City is responsible for the remaining 25 percent. The most recent agreement, approved by the City 
Council on June 20, 2023, supports shuttle services on a split route (Hillsdale Commuter Caltrain (HCC) East and West), 
connecting the City’s Caltrain stations to high-ridership areas, including the San Mateo Medical Center, Alameda/20th, and 
office buildings near Concar/Amphlett. The agreement also updated the management and operational structure of the 
shuttle program and clarified mutual funding responsibilities between Commute.org and the City. The current agreement 
is set to expire on June 30, 2025.
Earlier this year, Commute.org submitted a grant application through the San Mateo County Transportation Authority’s 
(SMCTA) call for projects to secure funding for the continued operation of the City’s shuttle routes. In response to changes 
in the grant structure, the shuttle team proposed consolidating the existing routes into a single east route to enhance 
ridership without increasing operational demands. This redesigned route will connect high-ridership residential and 
commercial areas east of Highway 101 with the Hillsdale Caltrain station, while also creating opportunities for private 
funding of the commuter service. Key service streets include Norfolk Street, Fashion Island Boulevard, Mariners Island 
Boulevard, and Bridgepointe Parkway. Residential communities within the service area include Lakeshore, Harbortown, 
Edgewater Isle, and Mariners Isle. Notable commercial properties served include 1400 Gateway, 901 and 951 Mariners 
Island Boulevard, 999 Baker, and the Sony PlayStation complex. The route is structured to provide bi-directional service, 
facilitating morning commutes for employees to commercial locations and residents to the Hillsdale Caltrain station, with 
afternoon service transporting residents from the station along the route and employees back to the station.  A map of the 
revised route is included in the attached agreement.  
SMCTA approved the grant application for the HCC shuttle route, and this agreement formalizes the terms and conditions 
for operations. As in prior years, Commute.org will contribute 75 percent of the total cost and the City will be responsible 
for the remaining 25 percent of the cost. As the shuttle service is provided by a third-party vendor from the San Mateo 
County Transit District, and managed by Commute.org, the agreement also requires the shuttle vendor to indemnify 
479 of 1111

--- page 480 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
participating agencies, including the City. Given the shuttle route changes, City staff will coordinate with Commute.org to 
provide community updates ahead of the July 1st service start. This agreement is for a two-year term, in accordance with 
the funding agreement between Commute.org and SMCTA. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The total two-year agreement cost for shuttle operations is $676,111. Commute.org’s maximum contribution is 75 percent 
of the total cost, or $507,083. The City is responsible for the remaining portion of the total cost, equating to approximately 
25 percent, or $169,028, for the two-year term. There is sufficient funding in the Citywide Parking and Transportation 
budget for the City’s share of costs.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Approval of this agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that there is no 
possibility that entering into an agreement with Commute.org to provide shuttle services will have a significant effect on 
the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SHUTTLE BUS SERVICE ADMINISTRATION AGREEMENT
HILLSDALE CALTRAIN COMMUTER
This Shuttle Bus Service Administration Agreement ("Agreement") is entered into on 
_____________ ("Effective Date"), between the City of San Mateo ("Lead Organization"), and 
Peninsula Traffic Congestion Relief Alliance ("Commute.org"). For clarity, references to "the 
Parties" mean the Lead Organization and Commute.org.  
RECITALS
A.
Lead Organization desires to participate in a shuttle bus service ("Shuttle Service") 
as part of the Shuttle Bus Program ("Program"). 
B.
The purpose of this Agreement is to set forth the terms and conditions of Lead 
Organization's proposed participation in the Shuttle Service and in the Program.
AGREEMENT
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing, the Parties agree as 
follows:
1.
Authorization.  Subject to the terms and conditions set forth below, Lead 
Organization hereby is authorized to participate in the Program.  The Hillsdale Caltrain Commuter 
(“Shuttle”) shall operate to and from the Hillsdale Caltrain Station, in San Mateo, California, and 
employment and residential areas along the route shown on the map attached to this Agreement as 
Exhibit A, and described in the schedule attached to this Agreement as Exhibit B.  The route and 
frequency of the Shuttle may be reasonably modified by Commute.org from time to time.  Lead 
Organization understands and agrees that more than one participant may be served by the Shuttle.  
2.
Lead Organization.  Lead Organization acknowledges that it has been selected and 
accepts the position of Lead Organization on behalf of all organizations served by the Shuttle.  As 
Lead Organization, Lead Organization shall provide the projected minimum Twenty-Five Percent 
(25.0%) financial share, excluding contributions from Commute.org described under Section 5 of 
the Agreement, Maximum Contribution; Payment.
Lead Organization may seek appropriate contributions from participating organizations for 
reimbursement of amounts expended by Lead Organization hereunder, it being understood that 
Lead Organization shall not seek or make a profit from serving as Lead Organization.  
Commute.org has no responsibility to collect any such amounts from such other organizations 
participating in the Program to be serviced by the Shuttle.
3.
Shuttle Service Vendor.  Shuttle Service is operated by the San Mateo County 
Transit District's Shuttle Provider (“Vendor”) in full accordance with the terms and conditions of 
481 of 1111

--- page 482 ---
the contract between SamTrans and the Vendor. Commute.org or its designee will have daily 
management responsibilities for the Shuttle. 
Commute.org shall be responsible for supplying and/or completing any grant-related 
reporting information.  Lead Organization shall assist Commute.org in obtaining appropriate 
reporting information.
4.
Term.  The term of this Agreement shall commence on July 1, 2025 and end on 
June 30, 2027.  Any obligations which are incurred under the Agreement which are expressly 
provided to extend beyond the term of this Agreement, including any contribution obligations 
incurred by Commute.org pursuant to Section 5, shall remain valid until fulfilled as provided for 
in this Agreement.  This Agreement may be terminated by either party without cause by giving 
thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other in the manner provided below.  
All subsidy funds provided for the Program shall be generated from non-Commute.org 
revenue streams.  At no time shall Commute.org be liable for additional revenue not included as 
part of the external authorized grant or for expenses disallowed by the granting entities (grantor). 
Commute.org budget decisions are subject to the discretion of Commute.org's Executive Director 
and Commute.org's Board of Directors.  This Agreement will terminate without penalty, liability 
or expense of any kind to Commute.org, if funds are not appropriated for the current fiscal year to 
fund the Shuttle Services.  If funds are appropriated for a portion of the current fiscal year, this 
Agreement will terminate without penalty, liability or expense of any kind to Commute.org, at the 
end of the term for which the funds are appropriated.  If expected revenues, from pending or future 
grant applications, which are intended to fulfill any portion of Commute.org contribution under 
this Agreement, are not approved and/or received by Commute.org, this Agreement will terminate 
without penalty, liability or expense of any kind to Commute.org, at the end of the term for which 
the funds are appropriated.  Commute.org has no obligation to make appropriations for this 
Agreement in lieu of appropriations for new or other agreements.  
The term of this Agreement may be extended to cover additional funding periods, by 
mutual written agreement.  In the event that the Agreement term is extended, the Parties shall set 
forth any terms, including Commute.org's maximum contribution that will be applicable during 
the period of extension.
5.
Maximum Contribution; Payment.  Commute.org's maximum contribution (which 
includes contributions from all other funding agencies involved, if any) for Shuttle Service shall 
not exceed Five Hundred and Seven Thousand Eighty-Three Dollars ($507,083) or Seventy-Five 
point (75.0%) of the estimated total costs for the Shuttle Service as determined by Commute.org 
whichever amount is less.  
6.
Lead Organization's Payment.  Lead Organization shall pay Twenty-Five Percent 
(25.0%) of the total actual costs of the Shuttle Service described in Section 1 of this Agreement.  
Lead Organization's 25.0% share shall equal One Hundred and Sixty-Nine Thousand Twenty-
Eight Dollars ($169,028), subject to a final adjustment as provided below.  
Lead Organization shall pay based on quarterly invoices from Commute.org.  Lead 
Organization shall pay each invoice within thirty (30) days from the date of the invoice.  In the 
482 of 1111

--- page 483 ---
event 25.0% of the actual cost of operating the Shuttle during the term of this Agreement exceeds 
Lead Organization's total payments, excluding any Administration fee, upon adequate 
substantiation of such cost overrun, Lead Organization shall pay to Commute.org the difference 
within thirty (30) days of Commute.org's request for it.  All payments required under this 
Agreement shall be made by check or money order and delivered to the following address:
Commute.org
Attention: Shuttle Department
400 Oyster Point Blvd., Suite 409
South San Francisco, CA  94080 
Lead Organization shall be solely responsible for collecting any amounts owed by other 
organizations participating in the Program to be serviced by the Shuttle, if any. 
7.
Audit Requirements.  For the duration of this Agreement, and for a period of three 
years thereafter, Lead Organization shall make available, during normal business hours and upon 
reasonable notice, to Commute.org and/or the grantor supplying funds for this Shuttle Service, or 
an independent auditor selected by either or both of them, all records relating to Lead 
Organization's participation in the Program including, but not limited to, all records of receipts and 
expenditures, promptly upon request.  Commute.org and/or grantor shall bear its own costs 
connected with such audit, including the cost of an independent auditor.  Lead Organization shall 
only be responsible for its own costs for making available all records relating to Lead 
Organization's participation in the Program in the event such an audit is requested. 
8.
Reporting Requirements.  For the duration of this Agreement, including any 
extension terms, and for a period of three years thereafter, Lead Organization shall assist 
Commute.org with any reporting requirements related to the performance of this Agreement.  
Commute.org shall bear its own costs connected with such reporting.  Lead Organization shall 
only be responsible for its own costs for making available all records relating to the Lead 
Organization’s participation in the Program in the event such a report is requested.  Records may 
include, but are not limited to ridership reports, rider surveys, customer compliments/complaints, 
schedule variants and effective dates.
9.
Marketing Program.  The Commute.org countywide shuttle marketing program 
description will be attached and made a part of this agreement as Exhibit C – MARKETING 
PROGRAM.  Any brochures, handbooks, articles, news releases, timetables, web content, or other 
publications regarding the Shuttle (collectively “Collateral”) if provided by Lead Organization, 
shall occur at its sole expense with no additional reimbursement from Commute.org.  All such 
Collateral must be approved by Commute.org in advance of publication.  All Collateral shall 
identify the grantors as funding sources in a manner approved by Commute.org, with copies to 
Commute.org.  See Exhibit D – GRANTING ENTITIES.  During the term of this Agreement, 
funding sources may change.  Commute.org will notify the Lead Organization of such changes 
and any additional marketing requirements that may apply as a result.  The updates will not require 
Lead Organization consent.  Lead Organization shall comply with update for newly drafted 
marketing materials.
483 of 1111

--- page 484 ---
10.
Passenger Comments.  Lead Organization shall refer any passenger comments 
and/or complaints regarding quality of service or equipment, to Commute.org (650.588.1600) or 
website (Commute.org), unless otherwise instructed.

EXHIBIT C
MARKETING PROGRAM
(As of July 1, 2025)
489 of 1111

--- page 490 ---
Commute.org has developed a countywide shuttle marketing plan that serves as the basis for 
promoting the shuttle program throughout San Mateo County. Commute.org and its partners use 
a variety of tools to create and execute focused marketing for the shuttle program.
The Commute.org website serves as a primary source of information about the shuttle program. 
Riders, and potential riders, use the site to get information about routes and schedules as well as 
real-time vehicle location tracking. The mobile version of the Commute.org site is optimized to 
provide shuttle riders with streamlined access to the information they need during their commute.
Commute.org maintains and publishes route specific GTFS data that is used by transit planning 
apps including those managed by Google, Apple, 511 and app developers. Passengers can plan 
trips that include shuttles anywhere Commute.org shuttles operate. Route and schedule 
information is also available in hard-copy format.
Commute.org works with the cities and property owners where shuttles operate to install and 
maintain signage indicating where the shuttles stop. New signage is being installed at stop 
locations throughout San Mateo County. 
Commute.org shuttle and employer outreach staff provide presentations about the shuttle 
program directly to employers and their employees as well as at community events. Rider 
appreciation events are conducted throughout the year and allow the Commute.org team to thank 
shuttle riders for their commitment to using shuttles for first/last mile transportation.

June 16th, 2025
SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition Monitoring and Upgrade Project – Contract Change 
Order 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Contract Change Order #3 with Technology Management Professionals dba TMPros, LLC in the amount of 
$156,864 to monitor and maintain the Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition Network at the Wastewater Treatment 
Plant for a revised total contract amount of $485,408; extend the term through June 30, 2026; establish a contingency 
amount of $20,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the contract in substantially the form presented 
and issue change orders up to the contingency amount.
Background:
The Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) uses a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) network to remotely 
monitor and control equipment and processes at the WWTP, Dale Avenue Pump Station, and throughout the sewer 
collection system. The system is critical to maintain the operation of these facilities and the network must be monitored 
and maintained as necessary. Technology Management Professionals dba TMPros, LLC (TMPros) has demonstrated the 
competency to monitor, patch, and maintain the WWTP’s network on which the SCADA system operates. 
In June 2023, Council approved an agreement with TMPros for $174,140. In June 2024, Contract Change Order #1 was 
executed to extend the contract term through July 2025. In July 2024, Contract Change Order #2 was executed to increase 
the contract amount by $154,404 for a revised total contract amount of $328,544. 
As part of the Clean Water Program, WWTP staff are currently designing and configuring the SCADA system and network 
for the new wastewater treatment facility. The project upgrade will integrate SCADA systems between the existing WWTP 
and the new wastewater treatment facility. Staff recommends approving the contract change order with TMPros because 
they are already in the process of integrating the SCADA network system and assisting with the network design for the 
new facility, which has been delayed into next fiscal year. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Funding for these services is budgeted in the WWTP Maintenance operating budget. Pending approval and adoption of the 
FY 2025-26 budget, sufficient funding will be available to cover the cost of this contract change order. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
493 of 1111

--- page 494 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
This Council action is exempt from CEQA in that it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility this activity will 
have a significant effect on the environment, because this activity relates to software technology support and 
improvements without any necessary physical changes that may impact the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section   
15061(b)(3).)

PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Centrifuge Parts, Service and Repair Services — Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution approving the award of a three-year agreement with GEA Mechanical Equipment  US, Inc. (GEA) for 
parts, service, and repair services of centrifuges at the Wastewater Treatment Plant in the amount of $600,000, with two, 
one-year options to extend at $200,000 each, for a potential not-to-exceed total agreement amount of $1,000,000; and 
authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement and no-cost time extensions in substantially the form 
presented.
BACKGROUND:
Centrifuges are vital pieces of equipment in the Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) plant process. The centrifuges 
remove water from digested solids to produce a compatible material ("cake") that can then be hauled away to be used for 
either landfill alternate daily cover or land application.  Reducing the liquid content of the cake is critical to reducing the 
cost of hauling. These GEA/WESTFALIA brand units are the last step in the solids handling process and are crucial to 
maintaining compliance in operations. Currently, one of the WWTP’s centrifuges is inoperable, a second requires a major 
service, and the third is due for regular service.  If all the centrifuges become inoperable, digested sludge would not be 
able to be processed. 
For these reasons, being able to purchase parts in a timely manner as well as have GEA factory-trained technicians 
available to perform service is recommended to minimize downtime in the event an operating unit fails. Due to the precise 
nature of centrifuge operation, it is highly recommended to hire factory-trained technicians to complete these repairs. In 
the past, repairs have been conducted by third-party vendors with unsatisfactory results. Additionally, one of the 
centrifuge units is still covered by the manufacturer’s warranty which requires service to be performed by factory-trained 
technicians. GEA/WESTFALIA is the original manufacturer of these units and has factory-trained technicians available 
locally (Patterson, CA) to conduct service or repairs, both at their facility and on-site through GEA. Staff requested a 
proposal from GEA for maintaining the three centrifuges.  
In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.050(b), Council may approve an award of an agreement 
without competitive bidding when calling for bids would be impracticable, unavailing, or impossible. Here, since 
GEA/WESTFALIA is the manufacturer of the centrifuges, it is the only provider that has factory-trained technicians available 
to conduct the service and repairs in a satisfactory manner through GEA.  As provided above, the City has used third-party 
technicians in the past, with unsatisfactory results.  Given the urgent nature of getting the centrifuges serviced and 
repaired with qualified factory-trained technicians, staff recommends for Council adopt a Resolution approving the award 
of a three-year agreement with GEA for parts, service, and repair of the centrifuges at $200,000 annually, with two 
optional one-year extensions also at $200,000 per year, for a total potential agreement amount of $1,000,000, and 
499 of 1111

--- page 500 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
authorizing the Public Works Director to execute the agreement and no-cost time extensions.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the WWTP operating budget to cover the cost of the agreement. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Approval of an agreement for WWTP centrifuge parts, service, and repair services is categorically exempt from CEQA as an 
“existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor 
alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving 
negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution 
Att 2 – Proposed Agreement
STAFF CONTACT
Robert Learmonth, Wastewater Treatment Plant Manager
rlearmonth@cityofsanmateo.org
650-522-7386
500 of 1111

--- page 501 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2025)
APPROVING AN AGREEMENT WITH GEA MECHANICAL EQUIPMENT US, INC.
 FOR CENTRIFUGE PARTS, SERVICE, AND REPAIRS SERVICES
WHEREAS, the City’s Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) has three centrifuges to remove water from 
digested solids as part of the wastewater treatment process; and
WHEREAS, these GEA/WESTFALIA brand centrifuges need to be maintained and serviced to avoid the 
stoppage of the wastewater treatment plant; and
WHEREAS, one centrifuge is currently inoperable, and the remaining two need major service or regular 
service; and
WHEREAS, one of the centrifuge units is still covered by the manufacturer’s warranty which requires 
service to be performed by factory-trained technicians; and
WHEREAS, GEA Mechanical Equipment US, Inc. (GEA) provides maintenance and service to 
GEA/WESTFALIA manufactured centrifuges; and
WHEREAS, GEA provides factory-trained technicians to complete service and repairs; and 
               WHEREAS, treatment plant staff have previously used third-party companies other than the GEA to 
perform service with unsatisfactory results; and 
WHEREAS, in accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.050(b) agreements may be awarded without 
competitive bids if calling for bids would be impracticable, unavailing or impossible and the City Council sets 
forth findings designating the reasons; and
WHEREAS, City staff recommends approval of the agreement with GEA because they are the only 
provider of factory-trained technicians able to provide parts, service, and repairs for GEA/WESTFALIA brand 
centrifuges.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS and 
RESOLVES that:
1.
This Council action is exempt from CEQA review as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the 
operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public 
or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving 
negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.) 
2.
In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.050 (b), calling for bids would be 
impracticable, unavailing, or impossible because:  
a.
The WWTP utilizes three GEA/WESTFALIA brand centrifuges;
501 of 1111

--- page 502 ---
b.
One centrifuge remains under warranty which requires factory-trained technicians; 
c.
One centrifuge is currently inoperable while the remaining two centrifuges require major or 
regular service; 
d.
GEA Mechanical Equipment US, Inc. is the only provider with factory-trained technicians 
available to complete necessary service and repairs;
e.
Immediate maintenance is required for the centrifuges at the Wastewater Treatment Plant;
f.
Delay in maintenance may lead to the stoppage of the treatment plant and may incur 
additional cost to haul the liquid sludge.
3.
The agreement with GEA Mechanical Equipment US, Inc., for centrifuge parts, service, and repair 
services in an amount not to exceed of $1,000,000 is approved.
4.
The Public Works Director is authorized to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
submitted on behalf of the City including any necessary no-cost time extensions.

This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________ 2025, by and 
between the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of 
California ("CITY"), and GEA Mechanical Equipment U.S. Inc., a Delaware corporation ("CONTRACTOR"), 
whose address is 400 S. Wuthering Hills Dr. Janesville, WI 53546.
RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires certain equipment maintenance and repair  services on CONTRACTOR’S 
supplied equipment (the “Services”) hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage CONTRACTOR to provide these Services by reason of its 
qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONTRACTOR has offered to provide 
the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONTRACTOR under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement (the “Services”), which is attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONTRACTOR
CONTRACTOR shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONTRACTOR under this Agreement.  CONTRACTOR shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work. CONTRACTOR warrants 
that the Services (including all materials/spare parts) shall be in good working order and free from 
defects in materials and workmanship for a period of ninety (90) days.  In addition to the foregoing, and 
not in limitation thereof, CONTRACTOR hereby agrees to assign to CITY all rights obtained under 
warranties given by CONTRACTOR's third-party CONTRACTORs and suppliers with respect to the Services 
(including all materials) provided by CONTRACTOR hereunder to the extent same are assignable.  If any 
such warranty is not assignable, CONTRACTOR shall pursue same on CITY’s behalf against the warrantor.  
This warranty of material and workmanship is the only warranty made by CONTRACTOR and is in lieu of all 
other warranties, express or implied, and CONTRACTOR disclaims on behalf of itself and its suppliers any 
and all implied warranties, including, without limitation, warranties of merchantability, fitness for a specific 
purpose (other than the purpose stated in the CITY's specifications set forth in the contract), suitability or 
performance This warranty shall not cover (i) any defects arising from corrosion, abrasion, use of unsuitable 
lubricants, operation outside of prescribed temperature ranges, or negligent attendance or faulty 
operation, (ii) ordinary wear and tear, or (iii) any defects caused by errors on the part of CITY in not providing 
suitable premises in which the equipment is to be located, adequate foundation works, or adequate 
protection against influences within or outside the premises which may affect the equipment or its 
operation.  CONTRACTOR's obligation under this warranty is strictly and exclusively limited to furnishing 
repairs or replacements for equipment or parts determined to be defective on inspection by an authorized

The CITY is responsible for operating any equipment and use any Services provided hereunder in 
compliance with any applicable written instructions and safety guidelines provided by CONTRACTOR. – 
SECTION 4 - TERM
The Services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence upon execution of this 
Agreement and continue for a period of three (3) years. At the City’s discretion, up to two (2), one-year 
extensions may be exercised through an amendment of this Agreement, for a total potential 
agreement term of up to five (5) years. 
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY only for Services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full 
performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONTRACTOR a fee in an amount 
not to exceed $600,000.00 pursuant to rates stated in Exhibit B, attached and incorporated by 
reference. At its option, the City has two optional, one-year extensions at $200,000 for each extension, 
for a total potential agreement term of five years, and $1,000,000 total agreement amount.

SECTION 6 – TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONTRACTOR.  CONTRACTOR agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice. In such event, CITY shall pay CONTRACTOR for all Services performed to 
date plus any costs incurred in connection with preparing to complete the remaining Services that are 
not otherwise able to be cancelled without costs. 
SECTION 7 - RIGHT TO USE DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONTRACTOR in the performance of this Agreement are based on 
intellectual property of CONTRACTOR related to the maintenance and repair of CONTRACTOR’s 
equipment and shall remain the sole intellectual property of CONTRACTOR. CONTRACTOR hereby 
licenses to CITY the right to use anu such documents solely in connection with the use, maintenance 
and repair of the equipment that the Services are performed on..
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONTRACTOR in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONTRACTOR shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONTRACTOR or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.
All drawings, notebooks, operating data, specifications, and other information, data and material 
(“Information”) furnished to CITY by CONTRACTOR shall remain the proprietary and confidential property 
of CONTRACTOR, and shall be used by CITY only with respect to the work covered by the Contract and not 
in connection with any other project.  Information shall not be made available to any third party without 
CONTRACTOR's prior written consent. CITY shall not, not permit any third party to, reverse engineer, 
measure or otherwise technically examine or test CONTRACTOR's Goods.  Any Information shall only be 
disclosed by CITY to its employees on a need-to-know basis for the operation, maintenance, and repair of 
the Goods.  Intellectual property or patent rights which may be obtained on the basis of the Information 
or the Goods shall remain the exclusive property of CONTRACTOR. The City shall treat the Information as 
confidential as allowed under law.  In the event the City receives a California Public Record Act request for 
the Information, the CITY shall immediately notify CONTRACTOR so that CONTRACTOR may promptly seek 
an appropriate legal action to prevent disclosure of its Information.
SECTION 9 - INTEREST OF CONTRACTOR
CONTRACTOR covenants that it presently has no interest, and shall not acquire any interest, 
direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which would conflict in any manner or degree with the 
performance of the Services under this Agreement.

SECTION 10 - CONTRACTOR'S STATUS
It is expressly agreed that in the performance of the services required under this Agreement, 
CONTRACTOR shall at all times be considered an independent contractor as defined in Labor Code 
Section 3353, under control of the CITY as to the result of the work but not the means by which the 
result is accomplished.  Nothing herein shall be construed to make CONTRACTOR an agent or 
employee of CITY while providing services under this Agreement.
SECTION 11 – INDEMNITY AND DAMAGES
CONTRACTOR agrees to hold harmless and indemnify City of San Mateo and the Estero 
Municipal Improvement District, their elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents from and 
against any and all third party claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising out of CONTRACTOR’s 
negligence or willful misconduct or the performance of this Agreement to the extent such actions 
result in the personal injury or death or damage to personal property , except for those claims arising 
out of CITY’s negligence or willful misconduct.  CONTRACTOR agrees to defend City of San Mateo and 
the Estero Municipal Improvement District, their elected and appointed officials, employees, and 
agents against any such claims. The CONTRACTOR’S duty to indemnify shall survive expiration or early 
termination of this Agreement. 
In no event shall CONTRACTOR or its suppliers be liable in contract or in tort or under any other 
legal context or theory, including negligence and strict liability, for any special, punitive, indirect, incidental 
or consequential damages of any kind or character, including, but not limited to, loss of use of productive 
facilities or goods, costs of product recall, plant downtime, damage to or loss of product, chemicals, 
catalysts, feedstock or other raw materials, loss of revenues or profits or loss under purchases or contracts 
made in reliance on the performance or non-performance of the goods or Services, whether suffered by 
CITY or any third party.  Except as provided below, CONTRACTOR's and its suppliers' aggregate 
responsibility and liability, whether arising out of contract or tort or any other legal context or theory, 
including negligence and strict liability, all claims for breach of any warranty or guarantee, failure of 
performance or delay in performance by CONTRACTOR or performance or non-performance of the goods 
shall not exceed the price paid for the Services hereunder. Notwithstanding the foregoing, for any claims 
for which CONTRACTOR is responsible for indemnity as provided herein for personal injury or death or 
damage to personal property, the monetary cap in the preceding sentence shall not apply and instead a 
separate cap of $3,000,000.00 shall apply.
SECTION 12 - INSURANCE
Contractor shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract the insurance specified 
in Exhibit C to this Agreement.

It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONTRACTOR is

not operate as a release of CONTRACTOR from said obligation.
SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law.  The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or 
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any 
applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this provision that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 17 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONTRACTOR warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONTRACTOR nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.

SECTION 18 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.
SECTION 19 - LITIGATION
CONTRACTOR shall testify at CITY'S request if litigation is brought against CITY in connection 
with CONTRACTOR'S services under this Agreement.  Unless the action is brought by CONTRACTOR, or 
is based upon CONTRACTOR'S wrongdoing, CITY shall compensate CONTRACTOR for preparation for 
testimony, testimony, and travel at CONTRACTOR'S standard hourly rates at the time of actual 
testimony.
SECTION 20 - NOTICES
All notices hereunder shall be given in writing and mailed, postage prepaid, addressed as 
follows:

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
•
Minor Service Decanters
Definition:
Visual machine inspection
Inspection of conveyor scroll for wear
Inspection of solids discharge ports and bushings for wear
Lubricant change for Gear Box*
Check oil level and refill if required the central lubrication system
Replacement of oil for the scroll bearings
Inspection of temperature feelers and Oil Pulse sensors
Inspection and adjustment of v-belts and speed sensors
Inspection of vibration monitoring system
Replacement of gaskets and O-Rings for feed –tube
Greasing of motor*
Check functions of PLC (if supplied by GEA)
Test run unit on product or water
Check vibration of unit, both before** and after service
Provide written report detailing overall condition and recommendations
*All lubricants that are provided by customer must be per OEM recommendations.
** Vibration check prior to service when practical.
Parts in accordance with Annex 2
•
Major Service Decanters
Definition:
Visual machine inspection
Inspection of the drive system
When applicable, replacement of the planetary gear’s shaft complete (SMEK) with a factory reconditioned shaft
Replacement of main bowl bearings, seals and O-rings
Replacement of conveyor scroll bearings, seals and O-rings
Inspection of conveyor scroll and bowl discharge ports for wear
Oil exchange Gear Box*
Check oil level and refill the central lubrication system, if required*
Replacement of oil for the scroll bearings*
Replacement of V-belts
Replacement of gaskets and O-Rings for feed –tube; -line and liquid discharge line
Inspection of temperature sensors
Inspection and adjustment of speed sensors
Check functions of PLC (if supplied by GEA)
Test run unit on product or water
Check vibration of unit, both before** and after service
Provide written report detailing overall condition and recommendations
*All lubricants that are provided by customer must be per OEM recommendations.
** Vibration check prior to service when practical.

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits of $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit applies, 
either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the general 
aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if Contractor 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limits of $1,000,000 
per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limits of $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury or disease. 
If the contractor maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall be 
entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the contractor.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City of San Mateo and the Estero Municipal Improvement District, their elected and appointed 
officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on the auto policy for liability arising 
out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf of the Contractor; and on the 
CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations performed by or on behalf of the 
Contractor including materials, parts or equipment furnished in connection with such work or 
operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to the 
Contractor’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 
forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary insurance 
where the claim arises from Contractor’s negligence or willful misconduct as respects the City of San 
Mateo and the Estero Municipal Improvement District, their elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents. Any insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City of San Mateo and the 
Estero Municipal Improvement District, their elected and appointed officials, employees, or agents 
shall be excess of the Contractor’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Contractor shall notify the City of San Mateo of any coverage that is canceled, except after thirty (30) 
days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.

to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s

required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Citywide Custodial Services — Amendment 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 8 to the service agreement with Imperial Maintenance Services, Inc. for Citywide Custodial
Services to increase the agreement by $78,665.32, for a revised total agreement amount not to exceed $6,499,300.54; 
extend the agreement term an additional month to July 31, 2025; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of 
$8,000.00; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute Amendment No. 8 in substantially the form presented and 
issue change orders within the contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The Fleet and Facilities Division of Public Works contracts with Imperial Maintenance Services, Inc. (Imperial) to perform
scheduled cleaning of City facilities, keeping them in good condition for both staff and members of the public. In February 
2018, a three-year service agreement was awarded by City Council to Imperial for Citywide Custodial Services with an 
annual not-to-exceed amount of $747,600. The approval also allowed for five (5) optional annual extensions of the service 
with annual increases to the budget.
Since the execution of the 2018 agreement, Amendment Nos. 1 and 2 were approved and executed to adjust the term of 
the agreement to align with fiscal year end and set minimum wage requirements to align with the City’s minimum wage
Ordinance instead of the State’s minimum wage requirements. Amendments Nos. 3 through 7 were approved and 
executed to provide the five optional annual term extensions, add annual funding to the agreement, include additional 
cleaning services for new buildings, and account for annual Consumer Price Index (CPI) increases.
Amendment No. 8 will extend the agreement term for an additional month in order to ensure a continuation in custodial 
services until a new long-term contract is in place in August 2025, as well as increase the agreement amount to cover the 
costs of the additional month of service. Therefore, staff recommends amending the existing Citywide Custodial Services 
agreement to Imperial Maintenance Services, Inc. to avoid disruption in service.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Funding for Citywide custodial services is budgeted annually through the operating budgets of the Fleet and Building 
Maintenance Fund and the departments receiving these services for the City facilities each department manages. Pending 
approval and adoption of the Fiscal Year 2025-26 budget, sufficient funding will be available to cover the cost of this 
amendment.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical change 
in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)

SUBJECT:
Bay Meadows Community Facilities District - Special Tax Levy Fiscal Year 2025-26 — Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to authorize the levy of special taxes for Fiscal Year 2025-26 for the Community Facilities District No. 
2008-1 (Bay Meadows).
BACKGROUND:
There are various annual reporting requirements with different reporting dates related to the City of San Mateo 
Community Facilities District (CFD) No. 2008-1 (Bay Meadows). The requirements include City Council approval of the 
“Annual Levy of Special Tax” resolution authorizing the levy of special taxes within CFD No. 2008-1. The annual levy is 
necessary to pay debt service on the outstanding bonds and to pay for certain public services. Approval is due each year 
prior to the County of San Mateo’s July 31st deadline for the inclusion of such special taxes on the consolidated property 
tax bills pursuant to Government Code Section 53340 of the Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982 (the “Act”), or in 
the case of direct billing, at a different time or manner if necessary to meet its financial obligations. 
CFD No. 2008-1 special taxes are for public facilities within the district that encompasses a planned 1,066 residential units 
(832 units on residential parcels and 234 units on mixed-use parcels), 68 below market-rate rental units, 770,000 square 
feet of office space, 85,000 square feet of retail space, 31,500 square feet of professional space above ground floor retail, 
and approx. 135,000 square feet of high school campus and commercial space. The special tax levy is authorized pursuant 
to Section 53340 of the Government Code and Ordinance No. 2012-1.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The special taxes being levied hereunder are at the same rate or at a lower rate than provided by the Ordinance. The total 
fiscal year 2025-26 Special Tax Levy for CFD No. 2008-1 is $5,137,824.21.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This community facilities special district tax is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity 
which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact 
on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2025)
APPROVING THE FISCAL YEAR 2025-26 LEVY OF SPECIAL TAXES AND APPROVING AND AUTHORIZING RELATED 
ACTIONS AND DOCUMENTS
WHEREAS, the Council (the "City Council") of the City of San Mateo (the "City") conducted proceedings 
under and pursuant to the terms and provisions of the "Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982," being 
Chapter 2.5, Part 1, Division 2, Title 5 (beginning with Section 53311) of the Government Code of the State of 
California (the "Act"), to form its Community Facilities District No. 2008-1 (Bay Meadows), City of San Mateo, 
County of San Mateo, State of California (the "CFD”), to authorize the levy of special taxes (the “CFD Special 
Taxes”) to finance the acquisition and construction of certain public facilities, the payment of certain 
governmental development impact fees and the provision of certain services, and to authorize the issuance of 
debt to finance the facilities and fees, and to establish the appropriations limit for the CFD, all as set forth in the 
City Council's Resolution No. 85 (2008) (the "Resolution of Formation") and Resolution No. 86 (2008) (the 
"Resolution Deeming it Necessary to Incur Bonded Indebtedness," and together with the Resolution of Formation, 
the "Formation Resolutions"), both adopted on August 11, 2008; and
WHEREAS, the authorizations contained in the Formation Resolutions were submitted to the landowner 
qualified elector of the CFD at a special mailed-ballot election on August 11, 2008; and
WHEREAS, the landowner qualified elector within the CFD approved the authorizations contained in the 
Formation Resolutions by a unanimous vote; and
WHEREAS, the City Council commenced proceedings to change the authorizations contained in in the 
Formation Resolutions (the "2011 Change Proceedings") by adopting its Resolution No. 113 (2011) ("Resolution 
of Consideration") on October 3, 2011; and
WHEREAS, the Proposed Amendments contained and defined in the Resolution of Consideration were 
submitted to the landowner qualified elector of the CFD at a special mailed-ballot election on November 21, 2011; 
and
WHEREAS, the landowner qualified elector within the CFD approved the Proposed Amendments 
contained in the Resolution of Consideration by a unanimous vote; and
WHEREAS, the City Council levies special taxes in the CFD pursuant to Ordinance No. 2012-1, which was 
adopted by the City Council on January 3, 2012, which requires City Council approval of each fiscal year’s levy of 
CFD Special Taxes; and
WHEREAS, the Formation Resolutions authorized the issuance of up to $120,000,000 in special tax bonds; 
and
WHEREAS, the 2011 Change Proceedings reduced this authorization to $92,000,000; and
WHEREAS, pursuant to the Act the City issued three series of bonds for the CFD (the “Prior Bonds”):
521 of 1111

--- page 522 ---
(i)
$31,800,000 aggregate principal amount of City of San Mateo Community Facilities District No. 
2008-1 (Bay Meadows) Special Tax Bonds, Series 2012 (the “2012 Bonds”); and
(ii)
$26,000,000 aggregate principal amount of City of San Mateo Community Facilities District No. 
2008-1 (Bay Meadows) Special Tax Bonds, Series 2013 (the “2013 Bonds”); and
(iii)
$28,475,000 aggregate principal amount of City of San Mateo Community Facilities District No. 
2008-1 (Bay Meadows) Special Tax Bonds, Series 2014 (the "2014 Bonds"); and
WHEREAS, as a result of the issuance of the Prior Bonds in the aggregate principal amount of $86,275,000, 
the remaining authorization for the issuance of special tax bonds for the CFD (not including refunding bonds as 
defined in the Act) is $5,725,000; and
WHEREAS, for the purpose of refunding the Prior Bonds and achieving debt service savings, the City issued 
on June 30, 2022, its $72,345,000 City of San Mateo Community Facilities District No. 2008-1 (Bay Meadows) 
Special Tax Refunding Bonds, Series 2022 (the “2022 Refunding Bonds”); and
WHEREAS the Amended and Restated Rate and Method of Apportionment for the CFD (the “RMA”) 
provides that the City may not levy less than 100% of the Assigned Special Tax on Developed Property (as those 
terms are defined in the RMA) unless the City Council has covenanted that it will not issue any additional bonds 
for the CFD except refunding bonds; and
WHEREAS, in connection with the issuance of the 2022 Refunding Bonds, City staff worked with the Bay 
Meadows master developer, Wilson Meany, to confirm that all infrastructure improvements to be financed by the 
CFD have been made and that no additional CFD bonds will need to be issued in the future, except to refinance 
the outstanding bonds for savings; and
WHEREAS, the Council adopted City Council's Resolution No. 83 (2022) covenanting not to issue additional 
bonds for the CFD except refunding bonds, which will allow the City to pass to property owners in the CFD the 
economic benefit of the refunding of the Prior Bonds; and
WHEREAS, staff has prepared the proposed Fiscal Year 2025-26 levy of CFD Special Taxes and recommends 
that the Council approve the proposed levy; and
WHEREAS, the CFD encompasses approximately 800 projected residential units and a significant amount 
of non-residential square footage in the Bay Meadows project area; and
WHEREAS, approval of the special tax roll prior to the County of San Mateo’s July 31st deadline for 
inclusion of special taxes on consolidated property tax bills is required pursuant to Government Code Section 
53340 of the Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982; and
 WHEREAS, City of San Mateo Ordinance No. 2012-1 (the “Ordinance”) authorizes the Public Works 
Director to determine the special tax amount in accordance with the Rate and Method of Apportionment, prepare 
the annual special tax roll, and present the roll to the City Council for consideration; and
WHEREAS, the Ordinance requires that the Public Works Director be authorized to send the special tax, 
special tax roll, and all necessary and appropriate information to the County of San Mateo Auditor to be included 
in the County secured property tax rolls for collection each fiscal year.

FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
City Clerk's Office
MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
City Council and Boards and Commission Rules and Procedures – Update
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve the proposed amendments to the Rules and Procedures for both the City Council and Boards and Commissions to 
reflect the direction provided by City Council, primarily regarding the Chapter on conduct of Council Members and 
Commissioners.
BACKGROUND:
At an offsite, facilitator-led workshop on May 10, 2025, held at the San Mateo Senior Center, the City Council received a 
staff presentation, heard public comment, and directed staff to develop a formal enforcement mechanism for the City 
Council and Boards and Commissions Code of Conduct.
This direction builds on an initial discussion held during the February 22, 2025, Blue Sky Workshop and reflects the 
Council’s continued commitment to fostering values of respect, integrity, and transparency city governance. The proposed 
amendments are outlined in Attachments 1 and 3 (City Council and Boards and Commissions).
Below is a summary of the proposed City Council Code of Conduct enforcement process:
-
Informal Resolution: Where Council members are allegedly not following the rules set forth within the Code of 
Conduct, any concerned Council member is encouraged to resolve concerns on an informal basis, either through 
direct conversations or through private counseling by the Mayor (or Deputy Mayor, if the Mayor’s conduct is at 
issue).
-
Filing a Complaint: If the alleged offense(s) persist, or are serious in nature, two Council members may jointly 
submit a complaint to the City Clerk, with a copy to the City Manager and City Attorney, which will be agendized 
for initial Council consideration. The Council Member who is the subject of the complaint may include a response 
for inclusion within the agenda packet and/or verbally address the complaint at the Council meeting.
-
Initial Council Review:  At this initial council review, upon hearing the complaint, the Council may: (1) choose to 
take no action; (2) issue a reprimand, requiring additional ethics training be completed; (3) schedule the matter for 
a future hearing to consider a formal resolution of censure; or (4) Direct the City Manager or City Attorney to 
investigate the matter directly or through a third party, and bring back the results to the City Council following the 
investigation.
-
Censure Hearing: If a Council majority directs the scheduling of a hearing to consider a formal action of censure, it 
should appoint one or two Council Members to draft the proposed censure resolution.  The Council Member who 
is the subject of the complaint will have an opportunity to rebut allegations. However, the formal rules of evidence 
shall not apply, and the hearing shall be generally informal.
-
Final Action: Following the censure hearing, the Council may decide to: (1) take no action; 2) issue a reprimand, 
526 of 1111

--- page 527 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
requiring additional ethics training be completed; (3) adopt a formal resolution of censure, potentially including penalties 
including revoking committee assignments, revoking seniority in the mayoral rotation, and restriction of expenses, 
including travel. All decisions are final and not subject to appeal or reconsideration.
The enforcement process proposed for Boards and Commissions mirrors the steps outlined above for City Council, 
including informal resolution, complaint submission, Council review, and potential formal action. However, a key 
distinction is that all complaints involving Commissioners are initiated and addressed by the City Council, rather than the 
applicable Commission body. Specifically, Council Members are responsible for identifying and pursuing informal or formal 
resolution of any alleged misconduct by Commissioners. The Council retains full authority over the review, investigation, 
and determination of appropriate action in these matters. These proposed revisions are intended to apply only to future 
conduct and cannot be used to address actions that occurred before their adoption.
Related Amendments:
During the May 10, 2025, Special Meeting, Council provided staff with direction to make additional amendments related to 
the Code of Conduct for City Council and Boards and Commissions, these are summarized as follows. Referenced sections 
are from the City Council Code of Conduct, and the citation may differ for the Boards and Commissions document.
1.
Chapter 6 - Section 1: 
Council Members are committed to governing for all, striving to avoid any appearance of impropriety, and running 
our meetings with civility, decorum, and respect. With that spirit, San Mateo Council Members shall: …
2.
Chapter 6 – Section 1(M):
Speak for the City only when specifically authorized (see Chapter 10, Communication, sections regarding “Speaking 
for the City” and “Speaking as an Individual”).
3.
Chapter 6 – Section 1(P):
Adhere to all federal, state, and local laws in the performance of their public duties, including conflict-of-interest 
laws.
4.
Chapter 6 – Section 1(Q): (this is already part of the Code of Conduct for Board and Commissions)
Pay close attention to the meeting and limit the use of electronic devices while on the dais, except as necessary to 
access agenda materials or respond to urgent personal matters.
5.
Chapter 6 – Section 1(R):
When acting in a quasi-judicial capacity: keep an open mind, do not rush to pre-judge any matter, and be fair and 
neutral towards all concerned parties. This applies to statements that are verbal and written, including those 
posted on social media.  At the beginning of the hearing, disclose any ex parte communications (oral or written 
communications from one party outside of the hearing) and describe the nature and substance of the 
communication.
Ethics Training for All Commissioners:
At the direction of the City Council, all members of City Boards and Commissions will be required to take ethics training at 
least once every two years, beginning with a training in 2025. Thus, members will have more than six months to complete 
their first training.  Training designed for Assembly Bill 1234 (AB 1234) will meet this requirement. The City Clerk’s office 
will make training materials available online to Board and Commission members.  
1.
Chapter 6 – Section 4: 
Commissioners shall complete ethics training at least once every two years, beginning with calendar year 2025.
BUDGET IMPACT:
527 of 1111

--- page 528 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
There is no budgetary impact associated with this administrative action.

direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
