In Progress: The Human Resources Department is currently 
developing a proposal to establish a formal and customized 
“Recruitment, Marketing, and Outreach Plan” for the City of San 
Mateo. The purpose is to expand recruitment, marketing, and 
outreach activities in an effort to develop partnerships to build 
pipelines for City jobs and diversify the City’s applicant pool, 
including youth and seniors. Currently, we are in the planning 
phase in identifying options and developing work plans, timelines, 
and estimated costs.  
 
 
 
Information 
Technology 
Implement a 
Customer 
Relationship 
Management 
system 
Apply the DEI Strategic Framework to the 
selection and implementation of a CRM that 
will coordinate and manage citizen requests 
Complete: Information Technology included DEI considerations in 
the selection of the product to be implemented for the Customer 
Relationship Management RFP. Specifically, the RFP asks vendors 
to provide the following features and functionality: 
 
• 
Mobile device accessibility (for those without home 
computers or Internet, but have access to a smartphone) 
• 
Accessibility compliance with WCAG 2.1 Level AA 
standards to ensure accessibility for users with disabilities 
• 
Bilingual Public Support in both English and Spanish 
• 
Enhanced Accessibility Features including support of 
accessible attachments (e.g., tagged PDFs), high contrast 
themes, and allowance for text resizing without loss of 
functionality.  
 
Information Technology also required the system allow City staff 
to enter cases for those who call or walk-in to a City location for 
help (people with no access to technology).

Department 
Equity Initiative 
Work Plan 
Status Update 
Library 
Strengthen and 
expand outreach 
services as well as 
improve services to 
neurodiverse 
patrons. 
Purchase new outreach van. Hire full-time 
community services coordinator. Identify 
underserved areas where we can expand 
outreach. 
Complete: The library has made efforts to successfully carry out 
their initiative. This can be seen through monthly outreach to 
Hillcrest Juvenile Hall and consistent engagement with English 
language learners in local public high schools help us connect with 
underserved youth. For the past year, children’s librarians have 
visited Peninsula Family Services and Intercommunal Preschool 
monthly to provide story times. They prioritize visits to preschools 
that are state subsidized. In addition, The Library has maintained 
a strong presence at local cultural and resource events, including 
the B Street Fiesta, AAPI Cultural Event, and Lunar New Year 
celebrations. These events allow The Library to build meaningful 
connections with residents across linguistic, cultural, and 
socioeconomic backgrounds who live in different parts of the city. 
The Library has made an effort to offer programming in the North 
Central neighborhood with the bilingual cultural performance, 
San Mateo Juega, and an accompanying resource fair. The hiring 
of the new Community Services Coordinator greatly assisted the 
work for Juega and other San Mateo Celebrates events as we 
were able to effectively utilize volunteers. The new outreach van 
was purchased and The Library is in the process of designing a 
wrap for the vehicle.  
 
Additionally, The Library partners with the SMC Veterans 
Outreach Group for monthly tabling at the library and participate 
in events such as the SMCO Veterans Christmas Event and the 
Veteran's Curation Program Networking Event. They are also 
enhancing our senior outreach through a new partnership with 
enABLE Care Management, launching our first collaborative 
program in July 2025. Librarians make visits to local senior 
housing such as the Villa Apartments to inform residents about 
library services and resources.  
 
The Library's first Spanish-language gardening program was a 
success and reflects our commitment to expanding bilingual and 
culturally relevant programming. Outreach to the Peninsula 
Jewish Community Center and participation in school-based 
resource fairs further The Library's goal of reaching a wide range 
of communities. The children’s division added a Portuguese

Complete: In regard to Parks & Recreation, the Commission title 
was updated to "Senior Advisory Commission" following 
evaluations of other agencies and input from resident surveys 
after the Commission shared the original Senior Citizens 
Commission title may be perceived as exclusive to citizens. On 
December 9, 2024, the Municipal Code was revised to include the 
title update and broaden the commission’s scope, orienting it to 
address city-wide projects and programs beyond focus on the San 
Mateo Senior Center. This initiative reflects the growing needs of 
the senior community across the city and allows increased input 
on City services.

Ensure that the program evaluator includes

partnership strengthens our collective response to domestic

In alignment with our commitment to Diversity, Equity, and 
Inclusion (DEI), we are incorporating DEI principles throughout all 
facets of the DVERT program, including policy development, 
training, and hiring practices. To ensure authentic community 
representation, we have initiated the formation of a working 
group that includes select community members who will provide 
guidance and feedback as the program evolves. 
 
Additionally, the program evaluator has been directed to include 
DEI as a core component of the evaluation framework, with a 
strong emphasis on collecting and incorporating community 
feedback on the program’s progress and impact. 
 
Following an exhaustive recruitment effort, a promising candidate 
for the DVERT role has been identified. A comprehensive 
background investigation is currently underway and is expected 
to be completed shortly.

custodial contract is incorporated into the new custodial 
agreement that starts on July 1.  Facilities staff will continue 
deploying the distribution equipment after budget is replenished 
on July 1 with adoption of the FY 2025-26 operating budget.

Glossary
Appropriation
An authorization granted by the City Council to make 
expenditures and to incur obligations for specific purposes.
Audit
A review of the City’s accounts by an independent 
accounting firm to verify that the City’s financial statements 
accurately reflect the City’s financial position.
Base Budget
Estimate of the funding level, excluding limited term items, 
required to continue existing service levels during the next 
fiscal year.
Budget
A comprehensive financial plan of operation embodying an 
estimate of proposed expenditures for a given period and 
the proposed means of financing them.
Capital Acquisitions
Items of a permanent or semi-permanent nature that last 
multiple years.
Capital Improvements
A permanent major addition to the City’s real property 
assets including the design, construction, purchase, or 
major renovation of land, buildings, or facilities.  Examples 
are the installation or repair of new or existing streets, 
traffic signals, sewer lines, roads, and parks.
Capital Improvement Program (CIP)
An ongoing plan of single and multiple-year capital 
expenditures, which is updated annually.
Compensated Absences
An employee’s paid time off of work, which can consist of 
elements such as vacation time and sick leave.
Debt Service
The payment of interest and principal on borrowed funds.
Deficit
The result of an excess of expenditures over resources.
Department
A major administrative division of the City with overall 
management responsibility for an operation or a group of 
related operations within a functional area.
Depreciation
(1) Expiration in the service life of capital outlay assets
attributable to wear and tear, deterioration, action of the
physical elements, inadequacy, or obsolescence; (2) the
portion of the cost of a capital asset that is charged as an
expense during a particular period.
Division
An organizational subunit of a department which 
encompasses a substantial portion of the duties assigned 
to a department.
Encumbrance
A commitment of funds for goods or services for which the 
expenditure has not yet occurred.
Enterprise Fund
A fund established to account for operations of the City that 
are financed and operated in a manner similar to private 
business enterprises.
Expenditure
The amount of cash paid or to be paid for services 
rendered, goods received, or an asset purchased.
Fiscal Year
The twelve-month period to which the annual budget 
applies.  In San Mateo, this period of time is July 1 through 
June 30.
Full Time Equivalent (FTE)
Conversion of a position to full-time equivalent.  For 
example, one person working half time would count as 0.50 
FTE; one person working full time would count as 1.00 FTE.
Fund
A separate, independent accounting entity used to set forth 
the financial position of operations related to the specific 
purpose for which the fund was created.  Examples of funds 
used in the City of San Mateo are the General Fund, Capital 
Projects Fund, and Sewer Fund.
Fund Balance
The total dollars remaining in a fund after current 
expenditures for operations and capital improvements are 
subtracted.
General Fund
The City’s principal operating fund, which is supported by 
general taxes and fees and which can be used for any legal 
governmental purpose.
208
952 of 1111

--- page 953 ---
Grant
A contribution by a government or other organization to 
support a particular function or project.  Grants may be 
classified as either categorical or block, depending upon 
the amount of discretion allowed the recipient.
Interdepartmental Charges and Credits
A mechanism through which one program may bill another 
program (charges) and be reimbursed (credits) for the cost 
of various services provided.  Interdepartmental charges 
and credits are based on the actual, direct costs of the 
service provided.  They function as cost allocation tools, 
allowing the financial reports to more accurately reflect the 
real cost of individual programs.
Interfund Transfers
The movement of money from one fund to another to cover 
certain costs.
Internal Service Fund
A fund which is used to finance and account for goods and/
or services provided by one City department to other City 
departments on a cost-reimbursement basis.
Non-departmental
Program costs that do not relate to any one particular 
department, but represent costs of a general, City-wide 
nature.
Non-recurring Costs
One-time activities for which the expenditure should be 
budgeted only in the fiscal year in which the activity is 
undertaken.
Operating Budget
Annual appropriation of funds to support ongoing program 
costs, including employee services, maintenance, supplies, 
and equipment.
Ordinance
A formal legislative enactment by the City Council, 
which has the full force and effect of law within the City 
boundaries unless it is in conflict with any higher form of 
law, such as a state statute or constitutional provision.
Program
An activity or group of activities which is an operational 
subunit of a department and is directed toward providing 
a particular service or support function.  Each City 
department may be responsible for a number of programs.
Proprietary Fund
A term also used to describe funds that are accounted for 
on a “flow of economic resources” measurement basis and 
on a full accrual accounting basis.  Enterprise funds and 
internal service funds are classified as proprietary funds.
Reserve
Funds set aside for specific purposes.
Resolution
A special order by the City Council which requires less 
legal formality than an ordinance in terms of public notice 
prior to approval.
Resources
Total amount available for appropriation, including 
estimated revenues, beginning fund balances, and fund 
transfers.
Revenue
Income received during the fiscal year from taxes, fees, 
permits, franchises, interest, intergovernmental, and other 
sources.
Special Revenue Fund
A fund used to account for revenue sources that are 
restricted by law or administrative action to expenditures 
for specified purposes.
Structural Deficit
The permanent financial gap that results when ongoing 
revenues do not match or keep pace with ongoing 
expenditures.
Glossary
209

MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee Increase for Fiscal Year 2025-26 — Adoption 
RECOMMENDATION:
Conduct a public hearing and adopt a Resolution to increase the Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee by 2.4 
percent to account for the annual Consumer Price Index (CPI) increase.  
BACKGROUND:
The City of San Mateo operates and maintains a stormwater system that provides vital services to protect neighborhoods 
from flooding and safeguard local waterways from pollution. This system comprises complex infrastructure, including 
surface drains, underground pipes and conduits, pump stations, creeks and channels, and the Marina Lagoon. 
Improvements are needed to address flood control capacity limitations, aging infrastructure, enhanced operations and 
maintenance, and compliance with state and federal mandates to reduce pollutants in stormwater runoff. 
The City’s stormwater system requires long-overdue upgrades to improve flood control and replace aging infrastructure. 
Prior to 2024, the system lacked a dedicated funding source, and available funds were insufficient to adequately maintain 
and upgrade the drainage system.
On July 17, 2023, the City Council approved and adopted the Stormwater Fee Report, which outlines the financial 
requirements for implementing citywide stormwater improvements. At that meeting, the Council directed staff to initiate 
a Proposition 218 process to establish a new property-related fee. On January 16, 2024, following a two-month balloting 
process, the City Council certified the results of the vote and ordered the levying of the Community Flood and Storm 
Protection Fee (Stormwater Fee) for Fiscal Year 2024–25.
Beginning in Fiscal Year 2025–26 and for all future years, the Stormwater Fee is subject to an annual adjustment based on 
the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) for the San Francisco Bay Area, as of each December, with a 
maximum allowable increase of 3 percent. The CPI increase from December was 2.4 percent. Furthermore, any portion of 
the CPI above 3 percent is to be reserved as “Unused CPI” and may be applied in future years when the CPI is less than 3 
percent. Since the CPI increase this year was below the cap, no Unused CPI will be banked. Pursuant to California Health & 
Safety Code Section 5473.1, the City Council must hold a public hearing before approving this annual adjustment. 
The following table shows the proposed changes in monthly fees for Fiscal Year 2025-26.
Land Use Category
Monthly Fee

Funds from the Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee for the initial FY 2024–25 have been allocated to support 
operations and maintenance staff in the ongoing cleaning of storm catch basins, as well as critical capital improvements 
for minor storm drain “hotspot” repairs at several locations identified by engineering and operations staff.
Future revenues from this fee will continue to support these functions, along with major capital improvements to be 
identified in the upcoming Storm Drain Master Plan update and Marina Lagoon dredging projects. Staff will present the 
updated Master Plan to the City Council later this year for review of the proposed capital improvements.
Staff recommends that the City Council adopt the attached Resolution to increase the Community Flood and Storm 
Protection Fee by 2.4 percent for all parcels citywide.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Revenues from Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee for Fiscal Year 2024-25, after minor corrections to individual 
parcels, totaled $3,903,989.57. The proposed CPI increase of 2.4 percent will increase anticipated Fiscal Year 2025-26 
revenue in the Storm Fund by approximately $93,700, for an estimated total of $3,997,700.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This Council action adopting a Resolution to establish the Community Flood and Storm Protection Fee rate for Fiscal Year 
2025-26 is not a project subject to CEQA, because it consists of a government funding mechanism or other government 
fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant 
physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
Pursuant To Government Code Section 6066, notice of the public hearing was published twice in the San Mateo Daily 
Journal on May 30, 2025, and June 6, 2025, respectively.

FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:

MEETING DATE:
June 16, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy — Policy Feedback
RECOMMENDATION:
Receive an informational report on the "Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy" project and provide direction 
on the policy pathways for inclusion in the final Sustainable Building Strategy.
BACKGROUND:
Building energy use represents 38 percent of communitywide greenhouse gas emissions in San Mateo. Building 
electrification refers to the transition from gas equipment to electric equipment for space and water heating, clothes 
drying, cooking, and more. While natural gas is a fossil fuel, Peninsula Clean Energy (PCE) provides carbon-free electricity 
communitywide. Electrifying existing buildings is critical to reducing communitywide greenhouse gas emissions and is a key 
strategy in attaining the objectives contained in City’s adopted Climate Action Plan. To continue to advance existing 
building electrification beyond reach codes and to meet the City Council decarbonization priority, City staff began working 
with Rincon Consultants to develop the Electrify San Mateo: Sustainability Building Strategy. This project aims to create an 
actionable roadmap with steps for the City of San Mateo to electrify buildings based on effectiveness and equity criteria, 
cost-effectiveness, best available science and data, and community feedback. The project team completed a building 
inventory and market segmentation analysis that was presented at the September 13, 2023, Sustainability and 
Infrastructure Commission meeting. After completing Phase I of community engagement, the project culminated in the 
identification of five policy pathways that were presented at the January 16, 2024, City Council meeting. City Council 
directed the project team to conduct community engagement on all five policy pathways to determine overall feasibility 
and support from the community. This report provides an overview of the project, analysis of the five policy pathways that 
will be considered by City Council and a summary of project community engagement.
Building Electrification Background
Electrification benefits include the added health benefit of reducing gas use in the home. Commonly used appliances that 
burn gas, including stoves, heating systems, and water heaters, emit hazardous air pollutants inside a home when 
improperly ventilated, or when leaks occur. These can include benzene, nitrogen oxides, sulfur oxides, carbon monoxide, 
formaldehyde, particulate matter and other harmful pollutants that increase the risk of respiratory disease such as 
asthma. Additionally, gas infrastructure carries an inherent safety risk due to the volatile nature of gas, including risk of 
methane leakage, damage during earthquakes, accidental explosions, or fires. 
Building electrification is also an essential approach to driving the state of California toward its ambitious climate goal of 
carbon neutrality by 2045. Additionally, at the regional level the Bay Area Air District (BAAD) adopted zero nitrogen oxides 
(NOx) standards that will prohibit the sale and installation of gas water heaters starting in 2027 and gas furnaces in 2029 in 
the nine-county Bay Area. These standards are the most ambitious in the country and outpace the state’s goal of banning 
the sale of new gas furnaces and water heaters throughout the state starting in 2030. 
975 of 1111

--- page 976 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
As part of the FY 2022-2023 goal setting session, the City Council directed staff to present to them policy options to 
decarbonize and electrify existing buildings and eliminate natural gas use by 2030. The City’s Climate Action Plan also 
identifies electrifying existing buildings as a key strategy to reduce communitywide greenhouse gas emissions. The City has 
shown great leadership in advancing building electrification through the adoption of local amendments to the state building 
code also known as reach codes. In 2020, the City Council adopted a reach code requiring all new residential and office 
buildings be all-electric starting on January 1, 2021. In 2022, the City Council adopted a reach code that expanded the all-
electric requirement to all new buildings and requiring electric-readiness and electric appliances for specific home remodels 
that started on January 1, 2023. In 2024, due to a federal court decision, the City suspended the enforcement of its reach 
codes that prohibit the installation of new gas infrastructure. The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in California Restaurant 
Association v. City of Berkeley determined that a local ordinance that generally prohibited the installation of natural gas 
infrastructure was preempted by federal law, namely the Energy Policy and Conservation Act (EPCA). In January 2025, the 
San Mateo City Council adopted an Energy Performance Reach Code for new construction that requires energy compliance 
margins for new buildings and repealed the unenforceable all-electric requirement.
Policy Pathways
The project team gathered feedback on five existing building electrification policy pathways. These policy pathways are 
meant to be considered as a menu of options to achieve the City’s climate goals, while considering implementation and 
cost challenges for property owners across the City. Most local governments considering a building decarbonization 
strategy combine multiple strategies together to produce the most comprehensive, and equitable policy result. The 
following provides a high-level summary of the policy pathways. The Policy Pathways Analysis attachment provides 
detailed discussion of each policy pathway including advantages and challenges of policy implementation, greenhouse gas 
emission reduction impact, cost effectiveness and equity considerations.
1.
Business as usual: The City could continue with adopting reach codes for new construction and focus on 
outreach and incentives. In the past, the City adopted local building code amendments to require more 
sustainable new construction than the State code. This approach has a limited impact because new 
construction accounts for a fraction of San Mateo’s development, however, this approach is already in place 
and thus received community support to date. There are no policy-related additional costs to building owners 
unless they voluntarily decide to electrify. A Zero NOx new construction approach would save an estimated 
1,770 MT CO2e per year by 2030 while a performance approach would save 20 MT CO2e per year by 2030.
 
2.
Reach codes for renovations: This approach would include the evaluation of additional reach codes to 
encourage and/or require electrification during renovations. New reach codes would vary depending on the 
updated State codes. One example of a reach code during renovations is requiring the installation of a heat 
pump when an air conditioner is installed. Reach codes during renovations help encourage electrification at an 
opportune time when a property owner is already considering home improvements. The upfront costs of 
electrification improvements can vary depending on the project and unique circumstances of your home. Cost-
effectiveness studies must be completed to validate any adopted reach codes. Past studies have shown on-bill 
savings over time make electric equipment upgrades cost-effective compared to gas equipment replacements. 
Two-Way AC reach code would reduce emissions by 2,200 MT CO2e per year by 2030, while a Flex Path reach 
code would reduce emissions by approximately 730 MT CO2e per year by 2030.
 
3.
Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of BAAD Zero NOx requirements: The Bay Area Air District 
(BAAD) adopted zero nitrogen oxides (NOx) standards that will prohibit the sale and installation of gas water 
heaters starting in 2027 and gas furnaces in 2029 in the nine-county Bay Area. A local ordinance ahead of 
these milestones would require the replacement of gas appliances with zero NOx versions when they fail or 
are voluntarily upgraded. This policy would have the most significant greenhouse gas emission reductions of 
the five policy pathways with an estimated 9,700 MTCO2e per year by 2030.
976 of 1111

--- page 977 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
 
4.
Commercial Building Performance Standards: This pathway would establish a new set of policy standards for 
commercial buildings aimed at increasing energy efficiency, and eventually, electrifying commercial buildings 
as a phased approach. This is a well-established policy mechanism that has been adopted by many local 
governments across the country. This approach can be customizable and lead to incremental reductions in 
energy use but not necessarily electrification. The development of such a local program would necessitate new 
staff time. Emissions reductions will likely not occur before 2030 due to the phased nature of the program. 
Commercial buildings account for 16 percent of communitywide emissions (83,660 MTCO2e) and thus 
represent an important opportunity for GHG reduction.
5.
Municipal Electric First Policy: This policy establishes a mechanism for local government renovations to go 
electric first. The policy ensures financial, and feasibility data is being collected for municipal facilities that will 
allow the City to compare costs to electrify versus replacing with gas equipment. This policy has a limited reach 
as it only applies to municipal buildings. It demonstrates City leadership on hard-to-electrify existing buildings 
that mirror the commercial sector. This policy is estimated to reduce emissions 146 MT CO2e by 2030.
Community Engagement
The project team conducted two phases of project outreach. The first phase of community engagement focused on 
educating the community about the costs and benefits of building electrification and about the project’s general goals. 
Outreach also focused on gathering feedback on the equity and effectiveness criteria the project team would use to 
evaluate policy pathways. The top concerns were costs, power outages and grid resilience, and the growth of the grid to 
accommodate future electric buildings. The top values were how the climate crisis is a priority issue, the need for the City 
to act now, and the availability of electric equipment incentives and rebates. 
 
The second phase of community engagement aimed to get specific feedback on the five policy pathways as directed by 
City Council in January 2024. The project team conducted presentations at a variety of in-person and virtual community 
meetings, City Commission meetings, engaged with commercial property owners and developers, and conducted an online 
survey. Throughout both phases of engagement, the project team heard from individuals with strong opinions on building 
electrification policy. While many community members continue to be highly supportive of local action, others are firmly 
opposed to additional local policy. Throughout the second phase of community engagement, the project team provided 9 
presentations and reached over 150 people through in-person and virtual events. In addition, over 200 people responded 
to the online feedback survey and staff maintains a list of over 200 emails of community members and stakeholders 
interested in this project. 
At the May 14, 2025, Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission meeting, the project team presented the five policy 
pathways, along with community feedback obtained up until that point in time, and commissioners provided feedback. 
Commissioners supported policy pathways 1, 2, 4 and 5 to advance existing building decarbonization and meet climate 
goals. Commissioners noted the importance of funding to help homeowners with the transition, suggested the City help 
provide funding and emphasized the importance of PCE’s Loan program. Commissioners also recommended the 
incorporation of a hardship exemption into potential ordinance language to address cases that are hard-to-electrify. Two 
commissioners expressed interest in Option 3 Zero NOx standards ahead of the Bay Area Air District but noted that both 
the timeline for this policy and enforcement would be very challenging. 
The project team also gathered feedback from the Senior Advisory Commission on May 12, 2025, and the Community 
Relations Commission on May 21, 2025, which is charged with providing the City’s DEI lens. The Senior Advisory 
Commissioners voiced concerns about the upfront costs of electrification but there was some consensus and support over 
the intent of building decarbonization and of Options 4 and 5. The Community Relations Commission discussed various 
ways to improve marketing to various community groups and emphasized the importance of equitable transition in 
existing building decarbonization. The Community Relations Commission unanimously supported all five policy pathways.
In summary, the project team found: 
977 of 1111

--- page 978 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
•
Option 1 Reach Codes for New Construction (Business as Usual), Option 4 Commercial Building Performance 
Standards and Option 5 Municipal Electric First Policy received the most community support. 
•
Option 3 Zero NOx Standards ahead of the Bay Area Air District, received the least amount of community support. 
•
Option 2 Reach Codes for Renovations received a mix of support and opposition. 
Detailed community feedback and survey results for each project phase are provided in the Community Engagement 
Summary attachment. Detailed community feedback as related to each policy pathway is provided in the Policy Pathways 
Analysis attachment.
Next Steps
Staff seeks City Council direction on which of the five policy pathways to include in the final “Electrify San Mateo: 
Sustainable Building Strategy.” Specifically, does the Council recommend inclusion of:
•
Option 1 Reach Codes for New Construction (Business as Usual) —most strongly supported,
•
Option 2 Reach Codes for Renovations—mix of support and opposition,
•
Option 3 Zero NOx Standards ahead of the Bay Area Air District—not well supported,
•
Option 4 Commercial Building Performance Standards—most strongly supported, or
•
Option 5 Municipal Electric First Policy—most strongly supported.
The City Council can recommend inclusion of a subset of policy pathways or recommend inclusion of all policy pathways in 
the Sustainable Building Strategy. After City Council provides direction, the project team will compile the policy pathways 
into a final document that will incorporate the project work completed to date including building and market 
segmentation analysis and electrification cost analysis. The project team aims to bring the final document for adoption at 
an upcoming City Council meeting, which could be July 28, 2025, depending on the options selected.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The development of the "Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy" was funded by an appropriation from the 
available fund balance in the Advance Planning Fund (Fund 225) and had no impact on the City's General Fund. 
The different policy pathways presented in this report have varying budget impacts for implementation. The 
implementation of reach codes (Options 1 and 2) is integrated within existing City processes for planning application, plan 
check and building permit review. The enforcement of Option 3 would also be integrated within City’s permit issuance 
process. The implementation of Option 4 would be the most resource-intensive of the five policy pathways and would 
require staff time for the development of an ordinance and to staff the new program moving forward. The cost impacts of 
Option 5 would vary depending on the facility equipment replacement needs; technical assistance programs, grants and 
rebates are available to support municipal electrification. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This informational report is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a 
physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Hello,
 
Switching from natural gas to carbon-free electric equipment in our homes and buildings is critical to
reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in our community. Gas appliances release air pollutants and
studies have shown that exposure to these pollutants pose significant health risks. In addition to
advancing our climate goals, electrification eliminates these risks and can provide a safer and healthier
environment. The Electrify San Mateo project team will present existing building electrification policy
pathways at three upcoming City Commission meetings:
May 12: Senior Advisory Commission
May 14: Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission
May 21: Community Relations Commission
 
We invite you to share your feedback and shape the Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy.
Community feedback will be shared with City Council tentatively scheduled for June 2025. Please see
the Public Meeting Portal for meeting agenda and information posted roughly one week before the
Commission meetings.

--- page 981 ---
1
Report Review: 
 
Sustainable Buildings Strategy  
Project Update - May Evaluation 
 
 
Summary: 
 
This review is provided in the form of a due diligence evaluation and consumer protection for 
adopting full electrification to existing structures in the City of San Mateo.  There appears to be 
numerous deficiencies in information and consumer safeguards addressed.  No indication that a 
particular initiative that meets or exceeds the BAAD initiatives will make a significant difference to 
the local micro-climate.  The primary consumer concerns appear to be affordability, achievability, 
and alternatives.  There is a factor of unintended consequences.  The escalating electricity and 
gas rates in a monopoly business environment creates a paradox of energy conservation versus 
energy consumption.  The expectation is whatever is determined will be voted upon as it affects 
existing structures/property, unavoidable impact to its owners, and consequences to its 
occupants; therefore, a highly convincing justification and well-devised plan of implementation is 
required.  Overall, a mix of the options available may have the greatest success for maximum 
effectiveness under affordable conditions.  
 
 
Review Objective:  
 
To provide an objective recommendation for an electrification and climate change abatement 
policy that may have the greatest level of success for adoption.  Progression to a goal is 
considered superior to complete abandonment.  
 
 
Background Review:  
 
This review is based upon the following information provided: 
 
"Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy - Feedback, May 12, 2025" 
"Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy - Feedback, May 14, 2025" 
"Climate Action Plan - Annual Progress Report - May 14, 2025"  
"Electrify San Mateo: Community Relations - Feedback, May 21, 2025" 
 
There are established bans for the sale of new gas water heaters (2027) and new gas furnaces 
(2029) with near-term deadlines.  If these near-term deadlines are maintained, this study may be 
an academic thought experiment too late for some affected parties.  The most significant scenario  
example is a fixed income living condition that must replace a failed gas water heater in 2027 with 
insufficient amperage for an equivalent electric water heater.  In this case, the lack of situational 
awareness and contingency planning for the impact of the ban is disturbing at the very least.  
 
 
Project Overall Review: 
 
There are five long-standing options to review in whole, in part, or in some combination: 
1. Business as usual. 
2. New reach codes for renovations. 
3. Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of BAAD Zero NOx requirements. 
4. Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings. 
5. Electric first policy for City facilities. 
 
 
981 of 1111

--- page 982 ---
2
Information/Knowledge Review:  
 
The Rincon Consultants Inc. report is the most objective information available. 
 
The community concerns were identified as (per page 3): 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
These are unquestionably valid concerns that need to be addressed:  Upfront costs of electrical 
equipment may be mitigated with consideration for flexible policies to choose electrification or 
other cost-effective strategies.  On-bill or operational costs may be mitigated by cooperation with 
Peninsula Clean Energy.  Installation and electrical infrastructure implementation challenges may 
be mitigated by flexible compliance paths.  Grid reliability and resilience is assuming and planning 
for a 27% increase in demand by 2035. 
 
The May 14, 2025, "Electrify San Mateo: Existing Building Electrification Pathways Analysis, 
Policy Analysis (pages 7 through 23) was vague and had some logic inconsistencies, so it was 
informative to some extent for background purposes.  However, there is an apparent difference of 
how a commercial building is treated compared to a residential property.  The Phased approach 
for commercial buildings with much more consideration taken versus a residential structure that is 
faced with the gas ban deadlines is disturbingly different and obvious preferential treatment.  
 
The May 14, 2025, "Summary of Community Engagement for Electrify San Mateo" was far more 
useful to review.             
 
Affordability: 
 
The following surveys were interesting, but may lead to wrong conclusions.  There should be an 
awareness of the conditions "house poor" or "rent poor" that is not taken into account. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
982 of 1111

--- page 983 ---
3
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
What would be more useful is the disposable funds available, which would be free discretionary 
cash per month and discretionary reserve funds.  The most troubling question is how large is the 
population in the City of San Mateo that can endure a significant financial impact aggravated by 
escalating energy costs, which have no foreseeable reason to significantly decline in part or in 
total due to the monopoly utility profit structure.   
 
The following are some items have not been addressed to determine what costs may be directly 
or indirectly incurred and to prevent predatory pricing for some items: 
1.  Increased property tax and/or local tax to support city electrification improvements? 
2.  Increased utility rate charge for a higher amperage grid infrastructure to support the City? 
3.  Fixed or capped charge schedule for utility-provided services? 
4.  Fixed or capped charge schedule for City fees? 
5.  Fixed or capped charge schedule for electrical amperage service upgrades?  
6.  Are costs and rebates dependent upon the whims of the state government? 
 
Without all this information, it is very difficult to determine if there is sufficient information for 
property owners to make financially-sound decisions.  The state legislative consideration of AB 
942 for solar is a significant blow to trust in a state government agreement. 
 
The highly volatile economic and product availability conditions that may persist for a number of 
years is a major factor in consideration as well as the sense of declining support nationally and 
the troubled state financial conditions.  The current observation is a negative environment of 
uncertain employment, higher inflation, and unfavorable outlook for climate change support. 
 
 
Achievability:    
 
There are practical considerations that have not been clearly addressed.  Some are vague and 
others have no sound explanation. 
 
For grid reliability, complete dependence on electricity for life-supporting space heating, and 
heated water is highly suspect.  An event similar to the February 2021 Great Texas Freeze must 
be avoided, especially for the at-risk population.  No detailed study has been presented as to how 
the 27% electricity increase may be sufficient.  It is logically hard to believe the two highest 
energy demand devices in a residence can be compensated by a 27% energy increase, 
especially during the winter months.  If out-of-state and/or in-state gas-fired generators are used 
983 of 1111

--- page 984 ---
4
to supplement the 27%, then this makes sense, but does create a hypocritical compromise.  Solar 
with a battery is less attractive due to the declining return on investment unless there is unreliable 
grid power, and is a financially presumptuous/arrogant comment.  A low NOx gas water heater 
and low NOx gas space heating may be a more valid alternative for those resourceful to use a 
backup battery/portable power station during a grid power loss. 
 
For supply issues, equipment, contractor, and city services availability are concerns.  The cost 
and availability of electrical hardware may be uncertain to schedule work with the current trade 
turmoil.  If a utility transformer is need for the higher amperage demand, this creates greater 
uncertainty.  Contractor availability to meet a particular schedule is an uncertainty by experience.  
Timely City and utility services are a further complication as it is highly uncertain if more support 
staff will be hired.  Equipment to provide alternative electrical energy while grid electricity is 
disconnected may be problematic as well as the negative impact to the resident occupants. 
 
Alternatives: 
 
There are various alternatives of various effectiveness compared to full electrification with positive 
aspects.  These are largely to favor renters to reduce incremental rent increases and the 
low/fixed income with restricted funding.   
1. Low NOx gas water heater and gas space heater. 
2. New or higher-effective insulation. 
3. Higher efficiency windows. 
4. Higher efficiency appliances. 
5. High capacity air exhaust (gas and by-products when cooking).   
 
There is an energy conservation versus energy consumption paradox in terms of energy rate 
escalation in a monopoly environment.  Ironically, it may be better to consume more energy for 
the utility to gain greater revenue and achieve the desired profitability at the existing rate.  Energy 
conservation may drive up rates to meet profitability targets.  Therefore, with other factors beyond 
consideration, these may not necessarily keep energy rates at a reasonable/affordable level.  In 
addition, what an affordable utility rate is has not been defined or even asked. 
 
Community Feedback: 
 
The following can be classified based upon some implied assumptions from the community 
feedback: 
1. Implemented electrification due to available assets. 
2. Implemented electrification with dissatisfaction concerns. 
3. Concern for one-time and on-going costs. 
4. Concern for increased rental/leasing costs to tenants. 
 
There does not appear to be definitive information to address the concerns. 
 
Unintended Consequences:  
 
These are "what if" scenarios/conditions that should be in consideration to mitigate risk as a few 
examples of consequences. 
 
For the property owner of an aging multi-family building, it may be more financially attractive to do 
a teardown and build a new structure under reach codes for renovations.  For example, it may 
have more a more efficient electrical space heating design, more electrical outlets, better 
insulation, energy-efficient windows, soundproofing, improved security, lower maintenance, and 
much more onsite parking for overcrowded streets.  Therefore, this would change the affordability 
of the residences and reduce the amount of available housing for an extended period of time, but 
much more attractive option for tenants. 
 
984 of 1111

--- page 985 ---
5
A developer/builder may avoid the City of San Mateo to repurpose an existing structure to meet a 
specified target market price, especially for affordable housing or affordable leased space for 
businesses under reach codes for renovations.  It may be more profitable for the target market 
price to build to lesser codes elsewhere.  Therefore, this may result in less affordable housing or 
business space.    
 
There may be a question of trust in general due to the declining benefit of solar and the pending, 
solar contract-breaking AB 942.  It now appears residential solar is a property owner-financed 
energy generating infrastructure expense and a solar battery that may be seen as a fire hazard. 
An electrification-accelerating set of reach codes appears highly suspicious, especially if the 
Federal and/or State initiative goals may be relaxed due to economic or political conditions.  The 
property owners may once again be spending funds under false expectations and those funds 
should have been held in reserve to pay for well-established, escalating energy costs in a 
monopoly environment.  Therefore, a convincing commitment to the initiatives is required. 
 
 
Recommended Policy: 
 
The following is the recommend policy for the greatest level of success to achieve the various 
objectives based upon the information provided: 
 
1. Electric first policy for City facilities:  This must be done or this would be complete hypocrisy.  
    The expectation certain structures may be excluded for historical reasons.  This would 
    establish the base impact to the city electrical grid and any infrastructure costs resulting in  
    utility rate and/or local tax increases.  No further discussion is required. 
 
2. Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings:  There is a proposed, relaxed 
    implementation phased plan.  At-risk conditions must have back-up contingency system.  A low 
    interest rate loan for full electrical would be an added incentive.  With the cost of the renovation 
    being a depreciated expense and the low interest rate loan, these should be sufficient to have  
    a high adoption rate.  This should establish if there is an impact to the city electrical grid and  
    any infrastructure costs resulting in utility rate and/or local tax increases.     
 
3. Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of BAAD Zero NOx requirements:  
    Completely invalid for consideration.  There is insufficient information for a consumer to make 
    an informed decision nor sufficient time to implement the requirement under certain conditions.     
 
4. New reach codes for renovations:  Full electrification-ready condition could be required for a 
    property in a vacant condition (between property owners) or major renovations in a vacant 
    condition.  Full electrification conversion is at the discretion of the property owner, especially if 
    there is no plan to change existing appliances.  Full electrification should not significantly 
    impact housing affordability for specific housing markets.  Low interest loans for the full  
    electrification expenses should suppress an escalating the cost increase and maintain 
    affordability.  The City would need to be monitor if the full electrification is used for the purpose 
    of forced evictions. This should establish if there is an additional impact to the city electrical 
    grid and any infrastructure costs resulting in utility rate and/or local tax increases.       
 
5. Business as usual:  Full electrification and BAAD Zero NOx requirements are waived/exempt 
    for rental properties, fix income residents, and low income residents.  This may assure no 
    significant increase to rental rates or unnecessary hardship to the low or fixed income.  The 
    other listed alternative changes are at the discretion and encouraged for maximum benefit. 
    This is similar and equal allowances to the relaxed conditions allowed for commercial buildings.  
    Affected parties may still may face the collateral cost impact due to the accumulated impact 
    from the other three policy conditions.  Voluntarily adopting full electrification is highly 
    encouraged as was for adopting solar.  
 
985 of 1111

--- page 986 ---
6
Conclusions: 
 
There is a fundamental problem for a lack of information as to how the various degrees of 
mitigating actions affects the local micro-climate outcome.  It is a cost versus benefit decision or, 
in this case for some, reducing the quality of life sacrifice for a indeterminate outcome.  The 
actual versus perceived financial status of the population is problematic for expectations to 
implement initiatives.  The magnitude of affordable housing needed should be a sanity check.  
 
There appears to be a lack of a systemic/comprehensive reference for what changes may be 
necessary for full electrification and/or electrification-ready.  A cost of electrification calculator with 
a menu of itemized estimated costs and estimated time-to-completion would be highly useful.  
This should be useful for an informed decision and/or approval.  The provided recommended 
policy is largely based upon current economic and policy conditions which are very volatile and 
may be prohibitive to do anything of significance.  The outcomes of past City initiatives and the 
fluid federal/state/local policy conditions may influence acceptance.  
 
The BAAD Zero NOx requirements ban for gas water heaters and gas furnaces appears 
impractical and a blow to initiative management creditability, and/or will create predictable 
collateral damage.  The gas devices can provide stand-by, systemic redundancy as a 
contingency.   
 
The full electrification has good intentions, but there is a lack of information as well as 
contingency planning that may endanger the safety of a portion of the population.  Predictably, 
failure to maintain benefits of adoption for a mitigating action will have negative consequences for 
any initiatives in the future or sustaining the existing ones. 
 
 
Alan Ouye 
May 27, 2025

Dear Chair Narita, Vice Chair Michael, and Andrea Chow
We thank you for the opportunity to provide this letter of support, and for your
continued climate leadership. As you know, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate
Change has determined that if we continue to emit greenhouse gas emissions at the
current rate, climate change will become irreversible in five years. The time to take
strong climate action is now, and, in light of the federal government’s inaction on
climate, it’s more important than ever that cities like San Mateo demonstrate strong
climate leadership. 
Item 1 under New Business on the May 14 agenda will go a long way towards
implementation of the following City Council objective which has already received
strong SIC backing:
“Establish policies to decarbonize existing buildings and infrastructure,
and eliminate methane gas use by 2030.”
We respectfully request that the SIC support all five objectives of the Sustainable
Building Strategy: 
Keep in place the very successful reach codes for new construction
Extend reach codes to retrofits of existing buildings
Support accelerated elimination of Nitrous Oxides, specifically the Air District 
requirements to replace failed gas water heaters and gas furnaces
Build upon an established program of building performance standards
Support electrification of City buildings
We want to take this opportunity to emphasize all that has been done already:
Significant City support for decarbonization – The City of San Mateo hired a 
consultant and provided valuable City staff time, to prepare a decarbonization 
study for adoption over the next few months
989 of 1111

--- page 990 ---
Even More City support for decarbonization – The City of San Mateo has 
created a valuable website: “Electrify San Mateo”
Concierge service for Electrification – Peninsula Clean Energy has created a 
“Concierge Service” for electric vehicles and electric home upgrades, available 
to everyone
Equity for renters – San Mateo community-based organizations are directly 
involved in helping renters within the City of San Mateo 
More Equity for renters – PCE has active rebate programs focused on renters.
Equity for homeowners – Free electric appliances are now provided by PCE 
for income qualified homeowners
Clean energy at rates lower than PG&E – Peninsula Clean Energy has 
provided $22.8 million in savings for residents and businesses within the City of 
San Mateo
Reduced GHG – San Mateo residents and businesses have signed on to 
electricity from PCE, which has reduced emissions from electricity by 98% in 
just 10 years. 
Significant rebates are available to all for EVs, EBikes, water heaters, 
heating/cooling and other components.
Many local community-based organizations are aligned in this important effort. For
example, we have learned that along with San Mateo Climate Action Team and 350
Silicon Valley, so many other organizations are working towards successful
implementation of sustainable building strategies: One San Mateo, El Concilio,
Climate Resilient Communities, Sustainable San Mateo County, Indivisible San
Mateo, Peninsula Interfaith Climate Action, Sierra Club, Citizens Climate Lobby, and
Physicians for Social Responsibility, just to name a few.

Re: Decarbonization Study agenda item
 
Across the next days, all of you will be hearing from the  Electrify San Mateo project team about the
findings from the Decarbonization Study. Unfortunately, I am not able to attend the commission
meetings.  Nevertheless, I strongly support all five of the decarbonization proposals contained within
the decarbonization report.  The five options under consideration are shown below.   
 
As a home owner, and resident of San Mateo City, I am three quarters the way through the process
of electrifying my whole home. I am changing the appliance energy source from natural gas to all
electric versions. I am doing this across a span of four years to maximize the federal tax credits.  So I
know in-depth about all of the implementation challenges. Including specific details associated with
my house, and listening to home owners at various climate related events about their experiences.
There are many points of contention. People make poor choices. Contractors try to maximize their
revenue instead of helping their clients.  And the rebate application processes are adding
burdensome overhead in Time-Of-Use reporting requirements.  Only by providing information
resources, and best practices,  to be shared with citizens can the best implementations with a
reduction in non-traditional, installation problems be achieved.  
 
Time is of the essence to begin implementation of the methods described in the report. Day -- by –
Day we collectively are putting more and more greenhouse gases into the atmosphere. The climate
disasters that scroll across our TV screens from stronger hurricanes, stronger tornadoes, large
temperature swings, wildfires and more will continue to worsen across the next decades.  Acting
today, by taking positive steps to reduce San Mateo City greenhouse gas emissions, will be a positive
step towards reducing the future climate hazards, rebuilding costs, and the health and safety of all
San Mateo residents.  Act now to get the electrification converstion process started as soon as
possible.
 
Regards
Dr. Gary Trott
Homeowner

Subject: Electrify San Mateo: Three Upcoming Community Meetings
 
Hello,
 
Switching from natural gas to carbon-free electric equipment in our homes and buildings is
critical to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in our community. Gas appliances
release air pollutants and studies have shown that exposure to these pollutants pose
significant health risks. In addition to advancing our climate goals, electrification eliminates
these risks and can provide a safer and healthier environment. The Electrify San Mateo
project team will present existing building electrification policy pathways at three upcoming
City Commission meetings:
May 12: Senior Advisory Commission
May 14: Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission
May 21: Community Relations Commission
 
We invite you to share your feedback and shape the Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable
Building Strategy. Community feedback will be shared with City Council tentatively
scheduled for June 2025. Please see the Public Meeting Portal for meeting agenda and
information posted roughly one week before the Commission meetings.

Subject: 
Electrify San Mateo: Existing Building Electrification Policy Pathways Analysis
In the City of San Mateo (City), the residential and commercial built environment make up 38 percent of 
greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. To achieve emissions reduction targets consistent with state goals and 
the City’s Climate Action Plan, the City must decarbonize its residential and commercial buildings. This is 
strengthened by the 2022 City Council priority to “Establish policies to decarbonize existing buildings and 
infrastructure and eliminate methane gas use by 2030.” The decarbonization or electrification of buildings 
refers to the transition from gas equipment to carbon-free electric equipment for space and water 
heating, clothes drying, cooking and more. Decarbonization can also focus on increasing energy efficiency 
and the use of renewable energy. In San Mateo, Peninsula Clean Energy (PCE) provides electricity that is 
50% renewable and virtually 100% carbon free and is committed to delivering 100% renewable and carbon 
free electricity by 2025. The City has already passed a New Construction Reach Code aimed at 
decarbonizing new buildings, however, electrifying existing buildings offers the largest opportunity and 
most cost-effective strategy for GHG emission reductions. 
In May 2023, the City, with the help of Rincon Consultants Inc. (Rincon), began developing the Building 
for the Future: City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy project as a part of the larger Electrify San 
Mateo initiative. The goal of this project is to develop a long-term strategy document to electrify San 
Mateo’s existing building stock, including policies and actions for both residential and nonresidential 
buildings. This report provides Rincon’s final policy findings which are based on the cumulative technical 
analysis and community feedback completed to date. 
Project Timeline and Overview 
The City of San Mateo and Rincon began the initial research for this project in the Summer of 2023. The 
initial analysis included building segmentation analysis and an electrification cost study. Rincon also 
conducted research on the current best practices and a wide range of feasible policy options being 
deployed by other cities around the country. The project timeline is shown below in Figure 1.
1005 of 1111

--- page 1006 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
2
Figure 1
Project Timeline to Date
Community Outreach (Phase I)
During the first phase of community outreach, the City aimed to understand the goals and concerns of 
the community as they relate to San Mateo’s buildings and decarbonization. Through the initial round of 
engagement, the City identified affordability, health and safety, resilience, and climate as the primary 
goals of the community. The largest concerns related to building electrification were the potential for 
higher costs, increased energy bills, and a difficult installation process. Based on this feedback and 
direction from City Council, the initial policy scan list was narrowed down to five potential policy 
directions. 
Policy Pathways 
The results from the Phase 1 of outreach helped narrow the initial policy scan into a list of five policy 
pathways, which were presented to City Council in January of 2024. These policies were chosen based on 
the GHG reductions potential as well as cost-effectiveness. The five policy pathways include 
1.
Continue adopted reach codes and focus on outreach and incentives (Business as Usual)
2.
Explore and adopt new reach codes to encourage and require electrification 
3.
Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of the Bay Area Air District’s (BAAD)1 Zero NOx 
requirements 
4.
Establish Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings 
5.
Adopt an electric-first policy for City facilities 
1 The Bay Area Air Quality Management District (BAAQMD) changed their name to the Bay Area Air District (BAAD) in 2025. 
1006 of 1111

--- page 1007 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
3
City Council provided feedback which included direction for City staff to continue exploring the feasibility, 
cost, and community support for each of the five options.
Community Outreach (Phase II)
In the second phase of community outreach (see Engagement Summary Memo for detailed results), the 
City aimed to get specific feedback about the five policy pathways. Phase II outreach events included both 
in-person and virtual workshops, presentations to the Senior Advisory Commission, Sustainability and 
Infrastructure Commission, and Community Relations Commission, as well as a second online feedback 
survey. Feedback received from Phase II of outreach is incorporated into an analysis of each policy 
pathway in the following sections. 
Addressing Community Concerns
Throughout the community engagement process, there have been polarized responses to building 
electrification. While most constituents care about addressing the Climate Crisis and agree that the City 
should pursue existing building electrification in some form, many residents also expressed concerns. The 
primary concerns identified during Phase I and Phase II engagement included: 
•
Upfront costs of electric equipment 
•
On-bill or operational costs of electric equipment
•
Installation and electrical infrastructure challenges
•
Grid reliability and resilience
•
Lack of education surrounding electrification (addressed in Policy Option 1)
Each of the pathways balance the City’s climate goals and the roughly half of respondents who strongly 
support building electrification with the concerns voiced during the engagement process in different ways. 
Each primary community concern is addressed below and further covered in the policy summaries. 
Upfront Costs of Electrical Equipment
For many building owners, making the switch from natural gas to electric appliances results in higher 
upfront costs without additional incentives or rebates. While rebates can cover up to 100% of these costs, 
rebates are not always available. However, not installing electric equipment when it is most cost effective 
can have significant cost impacts in the future. Therefore, the City has identified flexible policies that allow 
building owners to choose electrification or other cost-effective strategies. Each policy below provides 
additional details of the upfront costs and savings. 
On-Bill or Operational Costs
Ongoing operational costs (how much our monthly energy bills are) were another concern regarding 
building electrification. However, recent studies by Peninsula Clean Energy show that the cost savings 
from building electrification are increasing as natural gas rate increases outpace electricity rates. Average 
savings (when using electric rates like e-elec) were $29 per household per month after electrifying both 
water and space heaters. These savings are demonstrated in Figure 3. 
1007 of 1111

--- page 1008 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
4
Figure 2
Average Monthly Bill Savings from Electrification
Installation and Electrical Infrastructure Challenges
While most electric appliance upgrades are relatively simple, some buildings present difficult edge cases. 
Some buildings may not have sufficient space or electrical infrastructure to easily upgrade. Upgrading 
electric panels and service lines can take months to complete. While all-building owners are encouraged 
to make a plan for electrification over time, many of the policies avoid these challenges by providing 
flexible paths to compliance or focusing on specific appliances which do not have these challenges. For 
example, the flex-path retrofit policy provides many options including insulation and other cost-effective 
efficiency upgrades at the time of major renovation. The AC to heat pump policy avoids these challenges 
because the heat pump upgrade uses the same electrical circuit as an AC, eliminating the need for 
upgrades. Grid Reliability and Resilience
Grid Reliability and Resilience
Grid reliability and resilience concerns arose due to the perception that increased electrical use would put 
strain on the grid and that electricity was less reliable than gas. However, it has been nearly 20 years since 
1008 of 1111

--- page 1009 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
5
California had a power outage due to lack of electricity.3 Furthermore, the utilities, the CPUC, and the CEC, 
are all planning for increased electricity usage over time. The CEC now assumes a 27% increase in peak 
demand by 2035 due to electrification of buildings and vehicles and is expanding the grid to meet that 
demand. Furthermore, neither gas water heaters nor most furnaces work without electricity, meaning gas 
appliances are no more resilient to power outages than their electric upgrades. Therefore, the policies 
identified below focus on these appliances.
Policy Findings Summary
To analyze the feasibility and benefits of each policy pathway, Rincon provided a description of each 
pathway, pro’s and con’s, precedent legislation in other cities and the state, GHG reductions potential, 
community feedback, cost-effectiveness, and equity considerations. Based on the detailed analysis in the 
Policy Analysis section, the following summary highlights the major takeaways of the five policy pathways. 
1.
Business as usual: The City could continue with adopting reach codes for new construction and expand 
outreach, education, and incentives. In the past, the City adopted local building code amendments to 
require more sustainable new construction than the State code. To continue to surpass State code, 
the City could consider other new construction reach codes including an energy performance or Zero 
NOx reach code. This approach has a limited impact because new construction accounts for a fraction 
of San Mateo’s development. However, this approach is already in place, cost saving up front and over 
time, and received community support based on our engagement efforts. There are no policy-related 
additional costs to building owners unless they voluntarily decide to electrify. A Zero NOx new 
construction approach would save an estimated 1,770 MT CO2e per year by 2030 while a performance 
approach would save 20 MT CO2e per year by 2030. 
2.
Reach codes for renovations: This approach would include the evaluation of additional reach codes to 
encourage and/or require electrification during renovations or at time of replacement for select 
appliances (air conditioners). New reach codes would vary depending on the updated State codes. 
One example of a reach code during renovations is requiring the installation of a two-way heat pump 
when an air conditioner (AC) is installed.  A Two-way AC time of replacement reach code could help 
residents save money by installing a single heat pump, rather than an AC and furnace, ahead of 
upcoming Bay Area Air District (BAAD) regulation which essentially mandates electric furnaces starting 
in 2029. The incremental cost of installing a heat pump instead of a traditional AC is the same (from 
$1,000 savings to $1,000 cost) and is covered by a $1,500 PCE rebate. Installing a heat pump today is 
alignment with future Air District regulations and thus could also save a building owner money when 
their furnace needs replacement. Reach codes during renovations also help encourage electrification 
at an opportune time when a property owner is already considering home improvements. Another 
policy example is a Flex Path reach code which would require additional energy efficiency or 
electrification actions at time of renovation or replacement of certain appliances. The upfront costs 
of electrification improvements can vary depending on the project and unique circumstances of your 
home. These policies target the most cost-effective time to electrify and provide a variety of pathways 
for compliance. Cost-effectiveness studies must be completed to validate any adopted reach codes. 
Past studies have shown utility savings over time make electric equipment upgrades cost-effective 
compared to gas equipment replacements. Additionally, the Flex Path reach code allows residents to 
3 https://www.baaqmd.gov/~/media/files/community-health/building-appliance-implementation/grid-impacts-final-
pdf.pdf?rev=14a3ea4d46704d19a48cf03e3984c90e&sc_lang=en 
1009 of 1111

--- page 1010 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
6
find the most cost-effective retrofit for their unique situation. Two-Way AC reach code would reduce 
emissions by 2,200 MT CO2e per year by 2030, while the Flex Path reach code would reduce emissions 
by approximately 730 MT CO2e per year by 2030.
3.
Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of BAAD Zero NOx requirements: BAAD adopted 
zero nitrogen oxides (NOx) standards that will prohibit the sale and installation of gas water heaters 
starting in 2027 and gas furnaces in 2029 in the nine-county Bay Area. A local ordinance ahead of 
these milestones would require the replacement of gas appliances with zero NOx versions when they 
fail or are voluntarily upgraded. This policy would have the most significant impact on greenhouse gas 
reductions with the ability to reduce emissions an average of 9,700 MT CO2e per year by 2030. 
However, this policy could incur large installation costs for residents in some cases which was the 
number one concern documented during both phases of community engagement. Due to the 
polarized community feedback, with some constituents being unsupportive of mandates that increase 
costs, it may be difficult to garner public buy in for a Zero NOx Equipment Requirement policy. This 
policy prioritizes GHG emissions reductions but does not address the community’s major concerns 
over costs and difficult installations. 
4.
Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings: This pathway would establish a new set of 
policy standards for commercial buildings aimed at increasing energy efficiency, and eventually, 
electrifying commercial buildings as a phased approach. This is a well-established policy mechanism 
that has been adopted by many local governments across the country and builds off of AB 802 
benchmarking requirements mandated by the state for buildings larger than 50,000 SF. This approach 
can be customizable and lead to incremental reductions in energy use but not necessarily 
electrification. The flexibility of this policy and phased approach allows for building owners to plan for 
appliance replacement and decarbonize buildings in the most efficient way for each unique situation. 
Commercial building space makes up over 39 million square feet within the City and is a significant 
contributor to GHG emissions. However, due to the phased approach, this policy would likely not lead 
to significant GHG reductions before 2030. However, it could have significant reductions before 2045 
as the thresholds are calculated and implemented. The development and operation of such a local 
program would necessitate significant staff time, so choosing a threshold that maximizes square 
footage covered while minimizing the number of building owners affected is crucial. Although this 
policy received majority community support, equity for renters is also a significant concern and 
guardrails would need to be set in place to avoid exacerbating the housing crisis especially for 
multifamily properties. 
5.
Electric first policy for City facilities: This policy establishes a mechanism for local government 
renovations to go electric first. The policy ensures financial and feasibility data is being collected for 
municipal facilities that will allow the City to compare costs to electrify versus replacing with gas 
equipment. This policy has a limited reach as it only applies to municipal buildings and GHG reductions 
potential would depend on the rate of equipment failure and availability of replacements. However, 
it demonstrates City leadership on hard-to-electrify existing buildings that mirror the commercial 
sector. Several other cities have adopted similar municipal electrification policies. Residents were 
largely in favor of this policy as it would not incur any costs to the community. The City’s municipal 
buildings are estimated to generate approximately 586 MT CO2e per year of natural gas emissions, 
this policy was estimated to be able to reduce this by 25% by 2030 for an estimated savings of 146 
MT CO2e.
