--- page 1011 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
7
Policy Analysis
Policy Option 1: Continue adopted reach codes and focus on outreach and 
incentives (Business as Usual)
Description 
Policy Option 1 continues to implement the City’s adopted reach codes and identifies opportunities to 
incentivize the electrification process using education, outreach, and connecting the community to 
rebates and technical support. The City’s adopted reach code requires energy compliance margins for new 
buildings will be effective through December 31, 2025, the end of the code cycle.  As part of this policy 
option, the City would investigate options around continuing the new building electrification reach code 
through either an updated performance standard which complies with the 2025 building code, or a zero 
NOx standard for new buildings. 
This option would also use staff time to conduct additional engagement and connect homeowners to 
incentives and technical information regarding building electrification. It would also promote existing 
programming and incentives run by partners (e.g. PCE) to assist in a voluntary shift towards electrification 
while preparing the community to electrify space and water heating end uses in advance of upcoming 
BAAD (previously BAAQMD) Zero NOx requirements. For example, this option could include an education 
campaign to encourage homeowners to prewire for water heaters and furnaces, so they are ready to 
make the switch once their appliance burns out. Another example incentive would be waiving permit costs 
for electric equipment upgrades. 
Policy Pros
•
Policy already in place/ has received community support 
•
No policy-related additional costs to building owners unless they voluntarily opt-in 
•
Allows community to prepare for upcoming BAAD requirements through City-led outreach and 
education 
•
Allows community to prepare for upcoming requirements through electric-readiness requirements 
Policy Cons
•
Limited impact and reach: The adopted reach codes do not affect the  existing building stock 
•
Relies on engagement to reach community members which can be difficult
•
Outreach and education programs on their own are extremely variable in their effectiveness. 
Investment of staff time and city resources into outreach may not achieve substantial shifts in 
behavior that will be required to decarbonize buildings, even with major outreach campaigns. 
•
Education and outreach campaigns can be very time/resource intensive with minimal programmatic 
results and unlikely to result in widespread behavior changes above baseline 
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential 
New construction reach codes have varying GHG reduction potential. For example, a new construction 
zero NOx requirement could save up to 1,770 MT CO2e per year by 2030 if all new buildings were built 
1011 of 1111

--- page 1012 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
8
without natural gas. An energy performance reach code for new construction would lead to approximately 
20 MT CO2e GHG reductions due to the stringency of the 2025 building code, however, precise reductions 
potential is unknown because the flexible nature of the pathway does not always guarantee 
electrification. 
As mentioned above, education and outreach and sharing incentives with residents is an important tool 
for the successful implementation of electrification policy but are not substantial enough to gauge GHG 
emissions on their own. It is difficult to predict what impact these voluntary programs would have on 
overall electrification rates, however, they are expected to be lower than the policy requirements 
identified in the other policy pathways. 
Community Feedback 
Across engagement events, this approach received broad community support. In the community survey, 
responses were 50% of respondents supporting this policy option, 11% neutral, and 38% do not support. 
At the in-person community event, many community members expressed that the City has not done 
enough in terms of electrification policy beyond new construction and that a “business as usual” approach 
is not sufficient to meet the City’s climate goals. Those opposed to this approach are opposed to any local 
building electrification policy and recommend reliance on regional and state regulations. The Senior 
Advisory Commission, Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, and Community Relations 
Commission were supportive of this policy pathway.
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations 
This policy approach would continue to incentivize all-electric new construction and direct the City to 
expand outreach efforts with the community to increase voluntary existing building electrification. New 
Construction reach codes have been shown to save money. Research from the New Buildings Institute 
(NBI) highlights that all-electric homes save between $7,500 and $8,200 in construction costs compared 
to mixed-fuel homes.5 These cost savings are primarily due to avoiding installing fees for natural gas 
infrastructure like gas meters and pipelines. These homes also save money over time especially when 
paired with heat pumps and solar. Outreach, education, and sharing incentives incur no cost to community 
members and can help connect people to resources that will lessen costs for electrification. The City will 
need to provide adequate staff time to support building electrification engagement and other programs 
like peer-to-peer home tours for this approach to be effective. These engagement activities, when paired 
with other strategies listed below, can help the community feel more comfortable and informed on the 
benefits of electrification. 
Policy Findings
The adoption of reach codes while expanding education, outreach, and incentives to promote building 
electrification have been critical pathways for the City of San Mateo to support decarbonization to date. 
This approach is supportive of the other policy options because expanding education and engagement 
can potentially increase the number of cost-effective voluntary projects and expand community 
understanding of new technologies and regulations (like the upcoming Air District’s Zero NOx rules).  The 
community expressed the need education campaigns like helping to facilitate peer-to-peer home tours 
5 NBI. 2022. New Study on Electrification Costs Shows Benefits to Building Owners and Society. https://newbuildings.org/new-study-on-
electrification-costs-shows-benefits-to-building-owners-and-society/
1012 of 1111

--- page 1013 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
9
and partnering with larger County initiatives like PCE’s helpdesk to ensure residents are receiving the 
guidance they need on their electrification journey. While this policy offers several benefits, it is important 
to acknowledge that this approach alone will not be sufficient to meet the City's climate goals.  . 
Policy Option 2: Reach Codes for Renovations 
Description
Option 2 would include the evaluation of additional reach codes to encourage and/or require 
electrification. Reach codes are typically evaluated on a three-year cycle in alignment with the new 
Building Code. New reach codes options would vary depending on the updated Energy Code and Green 
Building Code. The City’s adopted reach codes apply to residential remodels. The City could consider a 
variety of reach codes that apply to different building types and/or equipment. The City focused on two 
priority existing building reach codes include a Two-Way Air Conditioning (AC) reach code or a Flexible 
Path reach code. These policies could be adopted individually or both adopted to complement each other. 
Other reach codes which were not covered with the community but may be important in the future are 
time of sale requirements similar to the policy adopted by the City of Berkeley.7
Policy Option 2a: Two-Way Air Conditioning Reach Code 
The Two-Way AC reach code would require all new air conditioning units to provide both heating and 
cooling through the installation of a two-way heat pump. A two-way heat pump is a versatile HVAC system 
that can both heat and cool a building by reversing the flow of refrigerant within the system. In heating 
mode, it extracts heat from an external source, such as outdoor air or the ground, and transfers it indoors. 
In cooling mode, it reverses the process, removing heat from inside the building and releasing it outside. 
This dual functionality eliminates the need for separate heating and cooling systems which can save 
building owners money in the long term. Two-way heat pumps run completely off electricity and heat and 
cool spaces with high efficiency, making them a great way to reduce emissions coming from homes. 
Policy Option 2b: Flex Path Reach Code 
Under the flex path policies, the City could adopt one or more policies that require additional energy 
efficiency or electrification actions at time of renovation or replacement of certain appliances. One 
example would be an energy efficiency checklist in which renovation projects over $75,000 must include 
two cost effective energy efficient improvements in their project. The flex path policies do not ban gas 
and provide flexible pathways for building owners to meet the decarbonization requirements. 
Policy Pros: 
•
The City has experience exploring, adopting and implementing reach codes
•
Two-Way AC is a cost-efficient and simple solution
•
Flex path offers variety of compliance pathways to reduce emissions and building owners could find 
the most cost-efficient pathways for their homes 
7 https://berkeleyca.gov/sites/default/files/documents/2025-04-15-Amendments-to-the-BESO.pdf  
1013 of 1111

--- page 1014 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
10
Policy Cons:
•
Community and builder resistance to new regulations.
•
Further analysis needed, dependent on reach code adopted.
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers
In May 2023, the Bay Area Air Quality Management District, now the Bay Area Air District (BAAD) adopted 
zero nitrogen oxides (NOx) standards that will prohibit the sale of gas water heaters starting in 2027 and 
gas furnaces in 2029 in the nine-county Bay Area. These standards are the most ambitious in the country 
and outpace the state’s goal of banning the sale of new gas furnaces and water heaters throughout the 
state starting in 2030. These policy efforts drive California closer to the ambitious climate goal of carbon 
neutrality by 2045. Reach codes during renovations present an  opportunity to ensure that the City’s 
residential building stock is prepared for this upcoming mandate. 
Two-Way AC policies are relatively new, so no jurisdiction has yet to officially adopt this reach code, but 
many are expected to in the 2025 building cycle. The “Flex Path” reach code style is more common with 
several jurisdictions who have adopted this policy listed below: 
•
City of San Rafael (Adopted 2024): San Rafael has adopted a "Flex Path" for single family remodels 
over 500 square feet. This policy requires homeowners to choose from a menu of energy-saving 
measures, such as insulation upgrades, HVAC improvements, or solar installations, to meet a target 
score based on their home's vintage. The program is designed to encourage energy efficiency and 
electrification while allowing homeowners to select measures that best suit their needs and budget.
•
City of San Luis Obispo (Adopted 2024): Starting January 1, 2025, single family major additions or 
alterations must install a set of energy efficiency measures to achieve a target score from a weighted 
menu of measures. Projects that include a new electrical panel and service upgrade to 200A shall 
include electric readiness components.
•
Town of Corte Madera (Adopted 2024): Single family additions or alterations greater than 750 square 
feet of floor area shall install a set of measures to achieve a target score from a weighted menu of 
measures; which varies based on building vintage. Projects that include a laundry room or kitchen 
remodel, or an electrical service panel upgrade, must also install electric readiness measures.
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential 
The Two-Way AC reach code would reduce emissions by 2,200 MT CO2e per year by 2030. Two-way heat 
pumps tend to be two to four times more efficient than both one-way air conditioners and traditional 
heating sources, leading to large potential GHG reductions.9 While the Flex Path reach code would reduce 
emissions by approximately 730 MT CO2e per year by 2030. The Flex Path reductions are lower since they 
only capture remodels over a certain size which occur at a lower rate than other appliance replacements.  
Community Feedback 
Across community engagement events, constituents were supportive of this policy approach. Community 
members recognized the importance of electrifying during planned renovation projects. In the community 
9 Canary Media. A new bill could speed up American electrification by 20 years. https://www.canarymedia.com/articles/heat-pumps/a-new-bill-
could-speed-up-american-electrification-by-20-years
1014 of 1111

--- page 1015 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
11
survey, responses were mixed with 49% respondents supporting this policy option, 6% neutral and 45% 
do not support. Multiple constituents expressed a desire for an exception for edge cases that are 
financially or spatially infeasible. Those opposed are concerned about upfront costs or are generally 
opposed to all local building electrification policy. The Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission and 
Community Relations Commission were supportive of this policy pathway.
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations 
Although the Two-Way AC policy would mandate installation of a two-way heat pump at time of 
replacement, this retrofit is highly cost-effective as it can both cool and heat a space, effectively replacing 
both a central air conditioner and furnace within a home. This reduces the need for separate furnace 
replacements. Implementing a Two-Way AC policy is particularly advantageous given the upcoming BAAD 
Zero NOx requirements. 
If a constituent's air conditioner breaks before 2029 and is replaced with a gas HVAC system, they will 
need to purchase an electric furnace when their furnace eventually breaks after the 2029 BAAD Zero NOx 
implementation date. Conversely, adopting a Two-Way AC policy in preparation for these requirements 
would ensure that all air conditioner replacements in the next few years have two-way heating and cooling 
capabilities and comply with BAAD’s ruling. This would prevent homeowners from installing outdated gas 
appliances for heating and cooling, which could be replaced by a single heat pump. 
Additionally, the difference between a central AC and a two-way heat pump is essentially zero (from a 
$1,000 savings to a $1,000 cost) especially after the applied $1,500 rebate from Peninsula Clean Energy. 
Peninsula Clean Energy (PCE) also offers on-bill financing through the Zero Percent Loan program11 to 
cover the up-front costs of installing a heat pump, which leads to utility bill savings over time due to 
appliance efficiency. This financing model can be particularly advantageous for low-income tenants who 
might not have access to traditional financing options. It removes the need for credit checks and upfront 
payments, making energy efficiency upgrades more accessible. Additionally, on-bill financing can be an 
effective way for tenants and landlords to manage payment responsibilities for energy efficiency 
upgrades. Tenants could enjoy the benefits of lower utility costs, without being tied to a load if they move 
(because the payment is tied to the meter rather than a person) and the landlord can improve their 
building without needing to pay an upfront cost.12 
A Flex Path reach code is also cost-effective by providing a range of retrofits and upgrades that building 
owners can choose from, allowing them to pick the most cost-effective and efficient options. Based on a 
conducted cost analysis conducted by the Local Codes and Standards team, each check list item would 
need to be demonstrated to have cost savings over the lifespan of the upgrade. Figure 3 shows some of 
the cost-effective strategies for the City of San Mateo based on the 2022 Title 24 Building Code. Heat 
pumps provide the most cost-effective solutions, often paying back in 6-7 years. Furthermore, many 
homeowners may receive additional savings through incentives provided by PCE, the State (Tech rebates) 
or the Inflation Reduction Act. 
11 Peninsula Clean Energy. 2025. 0% financing & rebates. Available: https://www.peninsulacleanenergy.com/residential/financing/
12 United States Department of Energy. Issue Brief: Low-income Energy Efficiency Financing through On-Bill Tariff Programs. Available: IB L-I EE 
Financing through On-Bill Tariffs_Final_0.pdf

--- page 1016 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
12
Figure 3
Cost Effective Existing Building Upgrades (Cost Effectiveness Explorer)
Both the Two-Way AC and Flex Path policies emphasize cost-efficient solutions for both the short and long 
term, ensuring that the City maintains equitable and sustainable practices.
Policy Findings
Both a Two-Way AC policy and Flex Path policy promote cost-efficient and equitable solutions for building 
decarbonization. Implementing these policies would help the City achieve meaningful greenhouse gas 
reductions, get closer to climate goals, prepare the community for future regulations,  and support 
residents in making energy-efficient upgrades. Both of these policies fit with the community feedback 
received by pursuing climate action and decarbonization policies but being sensitive to cost impacts, the 
need for flexibility, and reliability/resilience. 
Policy Option 3: Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of BAAD’s 
Zero NOx requirements 
Description
BAAD will require zero NOx (currently the only options for Zero NOx appliances are all-electric) appliances 
for water heating by 2027 and space heating by 2029. This policy bans the sale of qualifying NOx -emitting 
appliances in the Bay Area, requiring all-electric appliances when covered gas appliances are replaced. 
Option 3 has the greatest potential to achieve GHG reductions and the goals outlined in the City’s CAP by 
moving faster than the 2027 and 2029 timelines outlined by BAAD. However, it could also result in higher 
upfront costs especially for space heating. Under this policy the City would adopt and implement a local 
version of the BAAD zero N0x threshold ahead of the current schedule to require all new space and water 
heating to have zero NOx emissions, and therefore, zero GHG emissions. 
This ‘time of replacement’ ordinance would require the replacement of gas water heaters and space 
heaters with zero NOx versions when they fail or are voluntarily upgraded. This approach maximizes the 
lifespan of the existing gas appliances and reduces the cost of compliance as the increased cost would 
only be the difference between a gas appliance and the upgraded electric appliance. Focusing on time-of-
replacement is the most cost-effective approach for the building owner. Furthermore, since these 
1016 of 1111

--- page 1017 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
13
appliances may function for dozens of years, it also is the timeliest option to replace gas appliances with 
electric-run appliances instead of locking in emissions from natural-gas appliances which could potentially 
continue to operate past the City’s target for carbon neutrality. 
Policy Pros: 
•
Community and workforce prepared for 2027 and 2029 BAAD requirements
•
On-bill savings for homes that go all-electric 
•
Largest GHG emissions reduction potential 
Policy Cons:
•
Higher upfront costs for residents for home electrification
•
Uncertainty of incentives 
•
Some homes may face electrical upgrades (panels or service lines) that increase costs and lengthen 
timelines for replacement 
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers
The BAAD requirements will be implemented in 2027 and 2029 and getting ahead of the ruling offers an 
opportunity to have the highest GHG impact. Currently, there are no jurisdictions who have adopted this 
policy for existing buildings. 
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential 
A zero NOx equipment requirement policy would have the most significant impact on greenhouse gas 
reductions with the ability to reduce emissions an average of 9,700 MT CO2e per year by 2030 assuming 
a zero NOx policy was put in place for water heating and space heating in 2026 for residential/small 
commercial appliances and 2028 for larger commercial appliances. 
Community Feedback 
Feedback on this policy was largely unfavorable. In the community survey, 44% of respondents supported 
this policy option, 5% neutral, and 48% do not support. Those opposed were concerned about getting 
ahead of the Air District’s schedule and challenges with compliance and enforcement. Those who support 
this approach emphasize that this approach has the greatest GHG reduction potential. The Community 
Relations Commission was supportive of this policy pathway.
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations 
Based on the initial cost study conducted for this project, a zero NOx policy median-cost scenario could 
increase water heating incremental costs by $3,200 and space heating incremental costs by $11,778 
before rebates. However, between state, local, and federal incentives high income households could see 
an overall cost savings for water heaters and incremental costs of $5,878 dollars for space heating. When 
also considering the avoided cost of an AC unit (once it breaks down) from installing a two-way heat pump 
that acts as a furnace and an AC unit, then the incremental costs in a median-cost scenario for space 
heating would be $4,278 before rebates and incur cost savings with rebates. Similar conclusions were 
1017 of 1111

--- page 1018 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
14
found in another cost study done for BAAD.15 However, many of the incentives originally modeled for this 
analysis have changed or have uncertain futures, especially at the federal level. 
Similarly to Policy Option 2, residents could utilize on-bill financing from PCE to cover up-front costs of 
electric water heater or space heating appliances, utilizing a zero percent interest loan and repaying that 
installation cost over time via their utility bill.11 The PCE program fosters equitable installation of electric 
equipment and allows renters to enjoy the same energy savings as homeowners. However, the availability 
of rebates and incentives is volatile which could impact the cost-effectiveness of an electric-appliance 
mandate. 
Policy Findings 
A Zero NOx Equipment Requirements policy would achieve the highest greenhouse gas emissions 
reduction , mandating electric heat pump water heaters and furnaces ahead of the BAAD requirement. 
This policy offers a significant opportunity to reduce GHG emissions and capture appliance replacements 
over the next four years before BAAD’s mandate is fully implemented. Implementing this policy could 
potentially save residents money depending on the availability of rebates on water and space heating 
appliances. However, there are cases in which this policy could result in increased upfront costs for 
homeowners, which was the biggest concern identified in Phase II of engagement. The changing rebate 
landscape also makes it difficult to understand how costs will change over time. Due to the polarized 
community feedback, with some constituents being unsupportive of mandates, it may be difficult to 
garner public buy in for a Zero NOx Equipment Requirement policy. 
Policy Option 4: Commercial Building Performance Standards 
Description 
Option 4 introduces new policy standards for commercial buildings (Building Performance Standards) 
aimed at increasing energy efficiency, and eventually, decarbonizing commercial buildings as a phased 
efficiency-focused approach. In comparison to relatively homogonous residential buildings, commercial 
buildings can be highly variable in their systems and needs. Commercial building heating and cooling 
systems are also frequently oversized, meaning replacing existing gas equipment in a like for like manner 
with electric equipment is not economical. Process loads also vary significantly across commercial building 
types. For these reasons, decarbonizing commercial buildings necessitates a flexible approach where 
building managers can first make strides towards understanding their buildings and cost-effective 
opportunities before committing to a specific approach. Typically, a BPS Program is implemented in 
phases where buildings first report energy use for several years (benchmarking), then perform a retro-
commissioning process to identify opportunities before finally reducing emissions below an established 
threshold (performance standard). 
•
Phase I: Benchmarking (Years 1-2): This initial phase establishes a building’s energy performance 
baseline by comparing its energy use intensity (EUI) to similar buildings. Covered Buildings (buildings 
and properties with buildings totaling the specified size threshold or larger) report their annual energy 
usage in Energy Star Portfolio Manager. The data collected in the benchmarking phase improves 
15 BAAD. 2024. Installation Costs for Zero-NOx Space and Water Heating Appliances. Available: 
https://www.baaqmd.gov/~/media/files/community-health/building-appliance-implementation/task1_electrificationcosts-
pdf.pdf?rev=3cb66a09f3094f94b35fa7fc90cfd4ec&sc_lang=en
1018 of 1111

--- page 1019 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
15
building manager and City understanding of building energy usage and will inform GHG-emissions 
performance standards. Benchmarking also identifies underperforming facilities and prioritizes 
improvements, with studies showing benchmarked buildings achieve average annual energy savings 
of 2.4 percent.17
•
Phase II: Retro-commissioning (Years 3-4): Retro-commissioning is a process through which a 
building’s energy consuming systems are analyzed for efficiency and proper operation. The retro-
commissioning process serves two primary purposes. The first is to uncover inefficient operations, 
increase energy efficiency, and save building owners money. The second is to identify cost-effective 
opportunities to reduce building GHG emissions through electrification and energy efficiency.
•
Phase III: Performance Standard (Year 4+): Jurisdictions set phased energy or emissions targets for 
GHG reductions over time on a square feet basis. The targets are based on data collected from the 
benchmarking phase and can be met through a variety of retrofits, such as upgrades, electrification, 
or renewable energy adoption. The most cost-efficient retrofits for each building were identified in 
the retro-commissioning phase. Standards often allow flexible compliance paths, such as achieving 
specific EUI thresholds or offsetting emissions via clean energy investments. 
Policy Pros: 
•
BPS approach addresses a high degree of variability in commercial building types by focusing on 
performance-based requirements
•
Phased approach allows for structured guidance for facility managers, uncovering cost savings over 
time
•
Focuses on worst performing buildings first
•
Reduces pressure on harder to electrify equipment types (e.g. gas stoves) through phased approach, 
longer runway for electric equipment to become widely available and cost-effective on-market 
•
Robust programming examples from Cities who have already implemented 
•
Tiers off of existing State energy disclosure laws (AB 802)
Policy Cons:
•
Necessitates new staff time and staff members to implement and track BPS 
•
Does not guarantee all-electric end usage in short term until phased retro commissioning phase, 
potentially missing some appliances that could be electrified 
•
Limited building stock reach, best suited to large commercial buildings (for example buildings >25,000 
sq ft)
•
Possibility to have equity implications from increased rents in multifamily if tenant protections are 
not integrated into the policy 
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers
Commercial Building Performance Standards are already mandated by California’s statewide building 
energy benchmarking and public disclosure law (AB 802), which require owners of commercial and 
17 National Association of State Energy Officials. 2025. Benchmarking and Building Performance Standards. Available: 
https://www.naseo.org/issues/buildings/benchmarking
1019 of 1111

--- page 1020 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
16
multifamily buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to annually benchmark and report their whole-building 
energy usage to the California Energy Commission. A City BPS could implement the same threshold as the 
state or have a more stringent threshold including buildings with a smaller square footage. 
•
Compared to other policy approaches listed here, building performance standards are a well-
established policy mechanism that has been adopted by many local governments including the City 
of San Jose, City of San Francisco, and City of Brisbane. City of Brisbane Building Efficiency Program 
passed in 2019, requires annual energy and water benchmarking and reporting, and compliance with 
Beyond requirements to show that they are high performing, or improving performance for 
commercial buildings 10,000 SF and larger.19
•
City of San Jose’s Energy and Water Building Performance Ordinance passed in 2018 and requires 
commercial and multifamily buildings above 20,000 square feet to annually benchmark and report 
their energy and water usage. Beginning in 2023, building have been required to meet energy and 
water efficiency standards on a rolling five-year basis.20 
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential 
Commercial building space makes up over 39 million square feet within the City with 44 percent and 30 
percent coming from space and water heating respectively. The building performance standard would 
likely not result in significant GHG emissions reductions before 2030 due to the phased in nature of the 
program. However, 83,660 MT CO2e were generated by commercial buildings in 2019 These emissions 
could be reduced by 2045 depending on the final thresholds adopted by the City.21 
Community Feedback 
Across engagement events, community members were supportive of a BPS policy. In the community 
survey, approximately 58% of respondents were supportive of a BPS policy, 12% neutral, and 29% do not 
support.  The Senior Advisory Commission, Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, and Community 
Relations Commission were supportive of this policy pathway.
During our direct outreach to building owners and developers, respondents understood the importance 
of a BPS policy and made suggestions of how to set thresholds to be most efficient and minimize 
unnecessary costs like setting individual reductions targets measured against a buildings own emissions 
rather than a City average and including a vintage threshold to exclude new buildings which are already 
more efficient. Building owners also shared that limited physical space for electric equipment upgrades 
and complicated tenant lease structures would be challenges to implementation that building owners 
would need guidance navigating. 
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations 
The cost of decarbonizing buildings under a Building Performance Standard (BPS) policy varies significantly 
based on factors such as building age, equipment types, and energy end uses. This variability necessitates 
19 https://www.brisbaneca.org/bbep
20 https://www.sanjoseca.gov/your-government/departments-offices/environmental-services/climate-smart-san-jos/energy-and-water-
building-performance-ordinance
21 City of San Mateo Climate Action Plan (Page 41) https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/DocumentCenter/View/93755/Climate-Action-Plan-2024-
Update?bidId= 
1020 of 1111

--- page 1021 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
17
a flexible, performance-based approach, allowing owners to prioritize cost-effective solutions tailored to 
their assets. The BPS framework’s phased implementation mitigates financial burdens:
•
Benchmarking identifies inefficiencies (e.g., leaks, malfunctioning systems) and establishes a baseline 
for improvement. Benchmarking reporting costs approximately $3,000 annually.
•
Retro-commissioning pinpoints low-cost operational optimizations (e.g., HVAC adjustments, controls 
recalibration), which typically yield 16% energy savings at an average cost of $0.30/sq. ft., often with 
payback periods under two years.
•
Performance standards are deferred for 4+ years post-benchmarking, enabling strategic capital 
planning for equipment replacements and system upgrades.
Electrification of commercial building electric retrofits will likely have to be paired with a suite of demand 
management, energy efficiency, or renewable energy projects in order to make building electrification 
economical for building owners. Specific cost-effective system upgrades include heat pump rooftop 
package units, which are the most promising electrification solution for commercial building 
electrification, offering a replicable model for a 1:1 equipment swap-out.25 Analysis from PCE found that 
for these easy to electrify units (packaged/split units with both heating and cooling) the marginal costs to 
upgrade to an all-electric packaged or split heat pump system were found to be negligible as shown in 3.
Figure 4
PCE 2035 Decarbonization Plan Rooftop Package Unit Marginal Cost Analysis
Equity is addressed by allowing building-specific compliance pathways and avoiding prescriptive 
mandates that could disproportionately burden older or under-resourced properties. There are additional 
equity implications when considering multifamily housing which could exacerbate disparities and lead to 
rent increases if not properly designed. Key considerations for equitable BPS implementation include anti-
25 Rocky Mountain Institute. 2022. Medium-Size Commercial Retrofits. Available: https://rmi.org/insight/economics-of-electrifying-buildings-
medium-size-commercial-retrofits/
1021 of 1111

--- page 1022 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
18
displacement measures for buildings that receive any City subsidy, incentive, or funding. Equity 
implications and design measures for multifamily are still being investigated. Due to the phased in nature 
of this program, those considerations can be addressed over time, but before thresholds for reductions 
are finalized (year 4+). 
Policy Findings 
A BPS policy builds on state legislation and addresses a large source of emissions reductions. There is 
overall community support and flexible and cost-efficient implementation. However, a BPS policy 
obligates a significant amount of staff time. Therefore, for efficient implementation, the City could set a 
threshold that would maximize the captured square footage while minimizing the number of impacted 
building owners. A threshold that initially only applies to buildings greater than 50,000 square feet (which 
are already mandated to report benchmarking data under AB 802) would ensure the City’s building stock 
is complying with state law and allow the City to understand the benchmarking process without a 
significant staff burden. 
Policy Option 5: Municipal Electric First Policy 
Description 
This policy establishes a mechanism for local government renovations to go ‘electric first’. This means that 
new appliance purchases, renovations, and replacements should prioritize using all-electric equipment. 
This policy would also allow for technical and cost infeasibility on a project-level when an all-electric 
alternative is either not available or deemed infeasible due to cost. The main purpose of the policy is to 
position the City as the leader in decarbonization within the community as well as prepare City facilities 
for upcoming requirements. The City would also learn best practices through this approach which it can 
share with the community and electrified city facilities would serve as demonstration buildings to highlight 
the comfort, health, and safety benefits of going all-electric. 
Policy Pros: 
•
Demonstrates City leadership in building electrification
•
Availability of significant local government incentives to electrify through Peninsula Clean Energy’s 
Gov BE and Willdan GK12 program 
•
Existing resources available through County of San Mateo including staff report, model code language, 
and case studies
•
Prepares City for future requirements while incentives are available
•
Lower operating costs 
Policy Cons:
•
Limited reach as policy only covers municipal buildings 
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers
Some other jurisdictions who have implemented this policy include:
1022 of 1111

--- page 1023 ---
City of San Mateo
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis
19
•
City of Mountain View: Municipal Green Building Policy (2020): requires analysis of opportunities for 
electrification upon major upgrade of City-owned buildings, and develops a minimum of LEED Gold 
for new construction.27
•
City of San Diego: Zero Emissions Municipal Buildings and Operations Policy (2022): any time a natural 
gas burning system is being replaced, requires the new equipment to be electric.28
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential 
In 2005 municipal natural gas use was 86,096 therms which equates to approximately 586 MT CO2e 
annually after accounting for natural gas leakage. If fully implemented, the electric first policy would aim 
to reduce these emissions over time as equipment needed replacement. The total reductions by 2030 and 
2045 would depend on the availability of all-electric replacements and the rate of equipment failure. 
Community Feedback 
Across community engagement events, there was broad support for this policy option. In the community 
survey, approximately 60% of respondents were supportive of this policy option, 15% neutral, and 24% 
do not support. Multiple constituents and building owners expressed a desire for the City to better 
understand the permitting process and technical implications, which would be achieved if the City led first 
by example. The Senior Advisory Commission, Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, and 
Community Relations Commission were supportive of this policy pathway.
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations 
This policy enables the City to conduct a comprehensive feasibility assessment to evaluate the cost 
differentials between electrification and gas equipment replacement for municipal facilities. By 
incorporating exemptions for projects demonstrating technical or financial infeasibility, the policy ensures 
a targeted approach to identifying the most cost-effective energy upgrades, consistent with 
methodologies applied to commercial buildings. Implementation costs will vary based on building-specific 
factors, including system age, infrastructure readiness, and operational requirements.
As these facilities are City-owned, the municipality retains full decision-making authority over capital 
expenditures, eliminating potential equity concerns that might arise in privately owned housing or 
commercial properties. Furthermore, since many City owned facilities are used by the public such as 
libraries or park properties, creating healthier and more comfortable spaces while lowering operational 
costs provide an equity benefit to the community. This approach ensures alignment with both fiscal 
responsibility and the City’s broader decarbonization objectives. 
Policy Findings
The adoption of a Municipal Electric First Policy establishes a process to systematically evaluate 
electrification opportunities during renovations and new projects for City-owned facilities. This policy 
aligns with the City’s climate goals while providing data-driven insights into cost and feasibility, enabling 
informed decision-making for future building decarbonization efforts. Furthermore, the municipal 
electrification first policy saw general support throughout the community engagement process.

28 https://www.sandiego.gov/sites/default/files/zero_emissions_municipal_buildings_operations_policy.pdf

Subject:
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement for Electrify City of San Mateo
A major component of the Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Buildings Strategy is a robust community 
engagement process. This project aims to create an actionable roadmap with steps for City of San Mateo 
to electrify buildings based on effectiveness and equity criteria, cost-effectiveness, best available science 
and data, and community feedback. Community outreach and public input are fundamental to shaping 
effective, equitable, and successful decarbonization policies. This memorandum summarizes the 
engagement conducted by both City staff and Rincon Consultants between Spring 2023 and Spring 2025.
Executive Summary of all Outreach 
Outreach has been conducted in two phases to understand community priorities, concerns, and needed 
support for successful policy implementation. During Phase I of engagement, the City presented at 
multiple meetings to gather feedback on the equity and effectiveness criteria and educate the 
community about costs and benefits of building electrification. This included a meeting with the 
Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, a City Council meeting, five General Plan Town Hall 
meetings, and an online survey. After Phase I of engagement, the initial suite of policy pathways was 
narrowed down to five policy pathways to advance existing building electrification within San Mateo. 
Phase II of engagement aimed at further refining these priority policies for inclusion within the final 
Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Buildings Strategy. During Phase II, the community engagement efforts 
included an in-person workshop and an additional virtual workshop for community members which 
focused on the five policy pathways, a virtual information session on Building Performance Standards for 
commercial building owners, presentations at City Commissions and community and stakeholder 
meetings, and an online survey. 
Throughout the community engagement process, there have been polarized responses to building 
electrification. While most constituents care about addressing the climate crisis and agree that the City 
should pursue existing building electrification in some form, many residents also expressed concerns. 
The primary constituent values identified during Phase I and Phase II engagement that are motivating 
building electrification included:
•
Urgent need to address the climate crisis is a priority issue
•
Health and air quality benefits of building decarbonization
•
Need for local action and the City to act now
•
Availability of electric equipment incentives and rebates to support property owners 
1024 of 1111

--- page 1025 ---
City of San Mateo
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement
2
The primary concerns identified during Phase I and Phase II engagement included: 
•
Upfront costs of electric equipment 
•
On-bill or operational costs of electric equipment
•
Installation and electrical infrastructure challenges
•
Grid reliability and resilience
•
Lack of education surrounding electrification 
In general, the results of the community outreach conducted by the City point to a general desire to 
address climate change and decarbonize buildings tempered by concerns about cost and feasibility. 
Based on these findings, a focus on policies that support decarbonization when cost effective and 
efficient while providing flexible alternatives for particularly challenging cases should be prioritized. 
Below is a detailed summary of outcomes and themes from each engagement event. A record of public 
comments and photos is listed at the end of this memorandum.
Community Engagement Phase I: Community Concerns and 
Values 
During Phase I of engagement, the City held multiple events to gather feedback on the equity and 
effectiveness criteria and educate the community about costs and benefits of building electrification. The 
goal of this phase was to both provide information to the community on what building electrification is 
and why the City is pursuing it, as well as learn about the core values and concerns the community has 
around upgrading their homes and businesses. This included a meeting with the Sustainability and 
Infrastructure Commission, a meeting with the Equity and Inclusion Task Force, a City Council meeting, 
five General Plan Town Hall meetings, and an online survey.
General Plan Town Hall Meetings
In Fall of 2023, five General Plan Town Hall meetings were held to solicit feedback on the City’s General 
Plan Update. The General Plan Town Hall events were in-person events located throughout the 
community, one event in each of San Mateo’s five districts. At each outreach event, doors opened at 5:30 
pm to allow attendees to view poster boards (example at end of memorandum) on various topics 
(circulation, land use, safety, noise, etc.) with a formal presentation on the Draft General Plan at 7:00 pm. 
An average of 60 community members attended each event.
The project team had a poster board summarizing the Sustainable Buildings Strategy project effort. 
Community members were very engaged at the Sustainable Buildings Strategy poster board. Staff 
received a mix of both supportive feedback for working on electrification requirements as well as 
concerns about costs related to electrification. 
Diversity Equity and Inclusion Task Force Presentation 
On September 22, 2023, presented to the City’s Diversity Equity and Inclusion Task Force. Feedback from 
the Task Force included: the need to include education, data, and storytelling in outreach. The Task 
Force’s biggest concerns regarding electrification were related to the electric power outages and costs 
1025 of 1111

--- page 1026 ---
City of San Mateo
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement
3
to homeowners. The Task Force also helped identify follow up opportunities for partnerships and project 
outreach.
Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission Meeting
At the September 13, 2023 Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission meeting, staff and the 
consultant team presented the building inventory and market segmentation analysis as well as building 
electrification cost analysis. Over 120 written public comments were submitted for the item with the 
majority in support of decarbonization. 24 verbal comments were also provided at the meeting. 
Proponents of decarbonization support electrification policy efforts to reduce gas use in the community 
to combat climate change, improve air quality, and improve health. Proponents also encouraged the 
leveraging of incentives and rebates for electrification.1 For those opposed, the number one concern 
about electrification is the costs to the property owner. Other concerns include power outages, growth 
of the grid to accommodate future electric buildings, and passed on costs to renters. 
Survey
The project team also launched an online survey to assess community priorities for building 
electrification that was open December 2023 through January 10, 2024. Results of the online survey were 
shared at the January 16, 2024 City Council Study Session. A summary of the survey results is included 
below. The survey had a total of 194 respondents from a wide range of socioeconomic backgrounds as 
shown in Figure 1 and Figure 2 .

The Phase I survey primarily focused on what priorities and concerns the community had relating to 
buildings and building decarbonization. As shown in Figure 3, affordable energy costs was the highest-
ranking quality for San Mateo Buildings, followed by a tie between Health and Safety and Resilience. 
Climate and sustainability ranked fourth followed by Modern Appliances and Functionality. 
Respondents also provided feedback on their biggest concerns regarding building electrification in San 
Mateo. Figure 4 shows that increasing energy bills was the largest concern relating to building 
electrification. This concern was followed by concern over increased housing/rent costs as the second 
most common concern. Respondents also ranked difficulty in accessing rebates (3rd) and building 
electrification ignoring other more pressing building issues (4th) as primary issues that would need to be 
addressed.

These survey results highlighted major concerns of building electrification of both upfront and on-bill 
cost, reliability concerns, and challenges accessing the funding and financing opportunities meant to 
decrease the cost of electrification. These topics were carried through into Phase II of outreach as the 
project team worked to refine the policy options based on community feedback.

8
City Council Meeting 
In January of 2024, Rincon Consultants and City staff provided a presentation to City Council on the Phase 
I results. The presentation focused on the results of the building segmentation analysis, cost analysis, 
engagement to date, and initial policy recommendations. During this meeting five policy options were 
provided for City Council feedback including: 
1.
Continue adopted reach codes and focus on outreach and incentives (Business as Usual)
2.
Explore and adopt new reach codes to encourage and require electrification 
3.
Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of the Bay Area Air District’s Zero NOx 
requirements 
4.
Establish Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings 
5.
Adopt an electric-first policy for City facilities 
City Council provided feedback which included direction for City Staff to continue exploring the 
feasibility, cost, and community support for each of the five options. 
Community Engagement Phase II: Policy Pathways Feedback
Following direction from City Council, City staff with support from Rincon Consultants, conducted 
additional outreach on the five policy strategies presented to City Council during Phase I. Phase II 
outreach events included an in-person community workshop, virtual community workshop, virtual 
meeting with commercial building owners, presentations to community and developer groups, and 
several upcoming Commission meetings. A second survey was also developed and distributed through 
these and other City channels. The second survey was developed to get more detailed information about 
support for/concern around the five policy pathways. 
Each community meeting and survey was advertised to the community through multiple avenues 
including the City’s social media channels, e-newsletter, and at City events, and to individuals who 
attended prior events. 
The following sections detail the feedback received from the community during each engagement 
opportunity. 
In-Person Community Feedback Meeting 
Feedback Meeting Summary 
The in-person community feedback meeting was advertised to the community through multiple avenues 
including the City’s social media channels, City e-newsletter, and direct emails. There were 20 attendees. 
The in-person community feedback meeting took place on March 5th, 2025 from 7:00 p.m. to 8:00 p.m. 
at the San Mateo Public Library. The goals of the meeting were for participants to:
1)
Understand the definition and importance of existing building electrification in the context of climate 
change and the City of San Mateo’s climate goals
2)
Note an overview and timeline of the City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy
3)
Recognize previously shared concerns and solutions about existing building electrification 
1031 of 1111

--- page 1032 ---
City of San Mateo
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement
9
4)
Be introduced to the five policy options and how each would impact the community 
5)
Share community feedback on the policy options through an interactive “Sailboat” Activity and 
Voting Activity 
A total of 25 community members attended the feedback meeting. City of San Mateo Sustainability 
Analyst, Andrea Chow, began the meeting and introduced Ryan Gardener, Director of Climate Action 
and Adaptation at Rincon Consultants, who gave a 25-minute presentation on goals one through four 
described above. Community members had the opportunity to ask questions a various points during the 
presentation. 
After the presentation, community members were split into three groups to participate in the Sailboat 
Activity facilitated by a City or Rincon staff member. Each group contained a variety of members 
including industry professionals like contractors, climate activists, students, and renters and 
homeowners who have electrified portions of their own homes. The Sailboat Activity helped community 
members brainstorm electrification challenges and support solutions to help the City achieve its 
decarbonization goals. The activity featured a poster board with a sailboat heading for an island while 
being supported by wind and held back by an anchor. The island represents the City’s decarbonization 
goals, the anchor represents barriers and challenges keeping the City from achieving its goals, and the 
wind represents various policy and support solutions to help the City reach its goals. Using post-it notes, 
participants were asked to first brainstorm various “anchors” or barriers to success. Then, participants 
were asked to brainstorm various solutions, or “wind”, to overcome such “anchors”. The activity lasted 
approximately 25 minutes.
After the Sailboat Activity, community members were asked to participate in a final Voting Activity 
where they used post-its on a large posterboard to vote for as many of the five policy options that they 
would support the City adopt. There was also a “do nothing” option where participants could show that 
they did not support any electrification policy. Any community member who voted and left their email 
on a post-it was entered into a raffle for the chance to win one $50 visa gift card. 
Key results from each activity are included below, and a full record of all activity responses and photos 
are included in the Appendix. 
Sailboat Activity Themes 
Between the three groups, participants shared 30 “anchor” comments and 41 “wind” comments. Overall, 
the main concerns in the “anchor” category included costs, lack of education, grid reliability, equity, 
supply issues, and resistant social factors. The main support requested in the “wind” category included 
cost savings, education and outreach, grid reliability, equity programs, adequate supply, and a changing 
market. Below is a summary of comments in each section.

Virtual Community Feedback Meeting
Feedback Meeting Summary 
The virtual community feedback meeting took place on March 13th, 2025 from 12:00 p.m. to 1:00 p.m. via 
Zoom to provide an alternative time to the in-person meeting for community members to actively 
engage with the policy feedback process. The goals and meeting format were the same as the in-person 
event. 11community members participated in the virtual feedback meeting. 
Andrea Chow and Ryan Gardner facilitated the meeting. After the 25-minute presentation, community 
members participated in the same Sailboat Activity as the in-person meeting but in a virtual format. 
Participants remained in one large group using the engagement software Mural. Community members 
could enter comments into the Mural board themselves or leave comments in the chat or unmute 
themselves and leave verbal comments for Rincon staff to add onto the Mural board on their behalf. 
After the Sailboat Activity, community members were asked to take an exit poll to vote for as many of 
the five policy options that they would support the City adopt. There was also a “do nothing” option 
where participants could show that they did not support any electrification policy. Any community 
member who voted was entered into a raffle for the chance to win one $50 visa gift card. 
Key results from the virtual Sailboat Activity and exit poll are included below, and a full record of all 
activity responses, meeting chat, and photos are included in the Appendix. 
Sailboat Activity Themes 
Participants shared 9 “anchor” comments and 12 “wind” comments. Overall, the main 
concerns in the “anchor” category included costs, lack of education, grid reliability, 
technology and engineering challenges, equity, and legislative red tape. The main 
support requested in the “wind” category included: cost savings, education, equity, and 
technology solutions. Below is a summary of comments in each section.

San Mateo United Homeowners Association Feedback Meeting 
Feedback Meeting Summary
The San Mateo United Homeowners Association (SMUHA) meets regularly to coordinate efforts 
between neighborhood and homeowner associations. The project team presented at the March 20th 
meeting at 7pm. There were 10 attendees.
1036 of 1111

--- page 1037 ---
City of San Mateo
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement
14
Results and Themes 
Similar to other feedback, most attendees voiced concern about the potential costs to property owners 
and the potential for costs to be passed on to renters. There was discussion of grid resilience and PG&E 
power safety shut offs. Most attendees were opposed to the City moving too quickly on building 
electrification policy. 
Commercial Building Performance Standard Information Session 
Feedback Meeting Summary 
The virtual Commercial BPS Information Session was advertised to building owners with buildings above 
50,000 square feet through email. City staff spoke at the Chamber of Commerce Public Policy Group and 
Chamber of Commerce Developers Roundtable to promote this event. The City and Rincon Consultants 
created a BPS 1-pager explaining the relevance of the policy to distribute to building owners (available at 
end of this memorandum). 
The virtual Commercial BPS Information Session took place on April 10th, 2025 from 10:00 a.m. to 11:00 
a.m. over zoom. The goals of the meeting were for participants to:
1)
Learn about the importance of a Building Performance Standard in the context of the City’s climate 
goals and legislation advancing a BPS across the state
2)
Note an overview and timeline of the City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy
3)
Learn about the phased implementation process of a Building Performance Standard
4)
Share feedback, concerns, and ask questions about the policy
A total of six building owners attended the information session. City of San Mateo Sustainability Analyst, 
Andrea Chow, began the meeting and introduced Ryan Gardener, Director of Climate Action and 
Adaptation at Rincon Consultants, who gave a 25-minute presentation on goals one through three 
described above. After the presentation, building owners had the opportunity to share feedback, 
concerns, and ask questions about a potential BPS. 
Key results from the discussion are included below, and a full record of all activity responses and photos 
are included in the Appendix. 
Results and Themes 
Overall participants were thankful for the event as they were able to learn valuable information and share 
their feedback and desires for implementation of the policy. Several building owners from both retail and 
multi-family backgrounds shared insights as to how a BPS might affect them and the ways in which the 
policy thresholds could be structured to make implementation more successful. The main comments 
included:
1)
Setting a threshold based on an average of all commercial buildings (comparing old buildings to all-
electric, new construction) could skew the threshold to require old buildings to convert to all-electric 
which could be financially infeasible. One idea provided by an attendee was to set an individual 
threshold where buildings must reduce their emissions compared to their own historical emissions 
rather than meet a City or building type average.
1037 of 1111

--- page 1038 ---
City of San Mateo
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement
15
4)
Converting to electric appliances also creates a possible space issue as electric rooms are 
increasingly taking up more space and old buildings may not have room for it. Relocating equipment 
due to space limitations poses increased engineering and financial burdens. 
5)
For new construction that must meet the recent Title 24 requirements, can there be a vintage 
threshold to avoid unnecessary financial and time burden for buildings that are already well-
performing. 
6)
For landlords with complicated tenant and lease structures, landlords would need City support 
to navigate conversations with tenants and financial responsibility.
Survey 
Survey Summary 
An online survey was created to collect community feedback from those who were unable to participate 
in any of the other engagement events. It was shared broadly through City email listservs, social media, 
and during City events including the State of the City event. The survey was also advertised at the end of 
every engagement meeting so participants could share it with their networks. 
The Phase II survey aimed to gauge support for the five policy pathways while being brief and accessible. 
The survey provided a background on the project and its importance, defined the five policy options, and 
asked community members which of the policies they would support. Respondents could choose options 
“Do not support”, “Neutral”, “Support”, or “Not sure.” Respondents also had the opportunity to leave 
other feedback in a comment box. 
Results and Themes 
The survey had a total of 217 respondents from a wide range of socioeconomic backgrounds as shown in 
Figure 5and Figure 6.

support vote.

Senior Advisory Commission
Feedback Meeting Summary
The project team presented to the Senior Advisory Commission on May 12, 2025. The Commissioners 
did not provide a formal policy recommendation and provided general feedback on the policy pathways. 
In general, the Commissioners agreed with the need to address climate through building decarbonization 
and electrification, but had reservations around requirements which could result in high upfront costs 
(specifically Policy Option 3) for those on fixed incomes.
Results and Themes 
•
Most commissioners not supportive of Policy Option 3 due to the potentially high upfront costs
•
Lots of discussion about upfront costs and concern for community members on a fixed income
•
General understanding and support of project goal and need to electrify
•
Some interest and discussion in Policy Option 4, one commissioner pointed out how this was 
adopted in a lot of other cities
•
Support of pursuing policy options along with providing resources and programs to help 
homeowners transition
•
Noted need for technical support especially for older folks with less understanding of new 
technologies

Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission 
Feedback Meeting Summary
The project team presented to the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission on May 14, 2025. The 
Commission’s policy recommendation supported Policy Options  1, 2, 4, and 5 with consideration of 
additional engagement and/or hardship exemptions where needed.  
Results and Themes
•
Discussion of greenhouse gas emission impact of different measures
•
Discussion of City funds and other programs/resources to support homeowners in electrification 
transition noted support for other programs like sewer lateral replacements
•
Need for programs like PCE concierge services to support transition
•
Some interest in Policy Option 3 but worried about compliance and concern for timeline
•
Concerns over Policy 2 and 3 were primarily tied to potential for high costs in edge cases. 
•
Interest in hardship exemption language in future ordinances to help with cases that are hard-to-
electrify
•
Some commissioners noted the need to phase in Policy 4 to ensure the City understands the 
costs/feasibility of commercial electrification
•
Discussion of compliance challenges

Commissioner discussion of language access to resources and programs and the need for 
different programs based on income-level

Discussion of contractor workforce transition

------- Forwarded Message ------- 
Date: On Saturday, June 14th, 2025 at 7:25 PM 
Subject: (No Subject) 
To: rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>, aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org 
<aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>, dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org <dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org>, 
idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org <idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>, nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org 
<nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear Mayor Newsom and City Council, 
The following is a stock letter and I am forwarding it to you since I agree with it. Please see message 
below this letter for my personal thoughts in addition to this stock letter:  
I am a local housing provider in your city, and I am concerned with the proposals to retrofit existing 
properties to all-electric systems.  
Conversion to all-electric is a major capital improvement that is very costly and complex. Mandating 
such a change should not be taken lightly without input from property owners and operators who will 
bear the cost of these new requirements. 
Should you continue with electrification of residential buildings, please consider the following: 
• The city should make funding available to help offset costs for the retrofit. This includes building
and permit fees and other expenses, such as potential loss of income during construction.
• The city should give property owners the maximum time allowed to comply.
• The city should not adopt additional restrictions or regulations on the rental housing market that
would make the retrofit even more cost-prohibitive or enact limitations that would constrain housing
providers’ control of their properties.
• The city should prioritize an incentive-based approach for phasing out natural gas appliances
instead of costly, unfunded mandates.
Property owners would be placed in the impossible position of being forced to make huge upfront 
investments – at a time when debt service costs are high – with little more than a hope of eventual 
modest cost savings. 
My concerns: 
1060 of 1111

--- page 1061 ---
2
 
It should not be mandated to retrofit all buildings and residential houses to use only electricity and 
not a combination of gas and electricity.  
 
There are not enough licensed electricians or contractors to do the job - retrofitting would be an 
impossible task for a home owner, housing provider and other businesses to do in a reasonable 
time. There is no regulations on costs an electrician would charge, and since demand would be high 
and supply of electricians low, there would be price gouging. The cost to retrofit older buildings 
would mean completely re-wiring the entire building/house. The cost would be immense and there is 
no justification for this - what authority does the council members have to even consider this 
proposal to force everyone to comply with retrofitting to go all electric?  
 
Electricity is more expense than gas. What happens to senor citizens who own their home and have 
to now come up with money to pay for the retrofit or complete re-wiring of their aged homes? How 
can on a fixed income or living off of their saving, pay for utilities when electric bill will be higher than 
a gas bill? 
 
The power grid goes down, there is no backup for anything - it has happened with solar flares, too 
much demand for electricity as cold spells in Texas and north east US, there is a domino effect, 
where one power provider goes down then others will go down. An example: In 1982 high winds 
knocked over power lines and it had a ripple effect and took out the power from SF to San Diego and 
all the way to Las Vegas NV, In 1996, two major outages effecting western Canada, Idaho, Montana, 
Utah, New Mexico, CA, and Arizona which effected 3 million people the electricity went out due to 
voltage instability in the grid.  
 
California imports electricity from Mexico and surrounding states, CA it is not self sufficient.  
 
California is becoming a failed state because of these types of regulatory adventures.  
 
The people that have the ability, and those that are young enough, are leaving the state and are also 
the people who provide income through taxes to the state, the state is biting the hand that feed it 
through these kinds of policies.  
 
Gabby Tedeschi-Laughlin

-o0o- JACO PROPERTIES, LLC -o0o- 
“A San Francisco Bay Area Workforce Housing Provider” 
P.O. Box 570 
San Mateo, CA 94401 
 
June 16, 2025 
 
San Mateo City Council 
Mayor Rob Newsom – rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org 
Deputy Mayor Adam Lorraine – aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org 
Councilmember Danielle Cwirko-Godycki – dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org 
Councilmember Lisa Diaz Nash – ldiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org 
Councilmember Nicole Fernandez – nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org 
Council Clerk – clerk@cityofsanmateo.org 
 
Re: 
Agenda Item 41 - “Forced All-Electric Retrofit” 
 
Dear Mayor Newsom and City Council, 
 
I am a local housing provider in San Mateo (we provide workforce housing in San Mateo and 
several other local communities on the peninsula), and I am concerned with the proposals to 
retrofit existing properties to all-electric systems. 
 
Conversion to all-electric is a major capital improvement that is very costly and complex. 
Mandating such a change should not be taken lightly without input from property owners and 
operators who will bear the cost of these new requirements. 
 
Should you continue with electrification of residential buildings, please consider the following: 
 
• The city should make funding available to help offset costs for the retrofit. This includes 
building and permit fees and other expenses, such tenant displacement payments and temporary 
housing as well as potential loss of income during construction. 
 
• The city should give property owners the maximum time allowed to comply. 
 
• The city should not adopt additional restrictions or regulations on the rental housing market that 
would make the retrofit even more cost-prohibitive or enact limitations that would constrain 
housing providers’ control of their properties. 
 
• The city should prioritize an incentive-based approach for phasing out natural gas appliances 
instead of costly, unfunded mandates. 
 
Property owners would be placed in the impossible position of being forced to make huge 
upfront investments at a time when debt service costs are high (some of our insurance premiums 
have increased from 350%) with little more than a hope of eventual modest cost savings. 
 
Please do your due diligence before any final adoption of new mandates. 
 
Sincerely, 
By, Michael Johnston 
For, Jaco Properties, LLC

Recommendations for item #41, Electrify San Mateo
Dear Mayor, Deputy Mayor, and Council, 
Thank you for ongoing, strategic climate leadership in these very tough times. Here are my 
recommendations for item #41, Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy, on the June 16, 
2025 Council agenda: 
1. I support adoption of building codes that eliminate gas in new construction,
2. I strongly supportadoption of building codes that eliminate gas in retrofit construction;
these codes will deliver the most significant reductions in Greenhouse Gases,
3. I strongly supportacceleration of NOx air quality standards for gas water heater and
furnace replacement; these codes will also deliver the most significant reductions in
Greenhouse Gases,
4. I support adoption of building performance standards for commercial buildings, and
5. I support electrification of city buildings.
I thank you for this opportunity, 
Ziyi Lu

From:

Subject:
Agenda Item #41 - City Council Meeting 6.16.25
Date:
Monday, June 16, 2025 3:57:08 PM
Dear San Mateo City Council,
I am the father of an 11-year-old daughter and I’m very concerned about her future.
Collectively, we are not acting with the urgency that the climate situation demands
and deserves. The science is clear and it is well-documented that the cost of
addressing the climate crisis will never be less expensive than it is right now – the
longer we wait, the greater the cost and the suffering. Time is running out. We must
act now to preserve a livable planet for future generations.
That being said, I want to thank the City of San Mateo for prioritizing this issue and
working to put a plan in place to decarbonize. San Mateo has proven to be a leader –
we need that to continue and we need more cities to follow.
I respectfully ask the City Council to please support the boldest and most meaningful
versions of the pathways outlined in tonight’s presentation. We are in a "yes, and"
moment. Following just one or two of these pathways will not adequately address the
challenge we have before us – each of the pathways offer solutions to different
problems so we need all of them, in one form or another, to be adopted in order to
meet the City’s goals. While taking these actions will confront the climate crisis, it’s
helpful to also highlight the co-benefits of these electrification pathways which include
better local air quality, improved public health, increased safety, greater resiliency,
more comfortable buildings, and lower energy costs. It’s also important to understand
that there are excellent resources and financial support mechanisms in place to assist
with this transition, particularly for low-income communities.
Thank you for your consideration and thank you for taking this issue seriously and
acting accordingly.
Sincerely,
John McKenna

To:
City Council (San Mateo)
Cc:
; Martin McTaggart
Subject:
Item #41 - Electrify San Mateo - Sustainable Building Strategy Policy Feed-back
Date:
Monday, June 16, 2025 2:12:26 PM
Dear Mayor Newsom and City Council,
I am a local housing provider in your city, and I am concerned with the proposals to retrofit
existing properties to all-electric systems.
Conversion to all-electric is a major capital improvement that is very costly and complex.
Mandating such a change should not be taken lightly without input from property owners
who will bear the cost of these new requirements.
Should you continue with electrification of residential buildings, please consider the
following:
• The city should make funding available to help offset costs for the retrofit, including
building and permit fees and other expenses, such as potential loss of income during
construction.
• The city should give property owners the maximum time allowed to comply.
• The city should not adopt additional restrictions or regulations on the rental housing
market that would make the retrofit even more cost-prohibitive.
• The city should prioritize an incentive-based approach for phasing out natural gas
appliances instead of costly, unfunded mandates.
Property owners have to make huge upfront investments – at a time when debt service costs
are high – with little hope of modest cost savings.
Please do your due diligence before any final adoption of new mandates.
Best, 
Laurie Watanuki

--- page 1072 ---
Electrify San Mateo Background
• Building energy use represents 38% of San Mateo's GHG emissions
• Building electrification is key in reducing fossil gas use and switching to 
clean carbon-free electricity provided by Peninsula Clean Energy
• Co-benefits of electric buildings
4

Decarbonization

Phase II: Community Concerns
▪Installation Cost
•
Short-term investment can be high due to 
unexpected repairs/ upgrades 
▪Installation and Electric Infrastructure Challenges 
•
Lack of space or need to upgrade electric panels 
▪On-bill Costs
•
long term operational costs of electric appliances  
▪Grid Reliability 
•
Power outages from PSPS and increased electricity 
demand could strain the grid
▪Lack of Education
•
Need for a "help-desk" and peer-to-peer coaching

Option 1: Business as Usual
• Continue to adopt new construction "reach codes" 
(ordinances that surpass the state's buildings code) to 
require more sustainable new buildings than the state 
code; AB 306 impacts
• Focus on education, outreach, and incentives for 
existing buildings
• Current incentives: Peninsula Clean Energy, 
Inflation Reduction Act, and California TECH
$(7,500) savings 
compared to 
mixed-fuel
$0 (pre-wiring 
required 
by code) 
~1,070 MT CO2e/yr by 
2030 if all new buildings 
had no natural gas

--- page 1087 ---
Option 3: Zero NOx Equipment Requirements
• The Bay Area Air District (BAAD) will 
require property owners in nine Bay 
Area counties, including San Mateo, 
to switch their water heaters 
(starting in 2027) and space heating 
equipment (starting in 2029) to 
electric when they need to be 
replaced
• This proposed policy would move 
up the deadline so that this 
requirement would be in effect in 
ahead of BAAD’s requirements
~9,700 MT 
CO2e/yr by 2030​

--- page 1093 ---
Community Engagement 
• Advertised event with BPS information 1-pager
• Building owners had the following suggestions and 
concerns:
▪Desire for an individual threshold where buildings must 
reduce their emissions compared to their own historical 
emissions rather than meet a City or building type 
average.
▪Desire for a vintage threshold, exempting newer buildings 
that are already well- performing
▪Concerns regarding space issues and complicated tenant 
and lease structures

--- page 1105 ---
Electrification Costs* At Parity With Gas!
• Heat pump water heater
• Sample size: 1,499 PCE projects Jan ‘21 to Apr 2025
• HPWH cost after rebates and tax credits
$ 2,533
• Typical gas water heater replacement (tank):    $ 2,500 - $ 3,500
• Typical gas water heater replacement (tankless):        $ 5,200
• Heat pump HVAC
• Sample size: 1,691 PCE projects Oct ‘22 to Apr 2025
• Heat Pump HVAC cost after rebates & tax credits:      $13,800
• Typical gas furnace replacement with AC
$16,000
• Typical gas furnace replacement w/o AC
$   6,800
*Not including electrical upgrades

--- page 1109 ---
BAY AREA AIR DISTRICT RULES 9.4 AND 9.6
●Bay Area Air District has adopted rules under the Clean Air Act, 
that prevent replacing gas water heaters with gas, and gas 
furnaces with gas, effective January 1, 2027, and 2029
●Air District has NOT asked for inclusion in the State 
Implementation Plan, for concern about being disallowed 
under the current Administration
●South Coast Air Quality Management District (SCAQMD) had 
taken a “market” approach, but has now rejected that 
approach.
1109 of 1111

--- page 1110 ---
Assembly Bill 306 - THE END OF REACH CODES
●Places 6 year “temporary” moratorium on new Reach Codes 
- including AC2HP, and Retrofits; effective June 1, 2025 
October 1, 2025, and only if following a building code other 
than the normal state process
●Ostensibly for the purpose of speeding housing rebuild in 
Los Angeles, after fires
●This is an Assembly Speaker Bill
